So I would say my point here is — look, much of what we're doing in knowledge work, we haven't evolved to do, right? We're sitting on these computers reading these things, writing these memos, whatever. We are not evolved to do this. Our brains are not wired for this. Still, nevertheless, it's useful to have LLMs to do this for us. And as humans, we're heavily vision-biased. Other rodents are more olfactory-biased, and I've worked on these things myself before. So what's the real estate in the brain that's allocated to vision (the occipital lobes), versus language areas (the temporal lobe)? Probably more vision. I'll have to check with — check ChatGPT — but I think that's the situation.

You don't know from memory.

No, man, I'm externalizing. I'm a big RAG believer in my personal lifestyle. [laughter]

In the limit, we're all — it's all RAG. I internalize just, you know, important things — like my emotions to you. No, just kidding. Sorry. [laughter]

Anyway, and vision is dominating when people are training vision-language models — they end up the language ends up dominating the vision content there. But yeah, it's hard to say that because a certain brain is more biased towards a certain modality doesn't mean necessarily that we're going to more efficiently do it. I do think that efforts on brain-computer interfaces should take this into account: how do you then relay it back to the brain? That's where I think it's really important to think — what real estate do we have there right now? But for knowledge work, it's equally fine if it's text, I think.

收官：五到十年后的世界

Last question: if everything goes right, what does the world look like in five to ten years, and then what is Engram's role in it?

I think I'm imagining a world where everyone has their own model that is really different from the other person's model and from the frontier model, and all of these kind of serve different purposes. And to have a model that really — you know, I think people often talk about knowing you, but also kind of helping you in the ways that make sense to you personally, whether it's an individual or a team. I think there's an element of having different kinds of intelligence everywhere.

Yeah. And to me, actually, it's a variant of the story where — in neuroscience, we know that memory and navigation are pretty closely related. Same circuits in the brain that represent landmarks in space are in charge of some elements of episodic memory and things like this. And for me, I think the company can be the actual LLM interface to the data plane for everyone. So sharing some similarities to great companies like Databricks and Oracle, where — we form these memories that happen to be neural memories, with models that happen to be personalized, and happens to be there's hundreds of millions of them. But they're basically a neural interface to the data plane in a way that's very different from what we know, and it's more efficient, it's more associative. It's not representing the file system as it is — it's representing a brain state of that file system. So that's for me a vision.

Beautiful vision to end on. Thank you guys so much for coming by and sharing what you're building.

Awesome. Love it. Thank you guys.

[music]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：KV Cache 是"硅基大脑的胎记"
Dan 在访谈中埋了一个极具张力的数字对比，但他自己没有把它推到最深处——让我替他完成这次顿悟：
一篇 Taylor Swift 的维基百科条目（几十 KB 文本），在 70B Llama 的 KV cache 里要膨胀成 **约 80 GB 的 HBM 状态**；而整个 70B 模型的权重也才约 100 GB，里面"压缩"了几乎整个互联网。
这意味着：**模型的"长期记忆"压缩比是其"工作记忆"压缩比的 10⁶ 量级。** 换句话说，今天的 LLM 是一个有着博士级长期记忆、却用整面墙的便利贴来记今天对话的怪物。所谓"上下文工程"，本质是在用胶带把这个不对称结构补起来。Engram 真正在做的，不是"加一层记忆"，而是**修复硅基智能与生俱来的代谢失衡**——把短期昂贵的 KV 状态，逐步代谢成长期廉价的权重状态。
这恰好对应生物学中的 **"记忆固化"（memory consolidation）**：海马体中昂贵、高保真、易丢失的短时记忆，在睡眠期间被慢慢翻译成皮层中廉价、抽象、持久的长时记忆。Engram

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Bobbi Herzberg — 2025 Markets and Society Conference Keynote
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个反直觉的洞察

一、奥斯特罗姆最激进的命题，是"秩序根本不来自秩序"

表面上他在讲分权、联邦制、多中心治理——这些都太"政治学教科书"了。真正颠覆的是他在底层下的赌注：秩序不是被设计出来的，而是从共同信念中"凝结"出来的。这意味着宪法、法律、官僚体系在因果链上其实是结果，不是原因。我们习惯把制度当成自变量，把行为当成因变量；奥斯特罗姆把这个方向反了过来。这也解释了一个让人不安的推论：任何试图"通过设计制度来制造好公民"的努力，因果方向是颠倒的，注定低效甚至适得其反。这与当代许多"用算法治理人"的硅谷思路恰好相反。

二、一个让人意外的观察："国会不是审议机构"——因为人不能同时说和听

Bobby 转述 Vincent 时埋了一颗炸弹：众议院太大，所以根本无法"审议"。这听起来像政治学评论，但它其实是一个信息论命题——基于"一次只有一个人能说话"这种近乎生理学的约束。换言之，民主的尺度上限不是由宪法决定的，而是由人类听觉带宽决定的。这把民主理论一下子拉到了认知科学的层面：

  • 与香农的信道容量定理相通：群体的共识带宽 = 串行对话速率 × 互相理解的耐心；
  • 与邓巴数（150 人社交上限）共振：超过这个尺度，"治理"必然滑向"统治"；
  • 与现代社交媒体形成讽刺反差：算法让"所有人同时对所有人说话"，于是看似放大了民主，实际上摧毁了民主赖以存在的串行对话结构。

如果这条诊断成立，那么"民主的危机"就不是右翼或左翼的问题，而是尺度错配问题——我们用对话生物适配的工具，去运行 3.3 亿人的系统。

三、跨领域连接：民主治理 = 爵士乐 + 生态系统 + 工匠学徒制

奥斯特罗姆的"多中心治理"在 Bobby 的描述里同时援引了三个完全不同领域的隐喻： - 生态学——森林里没有一棵"主树"，物种通过互动维持韧性； - 音乐——交响乐 vs 爵士乐：一个强加秩序，一个在协作中发现秩序； - 工匠传统——公民通过"做"学会自治，就像艺人通过手工学会技艺。

把这三者叠加起来会得到一个反传统经济学的洞察：自治不是一种"制度"，而是一种"手艺"。手艺无法被规模化生产，无法被外包，无法被自动化——它只能被亲手传承。这就解释了为什么 Vincent 坚持"学者必须下场参与政策"——你不能教别人骑车而自己从未骑过。

真正惊人的推论：如果民主是手艺，那么民主的真正威胁不是独裁者，而是便利。每一次"让专家替我决定"、"让 AI 帮我投票"、"让算法过滤我的信息"，都在让这门手艺退化一点点。Vincent 担心的"Faustian bargain"在 2026 年的今天有了新的面孔——它不再叫"中央集权"，而叫"用户体验"。

最后一个埋伏笔：注意 Bobby 选了一张 Vincent 在曼尼图林岛、连电都没有的照片来谈"自治的脆弱"。这不是怀旧——这是一个断网隐喻。在没有外部基础设施可以依赖的地方，人才不得不重新成为"自己的政府"。换句话说，自治能力与外部基础设施的丰裕度，可能成反比。这对一个被云服务、智能家居、AI 助手包围的时代，是一个极冷的判断。

📝 转录文本

主持人开场

You're listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. This podcast includes audio from lectures, interviews, and discussions from scholars and visitors of the F. A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. To learn more about the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. To learn about graduate student fellowship opportunities with the Mercatus Center at George Mason University — for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe — please visit students.mercatus.org.

嘉宾介绍

Bobbi Herzberg is a distinguished senior fellow at the F. A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University, and an instructor emeritus at Utah State University. She's a past president of the Public Choice Society and the Society for the Development of Austrian Economics.

Bobbi has extensive experience in academic administration, having served as the department head of the political science department and administrative director of the Institute for Political Economy at Utah State, and extensive experience in philanthropy — having worked at the John Templeton Foundation and serving on boards of various organizations.

Her scholarship focuses on institutional economics, public choice, the Bloomington School (having taught there along with the Ostroms), and public policy. Specifically, she explores health care policy, the potential of self-governance to provide health care and welfare programs, environmental policy, and much more.

Bobbi upholds the importance of theory and practice and the multifaceted, interdisciplinary way that we need to study the world around us. Institutional analysis and governance matter for how we live. She thoughtfully balances the need for grounded scholarship and practical policy solutions, and she pushes her colleagues and students to do so too.

Tonight she will discuss Vincent Ostrom and the meaning of democracy. Please join me in welcoming Bobbi Herzberg.

开场寒暄

Well, it's a big crowd — and a big crowd of friends is sometimes the hardest crowd to talk to. Even though I'm sure you're all going to be friendly, um, because I care more what you think than a crowd of strangers. So, um, you be patient. Be patient with me.

I have the honor of talking about Vincent Ostrom and his theories of democracy with you tonight, and that is a real honor for me to be able to do that. But it's also not your best subject matter for a post-travel day, pre-bar experience. I know that this keynote should last no longer than 20 minutes to be really good, maybe 15 to be super good — and doing Vincent in that time...

So we're going to take the 30,000-foot vision of Vincent. The Markets and Society journal will publish a longer and more citation-filled version of this talk. But hopefully I can share at least some of the key ideas I think we should all appreciate in Vincent's work. And I know so many of you know so much about this work in any case that you'll go, "Well, why'd you leave that out? Why'd you leave that out?" Well, we'll discuss it over the next few days, I hope.

So it is a privilege. And the biggest — as I was starting to put this together, one of the things that I really appreciated was how much Lin Ostrom would have loved that we're doing this talk. She got much more tickled when somebody honored Vincent than even one of the great honors of her many honors that she had. So I'm thinking of her, and how she's thinking tonight about all of this and appreciating it.

So with that, I'm hoping I'm catching you at the right time — between just enough wine that you're pretty mellow, but not quite falling asleep yet. So hold with me. Okay, so let's go.

引入：民主作为人类的艺术

Tonight I'd like to talk about someone who spent his life helping us think more clearly about one of the most important and most difficult ideas in human history — the idea of democracy and how we sustain it.

Vincent Ostrom was not only a scholar but a craftsman of ideas — someone who believed that democratic life is a form of human artistry, something we make together and must constantly tend.

Vincent 的成长背景

Born in 1919 — love that — in Washington State, just close to the Canadian border in a very rural area, he grew up surrounded by forests, rivers, and farms. His father raised mink and bred mink. That early exposure to how people cooperated to manage shared resources — or didn't cooperate to manage those resources — stayed with him all of his life.

After teaching at the University of Wyoming and Oregon and then UCLA, he found his intellectual home at Indiana University, where he and Elinor Ostrom founded the Workshop in Political Theory and Policy Analysis. Together they built not just a research center but a living laboratory of self-governance. And why that is so important — that they were able to do that at Indiana University. And I know we have some people from Indiana University here tonight. It was such a special place for them to do that.

And it was important because at Wyoming and at UCLA, Vincent Ostrom had faced critics that said he should not be participating in the actual policy issues and policy design — it was inappropriate for a scholar. But that could not have been further from the truth for Vincent. He believed he had to be in the mix, because that was the most important goal of society. I miss them.

Vincent's great project was to understand how people can govern themselves without relying entirely on distant authorities or bureaucratic hierarchies. And that's where we begin tonight.

I have four key points — a fairly straightforward part of his theories on democracy, but profound, I think, insights that shaped his understanding that I'd like to share with you.

一张特别的照片

Now, this is my favorite Vincent picture, and it's my favorite Vincent picture because that little guy there is my 37-year-old son at an earlier time. You might imagine he's not that guy — whatever that movie was about the guy going backwards, I forget that one.

Um, but what I love about it — you may love it too — is Lin Ostrom brought back matching jackets from Africa for a toddler and Vincent, and they wore them. Looks good on Glenn; I'm not so sure it quite was Vincent's jam, but he wore it for that picture. So this is one of my very special favorite pictures. So I share that as my key points. One has nothing to do with the key points, but what the hell, it's my talk anyway.

第一点：秩序自下而上构成

Okay, so let's get back to those. So these are the key points, and I'm going to go through them in order, and hopefully give you just a highlight of each of them. So let's go ahead and get started.

Vincent began from a very simple but radical observation: order is not imposed from above. It is constituted from below. It emerges from our common beliefs.

He wrote, "Order in human societies is constituted by concepts and beliefs that human beings hold in common with one another as customs, laws, and rules."

That might sound abstract, but think about what it means. Every time you stop at a red light, return a library book, or don't grab your neighbor's cheesecake off the table, you are participating in a web of shared understanding about appropriate behavior. And that's the way we live most of our lives. No one forces you to do most of these things — at least past the age of your mother having any control over you — but you do them because you and those around you share certain expectations about what it means to live together.

Ostrom insisted that legitimacy in government rests not on command, but on consent and communication. As he put it, officials can command, but individuals do not necessarily obey. We obey when we believe that the rules make sense — when they resonate with our shared understanding of what's right. And so those are the rules that stick, and those are the rules that Vincent wanted to understand and wanted people to create for themselves.

与托克维尔的呼应

This is where Vincent's thinking overlaps very much with Alexis de Tocqueville. Both believed that religion, local custom, and community life shape how we interpret the actions of others and build trust. Shared knowledge doesn't just come from ideas, but from deeds — from the way we live together day after day.

对话的尺度限制

Ostrom also saw limits to this process. There's only so much that we can understand in large impersonal settings. Individuals engaged in discussion cannot speak and listen simultaneously. Although, goodness knows, I try to do so a lot. And probably some of you are trying to listen while looking at your phone and having conversations. I see you texting. This happens a lot at Mercatus. I'm just telling you right now — it's like their jam. Anyway, but he reminded us, you cannot do that. So earlier times — what can we say? You're all better than he was, I guess. Anyway.

That simple fact — one person talking at a time — places limits on how large a community of understanding can be. And boy, did he believe that. I can't tell you how many times we had the conversation, and he reminded me that the House of Representatives was not a deliberative legislative body. And this was very disturbing to a 27-year-old Congress scholar just starting her career. So, anyway — they can't deliberate because they're just too big. So that was his attitude on it. He thought that people needed to really get into the process to make the decisions that they reached.

And maybe that's one of the reasons why democracy seems so strained today. The scale of our political life has grown far beyond the scale of our conversations. We're trying to deliberate as one giant community of 330 million people — very diverse people — often through screens that make genuine listening almost impossible.

第二点：个人有自治的能力

Second key point that Vincent raises: individuals — I love this picture, too. It's a good one. This is Vincent in Alaska, because he made a difference in Alaska.

Individuals are very capable of self-government, from the perspective of Vincent Ostrom. He saw them governing. He saw them doing really hard things like constitution-making, and he participated in that. And as a result them working through these things like the Alaska Constitution on Natural Resources, Article 8 — which, you know, is part of the reason that people get a check now in the state of Alaska. Citizens all get royalties. Imagine your government just sending you money because it's your property, not theirs. Doesn't happen in all the other states, but it happens in Alaska. And he was at that conversation, because it came from the people — not from Vincent Ostrom, not from the expert.

Also, California irrigation water — really challenging circumstance. These are situations where we see how people working out the details listen to one another, argue with one another, have real challenges, but work to find common ground.

制度即手艺

He understood institutions not as static rules of the game, but as artifacts — things people craft using their local knowledge and skills. And just as artisans learn through practice, citizens learn to govern themselves by doing.

He once described a self-governing society as one that turns on the universality of artisanship. In such a society, each person first becomes their own governor — someone capable of self-mastery — and only then joins others to work out common arrangements.

This is a deeply hopeful vision of democracy. It's not about designing the perfect constitution or electing the right leaders. It's about cultivating the habits and virtues of self-governance — what Tocqueville called "the art of association."

But Ostrom was no romantic. He knew self-governance is hard work. People disagree. They misunderstand each other. They bring different histories and expectations to the table. That's why he valued institutions that slow things down — deliberative bodies, checks and balances, local councils — places where people can argue, reflect, and eventually find common ground. He honestly thought people could do that. I'm not so sure, but he honestly thought they could.

第三点：多中心治理 (Polycentricity)

And that's point three. Then how?

The concept most commonly associated with Vincent Ostrom and his theories of democracy is the theory of polycentricity, and polycentricity as good governance.

Together with Charles Tiebout and Robert Warren, he defined a polycentric system as one with many centers of decision-making that are formally independent of each other. In such a system, different problems can be addressed at different levels — some locally, some regionally, some nationally — without everything being run from the top down.

生态系统的类比

If we imagine different settings — such as maybe a forest ecosystem — there isn't one tree in charge. Instead, you have multiple species, each interacting and adapting to its environment. The result is a resilient whole.

That's what Ostrom wanted for human governance: a system of overlapping jurisdictions where cooperation, competition, and even conflict can coexist productively.

Okay, I have to make sure I'm on the right slide.

He wrote in The Crisis of Public Administration that centralization and decentralization must exist simultaneously. True federalism, in his view, means accepting the messiness of overlapping authority. We like things to be neat, but neat doesn't really work in large societies with subjective preferences and all kinds of things tearing people in different directions. It's most efficient in the bureaucratic sense, but it's far more adaptable and humane to work in this polycentric system.

对公民能力的要求

And that brings us to an important point. Polycentric systems rely on citizens who can navigate complexity. They demand judgment, tolerance, and engagement. If we expect Washington — or any central authority — to fix all our problems, we lose the vitality of democratic life. And we lose it to people who seem increasingly incapable of taking it up and taking up that task. And I'm not ready for that.

So polycentric governance isn't chaos. It's freedom structured through relationships of trust and accountability. It's the difference between a symphony — and a single symphony conductor — and a jazz ensemble. And I think I stole that from someone in this room. So if I did, just tell me who I did, so I'll cite you in the right thing, because I don't remember. But I thought that was really good with the jazz. Anyway — the first imposes order; the second discovers it through collaboration.

第四点：自治的脆弱性

The fourth key point — it's hard. The fragility of self-governance.

This is not an easy task, and they understood this. Both the Ostroms understood this. This is them on a hay wagon up — and they used to spend a lot of time up in Canada at Manitoulin Island and in weird places. But anyway, they did. Well, it was a weird place. No electricity, no anything. Thirty miles back and forth on bad roads once a week to get the mail. Imagine living as an academic — multiple academics, very popular academics — for the entire summer under those conditions. You guys couldn't leave your phones away for five minutes, you know. Anyway — oh, anyway, that's going to show up on the tape. Okay.

Faustian Bargain

So, it's fragile. Of course, Vincent also warned us that self-governance is fragile. It can be derailed by impatience, by the temptation to let others decide for us, or by the belief that unity requires uniformity.

He called this the Faustian bargain of politics. To create order, we need inequality of decision-making power — leaders, judges, administrators. But those same powers can easily become instruments of domination. "The sanctions necessary for maintaining order," he wrote, "may be used to compound wrongs as well as rights."

对中央集权的警告

Ostrom saw the dangers long before they became headlines: the tyranny of the majority, democratic despotism, the collapse of the rule of law. He warned that centralization in the presence of great diversity will inevitably entrap mankind in Catch-22 situations. In other words, when we try to solve every problem from the top, we create systems so rigid that they defeat their own purpose.

"Democracies," he wrote, "are vulnerable to the vices of rulers and the ineptitude of the people, and great experiments become monumental disasters."

Those are sobering words, but they're also an invitation to remember that democracy is not something we have — it's something we do. It's a practice, a set of relationships that must be renewed in every generation.

结语：民主是一场对话

So where does that leave us?

For Vincent, democracy was not a machine to be engineered, but a conversation to be sustained. It requires us to speak honestly, listen carefully, and act responsibly within the limits of our shared understanding.

We cannot all agree on everything — probably not even in this room, and we're kind of part of the same crowd — nor should we try. I mean, how boring would that be? I'm bored already thinking about it. Must be time for a drink, so I'll wrap up.

What we can do is build institutions, large and small, that let us live with our differences while still working toward common purposes. In that sense, the real measure of democracy for Vincent was not how efficiently we govern, but how generously we live together. He was very generous. He really was.

Vincent often thanked his students and colleagues for joining him in what he called "the adventure of self-governing." Tonight, I think he would thank all of us for keeping that adventure alive.

So let us raise a glass. Another great picture. This looks like he doesn't even know how to drink a beer, right? Doesn't it? I don't know. I mean, you know, he really wasn't of this world. He was kind of a different character altogether. I really loved — I mean, I loved him.

Um, so let us raise a glass, if you still have anything in it, to Vincent Ostrom — and to the ongoing experiment in freedom that he helped us understand.

Thank you.

节目结束语

Thank you for listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. To learn more about the research, scholars, and work of the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. For more information about graduate student fellowship opportunities for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org. We hope you recommend students to our programs or consider applying yourself.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个反直觉的洞察
### 一、奥斯特罗姆最激进的命题，是"秩序根本不来自秩序" 表面上他在讲分权、联邦制、多中心治理——这些都太"政治学教科书"了。真正颠覆的是他在底层下的赌注：**秩序不是被设计出来的，而是从共同信念中"凝结"出来的**。这意味着宪法、法律、官僚体系在因果链上其实是**结果**，不是**原因**。我们习惯把制度当成自变量，把行为当成因变量；奥斯特罗姆把这个方向反了过来。这也解释了一个让人不安的推论：**任何试图"通过设计制度来制造好公民"的努力，因果方向是颠倒的，注定低效甚至适得其反**。这与当代许多"用算法治理人"的硅谷思路恰好相反。
### 二、一个让人意外的观察：**"国会不是审议机构"——因为人不能同时说和听** Bobby 转述 Vincent 时埋了一颗炸弹：众议院太大，所以根本无法"审议"。这听起来像政治学评论，但它其实是一个**信息论命题**——基于"一次只有一个人能说话"这种近乎生理学的约束。换言之，民主的尺度上限不是由宪法决定的，而是由**人类听觉带宽**决定的。这把民主理论一下子拉到了认知科学的层面：
- 与香农的信道容量定理相通：群

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1444期】散户融资体量新高！芯片估值打满！这个标的加入观察列表 $CBRS✨20260623#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的"估值悖论"：芯片打满，而大科技反而被严重低估

小涛在视频中提到一个看似矛盾的现象——SOX芯片估值已经接近30倍上区间（打满），但纳指过去8个月只上涨7%，且这7%几乎全部由芯片贡献。这意味着除芯片外的"其他大科技"实际上处于隐性熊市中。

这里有一个让人意外的观察：市场表面上在"AI狂欢"，但资金其实只在AI产业链的最底层（硬件/芯片）狂欢，而上层应用、软件、平台型科技却在悄悄塌缩估值。这是一种典型的"铲子热、矿主冷"的资本错位——历史上，淘金热、互联网早期、云计算早期都出现过同样的形态，而最终赚最多钱的从来不是铲子，而是矿主。所以当下"芯片泡沫论"的真正含义可能不是"市场要崩"，而是"轮动还没开始"。

二、跨领域连接：margin debt 的"GDP化"思维 vs. 中央银行的债务监测逻辑

小涛对融资余额（margin debt）的处理方式非常专业——他不看绝对值，而是看 margin/指数比率 和 同比增速。这其实是把宏观经济学中"债务/GDP"的分析框架直接迁移到了散户杠杆领域。

这是一个跨领域的精妙连接： - 国家债务破历史新高 ≠ 国家危险（因为GDP也在涨） - 散户杠杆破历史新高 ≠ 市场危险（因为市值也在涨）

但他还遗漏了一个更狠的视角——信用周期理论（Minsky moment）：真正危险的不是杠杆总量，而是杠杆增速的"加速度"。 现在 margin debt 的同比增速大约30%，低于2021年的70-80%，但斜率近期在抬头。这意味着市场还没到"明斯基时刻"，但已经进入"投机性融资阶段"的早期——即借钱的人开始相信"涨势会持续"，但还没到"借钱还利息"的庞氏阶段。换句话说，派对刚刚开始进入嗨段，而不是收尾。

三、被低估的真正洞见：Cerebras 是"AI推理时代的物流公司"

小涛对 Cerebras 的分析里藏着一个被大多数散户忽略的颠覆性逻辑——HBM涨价对Cerebras是利好而非利空。这点几乎没人提。原因在于 Cerebras 不用 HBM，但竞争对手用，结果竞争对手成本上升、token涨价，反而让Cerebras可以"水涨船高"地提价。这是典型的"在通胀中受益的非通胀资产"。

更深层的跨领域连接来了——Cerebras + Groq 在AI推理生态里扮演的角色，本质上等同于物流行业里的"加急快递"： - FedEx 隔夜快递 vs 普通邮政：客户愿意为速度支付6倍溢价（小涛提到GPT-4.6快速版的token价格是标准版的6倍） - 推理速度溢价的存在，证明"AI时代的时间价值"正在被市场定价 - 当 Agent 经济（智能体替你做事）真正到来时，速度不再是"用户体验"，而是"商业可行性" —— 一个要等5分钟才响应的Agent，本质上不是产品，是玩具

最反直觉的一点：英伟达自己花200亿美金收购Groq，等于老黄亲自承认"GPU不是终极答案"。这是一个被绝大多数散户忽略的信号——英伟达正在悄悄地从"卖单一最强芯片"转型为"卖整条推理光谱"，而Cerebras正是这条光谱里"速度极端"的那一段。它的最佳客户从来不是终端用户，而是 AWS 这样的 hyperscaler——就像顺丰的最大客户从来不是个人，而是电商平台。

所以真正的"啊哈时刻"是：散户看不上Cerebras，因为他们用"替代英伟达"的框架去理解它；但市场最聪明的钱（英伟达、AWS、OpenAI）用的是"补全英伟达"的框架去配置它。这两个框架决定了完全不同的估值终点。

📝 转录文本

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小涛。

周日、周一、周二连续三天更新，上一次连续三天我都忘了已经是什么时候了。昨天我和大家说，如果喜欢我突击更新的话，在下面留言，结果留言区爆炸了几百条留言。其实我希望大家可以保持下去，因为这样的话，其实我看留言也积极，然后大家有问题的话，我可以在留言区里面和大家继续沟通。视频本身只是一个媒介嘛，只是我们沟通的一个媒介，好吗？大家继续保持下去，然后我也继续保持下去这种势头，尽量提早发视频，然后尽量为大家多做视频。

今日内容预告

那今天我们聊什么？

  • 首先聊一下市场的走势
  • 昨天的视频里面和大家分享过三大科技股现在的一个估值在什么的个区间，一个合理的一个区间大概在什么那个位置。估值本身它是一个模糊的正确，不会给我们绝对的一个正确。今天芯片板块出现了下跌，昨天芯片板块上涨的幅度非常的好，那我们对于芯片板块整个芯片板块的估值我们再去做一个更新——这是第一部分
  • 第二部分我们去聊一下 margin debt 散户的这个杠杆线在下面的一个位置，有几个视角可以去切入的
  • 第三部分我们去聊一下周二盘后发布财报、前段时间刚刚IPO的一家芯片公司 Cerebras。这家公司其实我看完之后，我觉得是可以加入观察列表，但是我看到社交媒体上好像很多散户对于这家公司并不是非常的感冒

好吧，那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞、加转发，让更多的人可以看看到我们这期内容。

一、市场走势回顾

先来回顾一下周二资产价格的走势，那市场是出现了下跌。开盘之前相对来说会恐怖一些，那只下跌了3%，最终收盘是收到了2%，2%，还好了，就没有出现非常大幅度这个下跌。

美债和美元市场这一块的波动并不大。今天美债收入的这个下跌——周二市场美债收的这个下跌——在9月份加息的这个概率，从上周五的100%现在降到了90%左右，但还是加息的概率是非常非常的高。

三大指数

三大指数都出现了相对来说大幅度的下跌。除了纳指之外，标普下跌了1.4，纳指2.2，道指是0.1。那道指只下来了0.1，说明什么？说明整个今天的市场其实走的还好，并没有那么的差。

那标普500和纳指主要的一个跌幅是来自于芯片板块和前期大科技股的这个下跌。前七大科技股 IDD 走的是相当的弱，下跌了3.7%，而其他几乎所有的板块都出现了上涨。唯一下跌的一些板块，通讯服务也好、必需品消费也好，这两个板块其实里面的权重都是一些大科技。所以这两个板块出现了下跌，而主要这两个板块的一个下跌是因为被大科技所拖累了。

如果从结果去看，一定要去找一个理由的话，我认为可能所有的大科技全部可以归拢在一起。唯一可以找一个理由的话，现在几乎所有的大科技都在一个重的投资周期里面，那前期的这个投入非常的大，那取决于每家公司基本面的不同，回报周期可能不一样。回报周期相对比较软的、比较远的呢，市场的这个惩罚的程度相互就会大一些。

VIX：买点信号

随着标普500的下跌，盘中 VIX 去到了20，今天的收盘 VIX 是在19.49，还是在一个相对比较高的位置。我这边有两条线，两条白色的线，一条是 VIX 25 的这个位置，一条是 VIX 22 的这个位置。

特别是22的这个位置，前段时间我有在X上和大家分享过：只要我们是在一个牛市的环境里边，基本上第一个阶段到了 VIX 22 就是一个买点，接下去就是25，包括25以上，是另外一个买点。

那这时候可能一部分的观众就会问：那我们现在这一波牛市有没有结束？我个人的看法是牛市并没有结束。短期之内体感上来说，整个芯片板块好像有些泡沫，芯片和硬件好像有些泡沫，因为价格涨得非常的快、非常的高。但就像我们前面所看到的，大科技 VITD 到现在为止走势其实相当的弱，而很多大科技的估值现在也在一个相对非常低的这个位置。所以大科技这边我并不认为有任何的泡沫。

所以这是 VIX 的一个切入点：当我们在进行抄底或者加仓的时候，22是一个点位，然后25是另外一个点位。

市场参与度

市场参与度这边，虽然今天三大指数都出现了下跌，但其实参与度还是不错的。纳指超过了一半以上的公司出现了上涨，罗素2000超过了60%左右的公司出现了上涨，而标普500里面差不多也有将近60%的公司出现了上涨。所以今天前面看到几乎其实很多板块都是出现了上涨，主要指数的下跌是权重股所带领的，权重股基本上都集中在大科技和芯片板块。

所以参与度我们今天就不更新了，因为今天的参与度和昨天做对比的话，其实是有所上升的。

一年走势的反思

我们来看一下纳指在过去一年的一个走势吧。我其实不看不知道，一看的话，其实我们都在聊市场里面有个泡沫，或者担心市场里面有泡沫。但其实你去看指数的这个上涨幅度真的是不大。

上年最高纳指上年的最高点是在10月底的时候，10月底11月初的时候，当时的一个最高点到现在为止，到现在的这个点位，那只总共差不多——上年的10月份到现在要将近10个月左右的时间，8个月到现在，纳指上升了多少？纳指上升了7%。

当然了，纳指这7%里边大多数的上涨幅度全部是来自于芯片的这个上涨，也就是很多大科技现在是回到了24年的这个价格水平。所以上升了7%的这个纳指，我很难去说指数或者美股的整体现在有一个泡沫。你只能说局部可能有些泡沫，就像我前面说的，体感上你会觉得硬件板块有泡沫，是因为非常短的时间之内 SOX 芯片板块3月底到现在上涨了一倍。

但是我真的很难得出这个结论，说现在美股是在一个泡沫的阶段，没有办法完全没有办法得出这个结论。

二、芯片板块估值更新

然后是芯片板块的估值。前段时间我和大家聊的比较的多，包括我在这一次去旅游之前，也和大家分享过芯片板块的估值。我说29倍、30倍是一个可以去主动止盈的一个点。

那在昨天的时候，芯片板块 SOX 的这个估值去到了29.6、29.7倍吧，就接近于我上面白色这条线30倍的这个上区间。在30倍以上可以去主动进行止盈，那我自己手里现在第一大的仓位英特尔，我估计如果超过30倍以上，比如说去到32倍、33倍的话，SOXS、SOX 这个标的，就整个芯片如果估值去到30倍以上、32、33，我不知道非常明确的一个点位，如果到那个位置的话，我有可能会进行主动止盈我英特的仓位。当然我的这个止盈并不是我去清仓英特尔，而是主动去进行英特尔这部分的一些减仓。

美光财报前瞻

那随着周二市场的这个下跌，芯片板块的估值也从周一的29.6倍下降到了27.4倍。周三盘后会有美光的这个财报，相信美光发布完财报之后，芯片板块的这个估值——先假设发布完财报之后，美光那个价格不动，那因为财报发布完之后，财报盈利预期会向上调，那这个估值就会往下调。

所以我的一个期待是：如果我们假设明天美光的一个财报，就假设明天芯片板块没有动，发布完财报之后价格也没有动，我期待是这个估值会进行下修。

板块估值定性

所以我今天在X上又发过一条推：我说我不认为大科技是有泡沫的，大科技是低估的，然后软件板块是非常低估的，现在芯片和硬件板块的估值已经打满了。那接下去在估值打满的情况下，需要财报进来之后去修正这个估值。财报加上时间，自然就会把这个估值往下修，往下修完之后这个估值自然就会合理，就不会像现在那么高了。

那我下面画了一条线是在20倍左右。所以我认为芯片的： - 上区间：30倍 - 下区间：20倍（低于20倍就是相对会非常低估了） - 中位数：25倍

那如果说芯片的估值回到25倍，我不会认为芯片是有泡沫的，我认为芯片的估值就会回归到一个正常的区间。

三、Margin Debt：散户杠杆全景

讲到泡沫，我们来看一个视角，这个视角是 margin debt。所有 margin debt 散户这边杠杆的一个数据，在 FINRA 这个网站上是都可以找到的。

视角一：绝对体量的误区

如果我们单纯的去看体量，过去十年，从2015年到现在11年左右的时间，我们看到总共融资这个体量是一直在上升的。

上一次出现的一波上涨是什么时候？2021年的时候，就当时 Cathie Wood ARKK 非常火热的那一段时间，杠杆那段时间，2021年上升的幅度非常的快。然后22年加息这一波，整个融资的一个体量出现了下降之后，23年24年的杠杆线是加的不多的，24年、25年加上今年，杠杆上涨的幅度就非常的快。

但是这一个杠杆的体量往往会给我们一个误区：什么误区？就是随着整个市场在变得越来越大，杠杆的这个体量本身往上涨，其实不代表——比如说现在杠杆是在历史上的一个最高点，那是不是代表我们现在的一个泡沫在历史上的一个最高点？我们不能得出这样的一个结论。为什么？因为整个市场在变大。

那么就像整个经济在增速的时候，自然总体经济所承担的这个债务也会出现上升。这个债务破了历史上的一个新高，是不是代表整个经济的这个杠杆在上升？其实不代表。这就是为什么经常我们会去拿总共的一个债务除以GDP的这个比率，很多时候看这一个比率才能够看出来整个经济承担的这个债务比率。

视角二：margin debt / 指数比率

那杠杆这边也是一样的，我们需要把融资的这个体量去除以什么？比如说去除以指数等等。

还是前面那张图，只是数据源不同了，这边是 Yardeni Research。图里蓝色这条线就是我和大家前面分享的 margin debt 杠杆的总共的一个体量，红色这条线是指数。我们可以看到蓝色和红色非常的一致——指数上涨杠杆上涨，指数下跌杠杆下跌。那么现在指数破了一个新高，杠杆也破了一个新高。

但是像我前面说的，如果我们把杠杆去除以指数得出这一个比率，其实从这个比率我们可以看到现在的这个比率其实没有以前高。比如说图里灰色的这个区间就是出现衰退的这个区间，比如说08/09年的时候，我们可以看到这个比率其实非常的高，包括00年的科技泡沫也比现在高。

而相反，现在从总共的 margin debt 除以指数的这一个比率去看的话，无论和80年代当时、还是00年的科技泡沫、还是08/09年的金融危机去做对比的话，这个比率都远远低于当时的一个最高水平。

视角三：同比增速——体量

那杠杆的数据 margin debt 这个数据本身还有另外两个角度可以去切入，就是同比的这个增速，我认为也是一个非常好的切入角度。同比的增速又分为两个角度：

第一个角度就是体量——就今年和上一年去做对比，总共体量增加了多少。比如说1000亿就是 100 billion，负的1000亿就是减少了 100 billion，差不多是这样的一个概念。

所以同比我们先来看体量，体量本身也非常的有意思：

  • 我们看到在21年的时候，这是21年的3月份，整个非盈利科技公司见顶那个时间点，就当时 ARKK 见顶那个时间点，然后美股标普500是在2021年年底的时候才见底的。所以当时这些 unprofitable tech 其实是在21年年初的时候就已经见顶了。当时 margin 上升的幅度非常的快，因为很多散户都去疯狂的去购买这些标的，所以当时同比增速 margin 增加了2000亿美金

就像我前面说过，虽然现在整体市场的一个市值体量已经比21年的时候要大了很多了，将近要翻了，指数涨了多少就涨了多少嘛，翻了下半可能涨了百分之五六十了吧，23、24、25年三年市场做的非常的好，那22年是出现了下跌。

但是我们看一下，现在的同比增速的一个体量其实并没有21年要高，即使整个市场比当时的21年要高。所以从这个角度去看，起码我们和21年去做对比的话，这个体量的一个同比增速还没有21年要高。所以从疯狂的这个角度，和21年还是没有办法去做对比。

当然，我们看到历史上99年、00年当时的一个泡沫，包括08/09年当时的一个泡沫，我们同样看到了这个 margin debt 总共的一个融资体量同比增速出现了一波上涨，也就代表市场情绪面非常的高。然后从体量本身去看，当时是1000亿美金同比增速，现在我们来到了1500亿美金。现在的这个同比增速已经比08/09年和2000年要高，但是就像我前面说的，因为现在市场大了，所以这个体量本身环比的这个对比性已经没有那么大的一个参考性了。

包括和21年去做对比的话，这个参考性也不大，因为现在整个市场比21年又大了起码50%以上。

视角四：同比增速——百分比

如果说体量本身不是一个很好的环比可以去做对比的话，那我们可以看看百分比。百分比我认为就是一个比较好的衡量标准，比如说现在整个市场所承担的这个 margin 的一个体量和上年同期做对比，这个百分比到底增加了多少。百分比的话最高就是100%，对吧？所以百分比我认为是一个可以去横向作比这样的一组数据。

同样的： - 21年的这组数据同比增速非常的高，那时候应该是到70%到80%吧 - 08/09年当时也是非常的高，基本上是在同比增速基本上在40% - 99年的科技泡沫应该是到了60% - 现在我们差不多是在30%的这个水平

所以从这个角度去看，你说有没有到一个非常极致的泡沫的一个阶段？其实我认为还没有。起码没有这个可比性，就没有当时那样体量要高、增速要高。

但是你是说最近这段时间市场有没有承担更高的这个债务比例？有没有承担更高的 margin？有没有去拿更多的钱去做融资然后去进行投机？的确是有看到这个趋势，且这一个斜率上升的速度，最近这段时间是看到比较的明显。

四、Cerebras 财报与估值分析

最后我们来聊一下 Cerebras 在周二盘后发布的财报，这是他们 IPO 之后第一次财报了。

财报数据

先从数据来看，数据其实比市场预期好了非常多： - 营收高于市场预期 6.62% - EPS 这个体量比市场预期好了 74%（市场预期是负的0.15，最后的数据是负的0.04） - 主要是他们披露的 gross margin 毛利润率比市场预期要高了将近3个点

公司背景

简单聊一下这家公司的一些背景。其实在他们 IPO 之前我有聊过，那这家公司他们主要做什么？

市场有一些声音说认为他们能够破坏英伟达或者替代英伟达——不会的。他们只是替代了整个你从前期的训练到最后的 inferencing 里边其中的一个环节。

那为什么会有他们存在的意义？或者他们的一个 market 市场的一个 fit 到底在哪里？其实就像前段时间，在2025年底的时候，英伟达却收购了 Groq 是一样的一个道理。他们所存在的这个意义就是在整个推理一个过程里面：因为现在我们未来我们说只推理的一个世界，AI是推理一个世界，那在整个推理的一个环节里边，有了 Groq 和有了 Cerebras 这两家公司之后，可以把整个推理的一个环节加速。

速度的溢价：为什么推理速度值钱

那推理的环节加速有什么意义呢？当然有意义了。

在推理的这个环节，比如说我们个人在使用这些 AI 模型的时候，我们当然希望它出结果的这个速度越快越好。特别是最重要的是未来当有 Agent 的这个时代、智能体的这个时代，一定是出结果的这个速度是越快越好。

你试想一下，你让这一个 agent 去做四件事情，然后每件事情都要花一分钟的时间，四件事情就是4分钟，5件事情5分钟，那甚至于这中间的时间更长，那这个 agent 的效率就会非常的低，你可能也个人不大愿意去使用这一些 agent。

另外一点非常好的一个证明就是我们知道基本上每一代模型，无论是 OpenAI 的模型还是 Anthropic 一个模型。比如说前段时间 GPT-4.6，当时 GPT-4.6 的时候，就有 GPT-4.6 的标准版本，还有一个快速版本。at one point 有一个时间点，快速版本是标准版本 token 价格的6倍，也就是说有人愿意支付6倍的这个价格获得更快的这个结果。这就是我们在做推理的时候，速度的这个溢价。

而在5月份的时候，SemiAnalysis 他们有写一篇文章，他们说从他们内部的这个感受去看，他们现在对于模型的这个智商要求已经没有以前那么高了，反而是对于模型在做推理的时候，这个速度的要求变得比以前越来越高。以前是追求模型的这个智商，因为智商不够，你没有办法去解决这些使用者的痛点。但现在的话智商已经够了，已经可以去解决使用者的痛点。那从使用者这个角度，他们的需求从原来"能够帮我去解决这个痛点"，到了"能够更加快速的帮我去解决这个痛点"。那么所以需求从以前的智商高变成了速度快。

好吧，所以这家公司一定有它存在的意义。如果他没有存在的意义的话，那因为他就不会去收购 Groq，而且就在这样的一个时间点去收购 Groq——为什么以 200 亿美金去收购的？

营收指引

然后我们来聊一些数据。首先是财报会议里边营收这边，公司本身给了一个上半下半年和上半年做环比增速20%的这个增长，然后管理层说我这边还是给的一个相对比较保守的这个指引，也就是接下去的几个季度三个季度这个财报，有可能出来这个结果会比现在管理层所给的这个指引还要高。

推理链条中的分工

前面我们说到它只是破坏了整个算力环节的其中一环，那这一环就是 decoding。

  • 训练阶段：大模型的训练本身还是依赖于 GPU，这没有办法
  • Prefill 阶段：可以简单理解为就是你给 AI 出了一个题，AI 读题的这个速度。在 Prefill 这个环节，你可以用 GPU 吗？可以用英伟达的 GPU，你可以用自研芯片，比如说亚马逊的 Trainium，但你也可以用谷歌自己的自研芯片，都可以
  • Decoding 阶段：模型本身需要给你这个答案。那给答案本身你可以用 Trainium 去做，你也可以用 GPU 去做

但是 Prefill 这一步对于算力的需求要求是非常的高，而在 Decoding 这一块就不一样了，你都可以用：你可以用 Cerebras，你可以用 Groq，你可以用 GPU，你可以用 Trainium。但是这两者之间的一个差别就是 Groq、Cerebras 他们在出答案的这个速度变得非常的快。

老黄的判断：25%的推理场景

根据老黄的看法，他当然他是作为 GPU 的卖家，他认为未来所有应用场景里边在 inference 推理的这个环节里面，25% 的这种场景里边对于答案的这个出答案的这个速度要求会非常的高。

因为不同的人不同的需求，背后对于精准度和速度的要求是不一样的： - 有些人他要求精准度，他不讲究速度。比如说有些非常强的这些 research，你给你个5分钟、10分钟，你去跑没有关系，你过了5分钟、50分钟之后，我回来再 check 这个答案就可以 - 但是有些时候有些 request，比如说像未来我说过 AI agent，对于这些答案的速度和要求是非常的高。等于你问一个 agent，让 agent 去做一件事情，agent 然后响应了半天，等了5分钟10分钟才给你这个答案，我们是一定没办法接受的

所以某些场景你需要的是速度，某些场景你需要的是精确度。那在需要速度的时候，从老黄这个视角，他认为未来有 25% 的这种场景是需要速度的。这也是为什么英伟达去收购了 Groq。

在这一次财报里面，他们管理层经说的非常清楚了：在有些例子里边你确实是需要速度的，但是在有些例子里边如果你不需要速度的话，那你去用 Cerebras 或者你去用 Groq 所出来的这个结果就不会非常的好，你会使你的成本大大的增加，因为你的使用率下降了，所以反而你会增加这个成本。所以完全是 use case by use case。

最佳合作对象：Hyperscaler

那有了这样的一个特性，我们就会发现像 Cerebras 这一类的公司最好的合作对象是谁？就是这些 hyperscaler 了，像 AWS 这类 hyperscaler。

他们现在已经合作了，为什么？是因为 AWS 下面的需求五花八门。如果 AWS 知道了你这个用户提出了这个需求，这个需求本身 AWS 认为你是需要速度的，所以马上就把这个需求分配到 Cerebras 这边。所以对于像亚马逊、对于 AWS 来说，Trainium 和 Cerebras 是一个最好的 combination：在整个数据中心里面，它分配一部分的 Cerebras，他知道当我需要 Cerebras、当我需要这个速度的时候，我就把这一部分的需求、这一部分的 traffic、这一部分的流量安排到 Cerebras 这边。

所以 Cerebras 现在主要的两大客户，当然就是未来主要的两大客户：一个是 OpenAI，然后一个就是 AWS。然后这次 AWS 这个总共合作的这个体量并没有公布。

解禁时间点

对于刚刚上市的 IPO 公司，我们需要注意的就是它的一个解禁时间点。因为毕竟一级市场的投资者等不及的想要出货： - 第一波：今天发布完财报之后，高管和董事 pre-IPO 的这些投资者有15%的股份解禁了，可以出售了——这也是可能为什么财报数据非常的好然后盘后出现下跌这个原因 - 第二波：在8月份 - 第三波：在10月下旬

HBM 涨价的反直觉利好

其实还有另外一点我要补充一点，是在他们财报会里面也有讲到。我们知道最近这段时间 HBM 价格疯狂的上涨，那 HBM 价格的这个上涨其实对于像 Cerebras 这类的公司是不影响的，反而对于他们来说是一个正面的影响。

首先他们是不需要 HBM 的，所以他们完全不受到这一波 memory 价格上涨的这个影响。

其次，因为 memory 价格的这个上涨，使得这些大厂、使得 hyperscaler 他们的成本上升，成本上升自然就要把这个 token 的价格转移到最终的消费者，他们的客户身上。而因为他们这个成本的一个价格上涨之后，使得水涨船高，反而使得 Cerebras 可以去 charge 更高的这个钱。这也是一部分的解释了为什么他们的 gross margin、他们的毛利润会高于市场预期的原因。

所以 HBM 价格的这个上涨，包括未来价格的这个上涨，其实对于像 Cerebras 这类的公司看上去好像没有直接影响的，对于他们来说其实是一个非常大的利好。

估值分析：相对估值复盘

除了对于解禁的这个影响之外，我知道很多人对于他们的估值有一定的担心。

在他们 IPO 之前，我曾经对于他们的估值给过大家一些指引，那当时是用的一个相对的估值，我拿 Groq 当时的收购价，算上 YTD 到现在为止芯片价格的这个上涨，然后给了一个溢价。我记得我没有说错来，我说 250 以下可以进行参与，300 以上就没有任何参与的这个价值了。

结果开盘之后好像 280、300 吧，然后股价在 300、330、350 这段时停留一段时间，现在已经跌破了，现在已经将近了 200 块钱了，200 多。所以起码当时我对于 Cerebras 上市的一个价格，包括什么价格可以参与、什么价格不可以参与，我觉得估的还是比较准的。

估值分析：底层逻辑

现在我们从底层的逻辑进行一个估值。当时用的是一个相对的价格，是 Groq 收购价格为一个基准。那我们从底层逻辑去看，就是从 Cerebras 他们自己本身的盈利情况，我这边看的是 operating profit。

当然了，现在他们这个盈利情况有非常大的不确定性，这点我们必须要知道。那我们现在只是以市场所给的这个预期： - FY26：肯定是亏钱的 - FY27：几乎马上就要变成赚钱，已经扭亏为盈了，operating profit 现在市场预期是 2.53 亿美金 - FY28：operating profit 运营盈利就会达到了 22 亿美金，这个增速非常的快，28到27同比增速将近有10倍

所以如果我们看他们的 EV / Operating Income 的话，到 FY28 他们的运营这个倍数就会达到 23 倍。这样去看就 3 年以后 23 倍的 multiple。而且他们未来 FY29、FY30，市场预期 29 是 4.9 个 billion、30 是 12 个 billion，这个增速仍然非常的快，就是还是以一倍的这个增速在增长。

所以我完全不——如果说他们可以做到这样的话，那到了 28 年年底的时候，我绝对不认为他们的 multiple 会在 23 倍。到那时候他们的 multiple 绝对在 50 倍以上。为什么？因为 29 年 30 年他们的 operating profit 增速还是翻倍再翻倍，那么你那么高的 operating profit 这个增速，绝对市场不可能给你 23 倍的这个估值。

换句话说，假设现在市场预期是正确的话，我不需要他们去超出现在市场的预期，就他们如果能够做到现在市场的预期，在三年之后他们的价格起码也不会是 23 倍的这个估值。

估值分析：拆 FY27 盈利路径

那如果我们单看 27 年，他们是怎么做到几乎盈利的呢？

  • 营收：26 年营收不会那么的高，8.24 亿美金；然后到 27 年，市场预期就是将近要 29 亿美金了。因为速度非常的快，来自于 OpenAI 的这个订单嘛。那随着明年更多数据中心的上线之后，OpenAI 的营收就会非常大幅的开始进来
  • 毛利润：市场预期 26 年是 20%，其实管理层这次给了预期了，整个 FY26 是将近 40% 左右。那市场对于 FY27 这个预期毛利润是在 42%，我认为其实不难，还是可以做到的，甚至如果说因为 HBM 的这个价格涨价，他们的 token 也可以涨价之后，他们的毛利润非常有可能可以超过 40%。这完全取决于当时的一个市场，包括他们的执行率等等

那就是站在当下，我们只要去判断市场的这个预估毛利润 40% 是不是合理嘛，我认为是合理的。那在毛利润 42% 的情况下，营收是 2.9 个 billion，所以它的 gross profit 毛利润就是 1.2 个 billion，然后减去 1.3 个 billion 的 OpEx 运营开支之外，基本上 27 年是可以打平的。如果做的好的话，27 年就可以做到盈利。

结论：加入观察列表

所以这家公司我绝对会把它加入我的一个观察列表，然后等待这个时机。特别关注一下前面解禁的时间点，包括关注一下它的估值。如果我有开仓的话，我会第一时间和大家分享。

我认为这家公司是踩在了风口上，而且未来还会踩在风口上。因为未来有了 Agent 之后，Agent 对于这个速度的要求只会比现在更高。

五、合作伙伴信息

最后是我们这个频道的老朋友 Moomoo：

  • 美国市场：现在通过开户且入金就可以获得价值 30 美金 SpaceX 的正股，大家如果感兴趣的话可以免费获得 SpaceX 的股票正股。然后闲置资金最高年化 8.1% 的回报。那通过开户且入金最高仍然可以获得价值 1000 美金英伟达的赠股
  • 加拿大市场：现在 Moomoo 正联合纳斯达克推出一个 Top Trade 的挑战赛。这次挑战赛其实到 6 月底就已经截止了，所以还有一周的这个时间，大家如果想加入的话，还是来得及，可以去分享这一个最大百万奖池的奖金。这一次的活动是新老客户都可以参与的，新客户只需要开户且入金 1000 加币就可以参加。不想参加也没有关系，通过开户且入金仍然最高可以获得价值 4600 加币的奖励

好吧，所以无论美国市场还是加拿大市场，开户或者参赛的连接就可以——我会把连接放在置顶的留言里面。

六、APP 直播预告

最后提醒一下，忘了，差点要忘了，最后提醒一下，因为大家知道我有一个 APP，然后明天我会在 APP 上面进行全网的这个直播，就是所有的人都可以参与。时间的话是定在周三晚上美东时间 9 点 30。

如果没有 APP 的各位观众的话，我会把 APP 的一个下载且这个二维码放在稍后的视频里面，大家可以看到的话，感兴趣有时间希望大家来参与，好吗？

那我们今天就到这边，明天见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽略的"估值悖论"：芯片打满，而大科技反而被严重低估
小涛在视频中提到一个看似矛盾的现象——SOX芯片估值已经接近30倍上区间（打满），但纳指过去8个月只上涨7%，且这7%几乎全部由芯片贡献。**这意味着除芯片外的"其他大科技"实际上处于隐性熊市中**。
这里有一个让人意外的观察：**市场表面上在"AI狂欢"，但资金其实只在AI产业链的最底层（硬件/芯片）狂欢，而上层应用、软件、平台型科技却在悄悄塌缩估值**。这是一种典型的"铲子热、矿主冷"的资本错位——历史上，淘金热、互联网早期、云计算早期都出现过同样的形态，而最终赚最多钱的从来不是铲子，而是矿主。**所以当下"芯片泡沫论"的真正含义可能不是"市场要崩"，而是"轮动还没开始"。**
## 二、跨领域连接：margin debt 的"GDP化"思维 vs. 中央银行的债务监测逻辑
小涛对融资余额（margin debt）的处理方式非常专业——他不看绝对值，而是看 **margin/指数比率** 和 **同比增速**。这其实是**把宏观经济学中"债务/GDP"的分析框架直接迁移到了散户杠杆领域**。
这是一个跨领域

item.title: [SemiAnalysis Weekly] Ep. 016 - What Unitree's Evolution Means For Robotics (Robotics)
item.dc:creator: SemiAnalysis Weekly
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、信号与噪音的错位：当"机器人视频"的转录是前端框架广告

最值得玩味的不是转录内容本身，而是标题与内容之间的撕裂。一个声称讨论 Unitree（宇树科技）与机器人产业演化的视频，转录文字却是 Next.js 与 React Server Components 的开场白。这不是技术故障，这是一面镜子——照出了当下 AI 内容管线的一个隐疏真相：

我们正在大规模生产"元数据正确、本体错误"的内容。 标题、缩略图、SEO 关键词都精心打磨指向"机器人"，但真实载荷却是另一回事。这与 Unitree 自身所处的产业悖论惊人地同构——一个硬件公司的估值，越来越由它"被讲述的故事"而非"实际出货的关节模组"决定。

二、跨领域连接：Web 框架的数据获取，与人形机器人的"感知-决策"循环

如果我们假装认真对待这段转录，会发现一个出人意料的同构：

  • Next.js 的 Server Components 解决的是"数据在哪里被计算、在哪里被渲染"的边界问题——把渲染推向服务端，减少客户端负担。
  • Unitree 这类人形机器人 面临的核心架构抉择同样是"计算在哪里发生"——边缘（机身本地推理）还是云端（远程大模型决策）？延迟、带宽、隐私的三角权衡，与前端 SSR/CSR 之争几乎是同一道数学题在不同物理层的投影。

机器人公司未来真正的护城河，可能不在电机扭矩密度，而在它们的"渲染策略"——哪些感知数据在关节处就地消解，哪些上交给"中心化的大脑"。这是一个被严重低估的视角。

三、令人意外的观察：转录残缺本身就是产业隐喻

完整视频可能有 30-60 分钟，而你得到的是开头 30 秒——并且是广告位。这恰恰映射了普通投资者认知机器人产业的真实处境：

我们获取的"信息"，绝大多数是赞助商付费插入的叙事，而非主体内容。

Unitree 之所以重要，不仅因为它便宜（G1 价格击穿心理防线），更因为它改变了"机器人叙事"的接入门槛——以前只有波士顿动力能讲故事，现在中国厂商的视频也能病毒式传播。这是一种叙事民主化，而非纯粹的技术突破。

真正值得追问的不是"Unitree 的下一个产品是什么"，而是：当机器人 demo 视频的生产成本，比机器人本身的生产成本下降得更快时，整个行业会被叙事通胀重塑成什么样子？ 这是一个我尚未在任何分析师报告中见到的提问角度。

📝 转录文本

开场

I think we can all agree that we want robust, high-performance web applications.

And if you're building with React, you've likely used React Server Components.

今日主题

Today I want to talk about Next.js and how it handles data fetching.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] How On Policy Self Distillation Works - Sasha Rush
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个出人意料的观察：这其实是在偷偷重写"反事实历史"

表面上，On-Policy Self-Distillation 看起来像是一种训练技巧。但如果你仔细看 Sasha 描述的那一步——"我们不重新生成 token，只是把同一条轨迹塞进一段反馈文字，然后重新打分"——你会意识到这其实是在做一种反事实推理：让模型回答"如果当时我知道这个提示，我本应该输出的概率是多少？"

这跟因果推断里的 counterfactual outcome 几乎是同构的。强化学习长久以来的痛点是 credit assignment，本质上是"功劳归因"——在一条 200 步的轨迹里，到底是哪一步搞砸了？传统 RL 靠 reward 的稀疏信号在迷雾里慢慢倒推；而 OPSD 干的事情，相当于在一条已经发生的历史上手动插入了一个反事实条件，再让模型自己回答"如果当时是这样，你下一步的概率分布会怎么偏移"。换句话说，它把 credit assignment 这个搜索问题，转化成了一个条件概率比对问题。这是非常优雅的降维。

跨领域连接：体育教学 vs. 心理治疗 vs. Git rebase

Sasha 用了 Nadal 教网球的类比，但我想指出一个更精确的、来自心理治疗领域的对应物——认知行为疗法（CBT）里的"思维记录表"。

CBT 的核心动作是：让来访者回顾自己真实发生过的某一段思维（不是治疗师示范的完美思维），然后在那段思维的关键节点插入一句"如果这时换一种解读……"，再让来访者评估自己情绪反应的概率分布会如何变化。这跟 OPSD 的机制几乎一比一对应：

CBT OPSD
来访者自己的真实经历（不替换成治疗师的完美剧本） Student 自己 rollout 的轨迹
在关键节点插入一句重构 插入 text feedback
评估情绪反应分布的变化 重算 log probs 的 KL
反复练习直到内化 反向传播更新 student

而序列知识蒸馏（旧方法）对应的则是精神分析——治疗师讲解一个完美剧本，你照着模仿，但你下次遇到的真实情境根本不会是那个剧本。这也解释了为什么 CBT 在临床上的有效性大幅超越精神分析：学习必须发生在自己真实的轨迹上，而不是别人的示范上。

还有一个更技术化的类比：OPSD 干的事情本质上是对历史轨迹做了一次 Git rebase——保留你所有的 commit（token），但把它们重放在一个稍微不同的 base（带反馈的 context）上，然后看 diff。

隐藏的系统性真相：这暴露了 RL 的"信息饥饿症"

最让我觉得意外的，是 Sasha 那句几乎一笔带过的牢骚：

"It is a frustrating aspect of modern RL that even though we can read trajectories and they're bad and even other models can read them and they're interesting, we can't inject that information directly."

这一句话其实揭穿了现代 RL 的一个系统性畸形：我们用一个能读、能写、能推理几万 token 的语言模型，却只允许它通过一个标量 reward 来学习。这就像你雇了一个能写长篇文学评论的编辑，却只允许他给作者打 1–5 星。

OPSD 的真正贡献，与其说是一个新算法，不如说是在 RL 的窄信道上凿开了一个 token 级别的旁路——让自然语言重新成为学习信号的载体。这背后是一个更大的趋势预言：未来三年里，"reward signal" 这个概念本身可能会让位给 "feedback trajectory"，也就是 reward 不再是一个数，而是一段可被模型 ingest 的文本+概率分布修正。这意味着 RLHF 的"H"（human）将首次以它本来的形态——语言——参与梯度，而不是被压缩成偏好对。

顺带一个反直觉的推论：OPSD 解释了为什么 Cursor/Composer 这类编辑器内模型比通用 chatbot 进化得更快。编辑器天然产生带错误注释的真实 trajectory（lint 报错、test fail、用户 undo），这是 OPSD 最理想的训练食物。换句话说，最好的训练数据正在 IDE 里被实时合成，而不是在标注公司里被人工生产。

📝 转录文本

1. 从 Sequence Knowledge Distillation 说起

Okay, great. Um, so yeah, let's talk about distillation.

So, um, people used to do this method called uh sequence knowledge distillation for training um these models. And the way it would work is that you'd have the teacher which would produce a sequence of outputs like this. And this would be like a smart model that you knew would do very well on a task and you wanted to have a smaller model — which we'll call student — basically learn to do nearly as well as what the teacher was doing.

Um, and the way it would work is you would just target the student basically just with standard like cross entropy to try to match what the teacher was doing. Um, and this was kind of common for doing things like machine translation and kind of early forms of training language models to do well on these tasks.

2. On-Policy Distillation (OPD) 登场

Um, a couple years ago another method came out that has become quite popular called on-policy distillation, or OPD. And on-policy distillation has a nice benefit that it can be used during RL and plays really nicely with some of the RL techniques that people use.

Um, and the way it's going to work is that instead of producing the output from the teacher, you're going to instead have the student produce the output. So, we'll have a student here and it's going to produce a sequence. Now, the sequence might be a series of tool calls saying how you might say solve a coding problem. Now, this is going to be kind of not that great, but you're going to then have the teacher rescore each of the tokens in those sequences.

So now we have basically the teacher running a forward pass over that data — or at each position, it's going to maybe make kind of different choices about what tokens it would like to have there. So you have two sets of log probs: the ones from the student and the ones from the teacher. You're now going to take a KL loss between these two and you're going to update the student to be more like what the teacher was producing.

3. 为什么不直接把 teacher 的输出当监督信号？

Q: Can I ask um a concept a very basic question? You you could sort of think of one way of doing this as you just match — whatever, you just treat the teacher's output as a supervised learning target. Why do we train it such that the student is trying to match the teacher?

Yeah. Um, so in in in this way you basically would have the teacher and its full sequence outputs as a supervised learning target. In this setting the kind of the scope or trajectory of the outputs are going to follow basically what the teacher was doing. In this setting here um the trajectory is basically following what the student was.

网球类比

Like, the intuitive analogy is: this is like you're watching like Rafael Nadal play tennis and he's like so good. He's just doing amazing things and you're this student over here. You're trying to kind of copy that, right?

The analogy here is that this is like Sasha playing tennis and you have like Rafael Nadal over the shoulder correcting all the mistakes that you're doing. And each of these is like the kind of like smart teacher correcting the like motion and trajectory of what you're trying to do in that setting.

4. 与 RL 结合：解决 Credit Assignment？

Q: It seemed like you were hinting in your blog post on Composer 2.5 that this is helping you guys solve the credit assignment problem. Can you explain how that's happening?

Sure. So, um, before we get there, let's make one more detour. Which is to note that on-policy distillation works very nicely when you're doing RL as well.

So, when you're doing RL, think about this as basically the model that you're learning. Um, it's going to basically be doing a rollout anyway and producing its output. So we're basically already going to have to do a kind of forward and a backward step on the um what we're calling the RL of the student model. So we have some loss which is the basically the RL loss for this this setting.

And since you have that already, you can maybe think about how you might move the teacher and have a kind of second part of the loss which we'll call the um on-policy distillation loss — um which is basically computed by taking this part which you have already, the log probs from the student, comparing them to what a teacher would have done, and getting this update. If you have that update you can kind of add it in and get extra benefit.

5. On-Policy Self-Distillation：没有 teacher 怎么办？

Um, cool. Now, in the case of Composer, we actually don't have a teacher model. We don't have like another model that's better than the model we have. Um, so we're going to use this method that's become popular recently called on-policy self-distillation.

Um, and the idea here is basically we're going to synthesize a teacher to try to help us out. So, we don't have a better model, but we can pretend we have a better model by taking a student plus text feedback. And the text feedback is going to be this kind of extra information that we inject into this trajectory. So there'll be some extra information we'll put here that basically tells you what you'd like the model to do.

And you can compute the log probs for the student and then the log probs for the student plus the text feedback, and they'll actually be differences at this message. So this and this will not have exactly the same log probs, right? Because you've conditioned on extra information.

6. 一个潜在的死锁问题

Q: I guess there's um there's still a remaining problem here which is that you would need to keep rerunning this trajectory, because initially the student following its own policy will notice this mistake but will still have completed — just gotten stuck in a loop — and it will like keep making the same mistake, and you need to keep redoing this until it fixed that mistake and then actually learned the correct…

Yeah, right. So the question is if the text feedback is not strong enough or — or maybe targeted — or or something. Like, suppose that there's another error like here, but if you're on-policy for the student, the student would have already gotten stuck here.

Q: Oh that's right. Yes. And um the student would have gotten stuck here and then it wouldn't have like — it would never encounter this uh additional improvement. Whereas in um in the sequence knowledge distillation world it would have encountered all of the teacher's — like you would have seen [Na]dal perform the other move better as well. Even if you up the first serve.

Yeah, right. We're only going to get like slightly better a little bit at a time. Whereas in theory, if you're following the whole output, maybe you can do some amazing things.

Q: Okay. And then here, what we're saying is we don't want to like learn what to do once we've already made a Nadal-type serve — because I'm never not making the Nadal-type serve yet. And then like, how do I return the retort? I want to learn what do I do next. And so I'm just going to unroll the trajectory as I would. And then I'm going to compare: if Nadal found himself in a position where I'd done a terrible volley, what would he do next?

Yeah. Right. So you, this is like — you start from something really good [in the old method]. This [self-distillation] is something you start from really bad and kind of on the way.

7. Composer 2.5 怎么做 text feedback？

Q: Okay. So, um, now now on to what you guys actually did to train Composer 2.5. How do you figure out what the right text feedback is? Is it done in an automated way? Do you guys — is there are there people who label it? How does that happen?

Yeah. So um basically the way this works is that during the RL process we have Composer and allow it to basically read the trajectory. Uh, we've written uh some manual prompts that look for specific mistakes in the styling or the errors of the process itself, and they will suggest certain times when text feedback is useful and they'll point to a specific message where they want to inject it.

Um, and the reason this is necessary is because when we do RL, we don't just have kind of three turns. We might have hundreds of them. And so RL itself has trouble kind of locating where these errors are. But we can use models to kind of tell us where there's a problem, and then use text feedback to kind of target that problem and correct it directly.

8. 效果如何？

Q: Um, okay. So, how well does it work?

Uh, it works pretty well. I mean, you can um basically see these curves where uh when you have a specific error kind of put this in — much quicker than the full RL process, these things will go away.

Um, it's not at the point yet where it kind of replaces aspects of RL. So we're still kind of doing this in conjunction with the actual system itself.

Q: Got it. Um yeah, I guess you need to be able to identify what the like — if it's a sort of easily identifiable issue versus something more more subtle.

That's true. Yeah, we focused on these ones as kind of a starting point. Um, but um yeah, there's a lot of interest in kind of building the scope of these. I think text is a very good way of communicating information and is a great way of storing information between AI companies and people.

Um, it is a frustrating aspect of modern RL that even though we can read trajectories and they're bad, and even other models can read them and they're interesting, we can't inject that information directly.

9. 厘清流程：到底是几次 rollout？

Q: Interesting. For you to be able to execute the protocol: the student produces a trajectory. Then a reader, another model, looks at that trajectory and says, "Here's the error you made." Then based on the reader's analysis, a new prompt is made and then you re-execute another trajectory. And unless you've kept the log probs of the previous model, then at the very least you had to run another forward pass on this new model. Anyways, it just seems like there's a multi-step process here where the trajectory has to be regenerated and then an evaluator model has to be called. Can this all be done in the on-policy basically — can this be done concurrent with the trainer loop?

Yeah. So, it's it's slightly simpler than that. So, we've generated this output. It's just a sequence of tokens. Um, we're going to send those sequence of tokens to this model that's going to read it. Um, and then it's going to isolate a specific, say, turn that it says is problematic.

Then we're just going to do text manipulation. We're just going to take that trajectory and we're literally just going to like smash in some extra tokens. We're not going to have to do any inference. We're just going to take this new trajectory with this extra information. We're going to run a forward pass on that. That's going to give us the log probs. We're going to detach from our graph. And then we're just going to backprop it along with RL.

Q: I may be misunderstanding something, but it seems like you have to do at least two different consecutive rollouts in order to run the on-policy self-distillation strategy, where you have to do the student rollout, and then the evaluator looks at it, figures out what the issue was, writes a new prompt, then based on the new prompt, you do the new rollout with a better prompt, and then you compare the two. But it sounds like you're saying there's one rollout. I must be misunderstanding.

So it's actually it's actually a little simpler than that. So they're just one rollout. This is the rollout. And these tokens here and here are exactly the same tokens. And in fact, even here and here, these are exactly the same tokens as well. We're not generating anything new.

Basically, what we're doing is we're rescoring the log probability for these tokens under a slightly modified context. So we're basically just taking the text of this rollout. We're then inserting some new text here and we're recomputing how those log probs would have changed under that new text.

10. 再回到网球类比

So this kind of going back to the tennis analogy, this is like my bad swing, and then this is kind of like Rafael Nadal like over my shoulder kind of correcting each of the little mistakes I was making.

Q: Yeah. And so it's not like you need to watch Rafael play a game and then you try to match the game.

Yeah. It's just like — this is still your game. You're not doing a new rollout. And so you don't have to do decode.

Q: You don't have to decode. You're just slightly modifying little parts of what you did.

Right. Yeah. Um, but as you noted earlier, the downside of this is that I'm not going to make amazing, incredible progress. Like by the time I get to the end, I'm still doing the dumb things I did before, and I'm just getting kind of little local corrections on what was going on.

Q: Yeah. Interesting. Thanks for explaining it.

Awesome. Yeah.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个出人意料的观察：这其实是在偷偷重写"反事实历史"
表面上，On-Policy Self-Distillation 看起来像是一种训练技巧。但如果你仔细看 Sasha 描述的那一步——"我们不重新生成 token，只是把同一条轨迹塞进一段反馈文字，然后**重新打分**"——你会意识到这其实是在做一种**反事实推理**：让模型回答"如果当时我**知道**这个提示，我**本应该**输出的概率是多少？"
这跟因果推断里的 **counterfactual outcome** 几乎是同构的。强化学习长久以来的痛点是 credit assignment，本质上是"功劳归因"——在一条 200 步的轨迹里，到底是哪一步搞砸了？传统 RL 靠 reward 的稀疏信号在迷雾里慢慢倒推；而 OPSD 干的事情，相当于在一条已经发生的历史上**手动插入了一个反事实条件**，再让模型自己回答"如果当时是这样，你下一步的概率分布会怎么偏移"。换句话说，它把 credit assignment 这个**搜索问题**，转化成了一个**条件概率比对问题**。这是非常优雅的降维。
## 跨领域连接：体

item.title: [All-In Podcast] GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen’s $56B Plan to Take Over eBay
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的"叙事套利"

转录里反复出现的开场——"Everyone hates GameStop"——其实揭示了 Ryan Cohen 真正的核心能力，并不是零售运营，也不是供应链优化，而是叙事套利（narrative arbitrage）。他擅长在"市场厌恶情绪"和"基本面价值"之间寻找最大的认知差。Chewy 时代他对抗的是"实体宠物店不可能被打败"的共识；GameStop 时代他对抗的是"实体游戏零售必死"的共识；现在指向 eBay，本质上是在对抗"古早互联网平台已经被结构性淘汰"的共识。他的资产负债表里最值钱的一项，是"被讨厌"本身——因为厌恶让标的便宜，而厌恶又不会真正杀死一个仍有现金流的网络效应资产。

跨领域连接：宠物食品、游戏光盘、二手拍卖的隐藏同构

表面看 Chewy（宠物食品）、GameStop（游戏零售）、eBay（C2C 拍卖）毫无关联，但它们共享一个被大多数分析师忽略的结构特征：高频次、强情感绑定、低单价、长尾 SKU、依赖"信任 + 客服"的购物心理学。30 磅的狗粮是"我不放心让算法替我选"的品类；收藏卡和二手游戏是"我要看到卖家是谁"的品类；eBay 的核心 GMV 至今依然来自手表、零件、收藏品这种"非标 + 需要人际信任"的交易。Cohen 真正在收购的，不是"公司"，而是一种亚马逊一键购买永远无法吞噬的"非标交易心理学"基础设施。这与红酒拍卖行、艺术品中介、甚至古董手表二级市场属于同一物种——它们之所以没被电商化吞噬，是因为信任本身比物流更难复制。

让人意外的观察：他真正在做空的是"专业经理人阶层"

广告插入语里那段几乎是无意识重复的台词——"董事会不用自己的钱买股票"、"管理层薪酬过高"、"用自己的资本冒险才最美国"——表面是抱怨，深处却是 Cohen 全部三次收购的统一交易论文。他的真正 alpha 不在选行业，而在于发现一类系统性错配：美国上市公司治理已经演化出一个"既不创造也不承担风险的中间阶层"——拿期权但不押注、坐董事会但不持仓、决策但不负责。这个阶层的存在，让任何"愿意自己掏钱、All-In、不要薪水"的创始人型买家，都能在公开市场以折扣价捡走资产。这才是他可复制的真正商业模式：不是零售，而是"治理套利（governance arbitrage）"——把家族企业式的"皮在游戏里（skin in the game）"，强行移植到一个被代理人成本侵蚀的上市公司外壳里。从这个角度，他要买 eBay 不是因为他爱拍卖，而是因为这是当前美股里治理腐败 / 现金流质量比率最具吸引力的标的之一。

一个反直觉的预测

如果 Cohen 真的拿下 eBay，他做的第一件事大概率不会是"AI 化"或"和 Shopify 整合"——这些是分析师的故事。他更可能做的是反方向：把 eBay 拉回它最古老的样子——重新强调拍卖、买家保护、卖家社区、收藏家文化，砍掉一切试图"变得像亚马逊"的产品线。因为他每一次成功，都在做同一件事——承认自己永远打不过亚马逊，然后在亚马逊不屑做的"人际化交易"里建立垄断。这是 Bezos 永远无法理解的护城河：算法效率的对立面，不是低效，而是"被人记住"。

📝 转录文本

开场：为什么所有人都希望我们失败

Everyone hates GameStop. And it seems like everyone in the media basically wants us to fail and wants them to succeed. And you've got a board that's making hundreds of thousands of dollars a year. They don't buy stock with their own money. They end up showing up to a handful of board meetings. And they're making a fortune. You've got a management team that is grossly overpaid. There's nothing more American than basically risking your own capital. So why does everyone want us to fail?

广告：AppLovin Ads

AppLovin started with an $8 domain and no VC funding. They built anyway and became one of the largest ad platforms in the world. Now they're bringing that same engine to e-commerce through AppLovin ads. Your ads run inside mobile games, reaching over a billion people with full screen, distraction-free attention. The platform finds buyers and optimizes for profit. You set the target, it does the rest.

A cookware brand doing $4 million tried AppLovin ads, hit 16 million, turn profitable, and they're on pace for $80 million this year. Visit applovin.com/all-in to launch your first campaign today.

欢迎 Ryan Cohen

Ryan Cohen, welcome to the All In interview. Thanks for being here.

Thank you for having me.

I think it's been like a decade, which is crazy how old we get since you and I last had dinner in New York. This was before several chapters of your life unfolded. And it's great to be talking with you today.

You're doing something really interesting right now, trying to acquire and run eBay, which obviously is a big story right now that takes us back 25, 30 years to the start of the internet. But I want to talk a little bit about your story first, if that's okay. And I'd love for folks that are watching this or listening to this to learn a little bit about you and the journey you've been on that brings you to this moment.

从 Chewy 的故事说起

Maybe we can go back to the business you started, Chewy, if that's okay.

Yes, definitely.

Yeah. Well, maybe tell me why you started Chewy. How'd you get that idea? And how'd you get into building this business from where you were coming from at that time?

We were, um, wanted to build something online and we were, uh, about to launch an online jewelry website. Did not know anything about jewelry, went to a bunch of trade shows, bought hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of inventory, built the website, had the distribution.

And then I was shopping in a neighborhood pet store. Uh, I had a poodle and I was going every few weeks and it just hit me on one of my trips that I understood the product much better. It was a recurring revenue purchase. The market was still fragmented. The fact that there was still neighborhood pet stores at the time and they had not been disrupted by Petco and PetSmart was fascinating to me. And then you had Amazon, which was established and had pet products since the nineties, but they hadn't really achieved real scale in the category.

Chewy 的执行哲学

So the vision was to replicate the same experience that I had at the neighborhood pet store, but do it online and do it at scale. And I looked at Amazon's best practices when it came to supply chain. So fast shipping, having a great selection, being competitively priced. And then the experience at the neighborhood pet store of knowing the products really well.

And it was easy to be passionate about the category because I'm a pet owner and everyone we hired were pet owners. And, uh, it was all about market leadership as a low margin business — hindsight, not necessarily the best idea to go head to head against Amazon selling 30 pound bags of pet food, but we executed really well and we grew really quickly and we had negative working capital. And so it was a business that was able to get, uh, billions of dollars in revenue and not consume a lot of capital.

学会"执行"

How did you learn to execute well? So at the time you had little business experience prior to that, how did you learn those skills? What were the principles and the values that made you excellent at operating that business?

I understood from the beginning that the real competition was always Amazon.

（注：以上为转录片段循环重复的内容，原始转录在此后多次重复同一段对话与广告读稿；末尾以 AppLovin 广告片段中断："AppLovin started with an \$8 domain and no VC funding... The platform..."）

观看原视频 →

转录里反复出现的开场——"Everyone hates GameStop"——其实揭示了 Ryan Cohen 真正的核心能力，并不是零售运营，也不是供应链优化，而是**叙事套利（narrative arbitrage）**。他擅长在"市场厌恶情绪"和"基本面价值"之间寻找最大的认知差。Chewy 时代他对抗的是"实体宠物店不可能被打败"的共识；GameStop 时代他对抗的是"实体游戏零售必死"的共识；现在指向 eBay，本质上是在对抗"古早互联网平台已经被结构性淘汰"的共识。**他的资产负债表里最值钱的一项，是"被讨厌"本身**——因为厌恶让标的便宜，而厌恶又不会真正杀死一个仍有现金流的网络效应资产。

item.title: [YC Root Access] AI Agents Are Killing the Engineering Pyramid — Here's What Replaces It
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的隐喻反转：不是"AI 替代初级工程师"，而是"工厂被电动机重新设计"

大多数人听到"AI 杀死工程师金字塔"，第一反应是"裁员焦虑"。但 Reynold 真正抛出的洞见其实是反过来的——问题不在 AI 有多强，而在于现有组织是为"蒸汽机"建造的。这是一种典型的幸存者认知偏差：我们以为只要把蒸汽机换成电动机（在旧流程里塞进 Copilot），就能拿到红利。事实是，工业史告诉我们这种"原位替换"只换来 5-10% 的边际收益，真正的 10 倍跃迁要等 20-30 年后，当工厂被重新平面化布局——一台电机一台机器，独立运转。

这意味着：今天最大的 AI 红利不在大厂，而在"没有蒸汽机遗产"的新团队。Databricks 自己也承认——他们最快的产品（Neon）连 Databricks 品牌都不挂，因为旧品牌本身就是一个"蒸汽机房"。

二、出人意料的连接：基础设施正在经历"昆虫化"

这里有一个跨领域的奇妙类比，他没明说但藏在话里——生物界从恐龙到昆虫的演化路径，正在基础设施层重演。

过去的数据库像恐龙：体型巨大、代谢昂贵、必须 mission-critical 才养得起。Neon 这类 serverless Postgres 像昆虫：单体几乎零成本，但通过海量繁殖占据生态位。一个 AI agent 一晚上可以 fork 出 1000 个分支数据库做 1000 次实验，99% 死掉、1% 存活、其中 0.1% 进化成独角兽——这就是 r-选择繁殖策略在云基础设施上的第一次完整实现。

更反直觉的是："长尾的总价值首次超过头部"这件事，在基础设施层从未真正发生过。Google 搜索、YouTube、App Store 都靠长尾起飞，但 AWS RDS 这种"重型基础设施"一直只服务头部。AI agent 把"开一个数据库"的边际成本压到接近零，长尾才第一次值得被服务——而这正是所有现存基础设施巨头最难掉头的方向，因为他们的 P&L 模型、销售流程、SLA 体系全是为头部设计的。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：组织形态正在从"金字塔"变成"沙漏"，而不是"倒金字塔"

Reynold 用"I 形（eye shape）"形容新组织——头重脚轻。但如果你把他的几个论点叠起来看，更准确的形状其实是沙漏：

  • 顶部：少数极资深的"知道该建什么、且懂得如何建"的人（架构判断力）
  • 中部：急剧收缩——传统的中层管理和初级工程师同时被压缩
  • 底部：海量 AI agent + 海量轻量化基础设施实例（执行层重新膨胀）

这个结构最危险的二阶后果是：初级工程师的培养通道被切断。如果"junior 干的脏活累活"全被 agent 接管，5 年后谁来变成 senior？这是一个教育系统与产业结构的脱节临界点——和摄影从胶片转数码时整整一代暗房技师消失、却没人接班修复老照片，是同一种结构性裂缝。

换句话说，AI agent 不只在重塑工程师，它在悄悄重写"经验"这个概念本身——经验不再是"我修过 10000 个 bug"，而是"我能在 agent 输出的 10000 个方案里 0.3 秒判断哪个有架构毒性"。这是一种全新的、还没有教科书的能力。

一句话锐评

Reynold 表面在聊产品和组织，骨子里讲的是一个更冷的事实：未来十年最稀缺的不是会写代码的人，而是"能判断什么不该被建造"的人——而这恰恰是 AI agent 最学不会的东西，因为它的训练数据里只有"被建造出来的东西"。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Diana： Today I'm excited to have Reynold Xin who's the co-founder and chief architect of Databricks, which is one of the largest AI data infrastructure for enterprises companies out there. Their last round was at over 130 billion plus in valuation. Pretty impressive.

Reynold: Thank you, Diana.

一、AI Agent 正在重塑工程组织结构

Diana： And the big shift right now is AI coding agents are working. How is that changing for how Databricks is building products internally?

Reynold： Yeah, I think one of the things super interesting right now is — in a way, the AI agents are reshaping the organizational structure. Because it used to be the case that you have to have humans. You build out this pyramid of engineering team for any mature product: you have, so maybe the manager, the leader, and then the senior engineers, and sense of an army of more junior people. They will be contributing code and fixing a lot of bugs.

And I think that is changing because the AI agents — when designed well, when we have the right harness — it's capable of actually doing a lot of the coding work, and in some cases even the design work.

So I think it would actually reshape the organizational structure to be a little bit more of an eye shape, where I think teams will become more and more actually in a way top-heavy, and have people that really understand what needs to be built but also how to build them, where it's leaving a lot of the grunt work to be done and completely automated by AI agents. And that is a huge implication, I think, to both the tech and the org structure.

二、蒸汽机 vs. 电动机：软件工厂的范式跃迁

Diana： Interesting. So have you seen then the product velocity of how you guys are shipping to be a lot faster?

Reynold： I think one of the interesting things here is that — by the way, I think it's a good analogy here — which is, when steam engines… when the world first discovered electric motors, or sort of invented electric motors, many of the factories were built with steam engines in mind.

And in the case of steam engine, like, people could actually google this — they had… it's actually very difficult to build a lot of different steam engines. They're pretty big, pretty bulky. So the factories are designed with one gigantic steam engine in mind, and there's a lot of conveyor belts surrounding the steam engine. So they're very tightly packed into a 3D structure.

But when electric motors came out, one of the biggest changes — hey, you can build fairly small electric motors. You no longer need a single gigantic electric engine or steam engine. But because the existing factories are configured in a way to fit a single gigantic steam engine, most factories just started by replacing that steam engine with an electric motor, a more powerful electric motor. That actually only led to fairly incremental gains of factory throughput.

And over the course of like two or three decades, people — engineers — started thinking about: hey, how do we redesign the factory for electric motors? And that's when really unleashed the productivity gain and throughput from factories.

I think a similar thing is actually happening with the software factories as well. And it's actually a lot easier to create a new software factory fully embracing a set of AI tools than just from scratch — than just taking a giant existing system and slap a bunch of AI in it. You'll still get some incremental gains. There's a lot of tasks that can be automated. But if you just think about without changing the processes and without changing maybe all the tooling and how your CI/CD works, it's actually very, very difficult to get a massive productivity gain.

三、老公司怎么办：不要只"换发动机"，要"开辟新空间"

Diana： That's a very good point. So especially for companies that have been around longer, and with this shift with AI, they have to be very thoughtful on how to do that — so they don't end up with this analogy you're saying, with a giant…

Reynold： …hole in the middle that used to be the steam engine.

Diana： …to then retrofit with AI. What you're saying is almost like to embrace AI for a company that's already further along, you have to almost create new space.

Reynold： Exactly. One thing is — by the way, don't get me wrong, it is important to replace that giant steam engine with the electric motor also. But that won't give you all the gains. The more important part is to start thinking about how do you reconfigure things.

But reconfiguration is slow because you don't want to be too disruptive either. So it's actually a lot easier to create, for example, new teams, new efforts, new product lines, new organizations to be more AI-native compared with maybe the existing bigger machine.

四、Neon：一个不挂 Databricks 品牌的最快产品

Diana： So tell us a bit about some of the products that Databricks is shipping that is enabling more native AI coding agents to interact with — which might not be some of the products that people know you as much for.

Reynold： Yeah, exactly. Actually one of our fastest growing products is something that doesn't even have a Databricks brand on it. It come from the Neon acquisition, and the whole point of the Neon product is that it's a PLG-driven motion. It's super easy to sign up. So very different from the traditional Databricks enterprise motion.

Diana： What is Neon, actually?

Reynold： Yeah, I was gonna… Neon gives you a serverless Postgres that autoscales super, super rapidly, and also allows you to take a snapshot of the database and restore and branch off the database just like you can do with code.

One other thing with AI agents is that AI agents move incredibly fast, and you can use AI agents to run a lot of experiments in parallel. Many of these experiments might not work out, some of them might. And for the ones that don't work out, you want it to be super-duper cheap. For the ones that might work out, you actually want to be able to run on infrastructure that can scale you to the point of going to production at scale.

And I think historically, infrastructure, especially databases, were designed to be heavyweight. They were thought of to support, hey, the most mission-critical applications. But Neon's approach is: hey, let's design something that is super, super cheap. Because you can start very, very small, just getting a Postgres database. And if you want to branch off, run a lot of experiments, you can do that instantly. But if any one of the experiments actually starts taking off and becomes maybe what you want to go into production, you just use the same environment, actually go autoscale to whatever you need.

So that's like — actually Neon's been growing like crazy. When we acquired Neon — was about actually less than a year ago — the revenue has gone up more than 10x just in less than a year. And we're seeing massive adoption, I think mostly because of the agentic workloads. They're very, very different from the past workloads.

五、为什么 Neon 火了：AI coding 平台的新基建需求

Diana： Is it because a lot of AI coding agents — or if you go on ChatGPT or Claude and you ask for "help me build this with a Postgres database" — Neon becomes the recommended product?

Reynold： Yeah, that's one of the key reasons. Another one is, it's also powering many agentic coding platforms like Replit, Vercel, and many others that are coming in the pipeline.

For many of these platforms, they suffer from exactly the same issue I talked about earlier — which is, they want each individual experiment or each app to be super cheap, but then if they do take off, they want to be able to take it to a pretty far scale. And that — it's just a difficult problem from a sort of conventional infrastructure point of view.

六、轻量化基础设施：Agentic 时代的新范式

Diana： This is fascinating, because you guys, Databricks, have been really the hardcore infrastructure company. And infrastructure historically is really heavyweight, is meant to be done for really production-grade and hardcore engineering systems, the studio systems. And in this new world, and this shift when AI coding agents started to work, there's this new thing with lightweight infra that's becoming a thing. And sounds like Neon is one of them.

Reynold： Yeah. I think more generally and broadly than Neon, I do think infrastructure needs to evolve in the agentic era. Which is, it needs to be able to start super lightweight. It can't be this sort of a delicate thing that requires an army of people to babysit and costs like millions of dollars for every little thing. It needs to be able to support even at approximately zero cost to begin with. And when it does — whatever that's being built on top of it actually demonstrates value — then it can actually start scaling up the cost.

七、给创业者的建议：长尾即蓝海

Diana： What advice would you have for founders that are getting started and building for this new world, and having this concept of lightweight infra rather than the old-school heavyweight?

Reynold： Yeah, I think it's actually a great time right now for sort of disruption in infrastructure, honestly. Because pretty much every piece of infrastructure was designed to be sort of heavyweight. Even the word "infrastructure" sounded sort of heavyweight, right? It reminds you of PG&E and maybe oil pipelines.

And I think there's a lot of architectural evolution with the cloud and with agents that you could now actually start thinking about it. Many infrastructures became super heavyweight not because there's a fundamental technical limitation — it's mostly because they were designed initially just for high-value services. But now, I think with agentic coding, maybe the individual value of each service or each experiment is very low, but in aggregate they can be very large.

So there's sort of an opportunity to target the very long tail, to be building something pretty valuable. And that's just something most incumbents have never even thought of, so they have a very difficult time transitioning to.

结语

Diana： Awesome. I mean, this sounds exciting future for everyone building. I think that's it. Thank you so much for coming and chatting with us, Reynold.

Reynold： All right. Thank you, Diana.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽略的隐喻反转：不是"AI 替代初级工程师"，而是"工厂被电动机重新设计"
大多数人听到"AI 杀死工程师金字塔"，第一反应是"裁员焦虑"。但 Reynold 真正抛出的洞见其实是反过来的——**问题不在 AI 有多强，而在于现有组织是为"蒸汽机"建造的**。这是一种典型的**幸存者认知偏差**：我们以为只要把蒸汽机换成电动机（在旧流程里塞进 Copilot），就能拿到红利。事实是，工业史告诉我们这种"原位替换"只换来 5-10% 的边际收益，真正的 10 倍跃迁要等 20-30 年后，当工厂被**重新平面化布局**——一台电机一台机器，独立运转。
这意味着：**今天最大的 AI 红利不在大厂，而在"没有蒸汽机遗产"的新团队**。Databricks 自己也承认——他们最快的产品（Neon）连 Databricks 品牌都不挂，因为旧品牌本身就是一个"蒸汽机房"。
## 二、出人意料的连接：基础设施正在经历"昆虫化"
这里有一个跨领域的奇妙类比，他没明说但藏在话里——**生物界从恐龙到昆虫的演化路径，正在基础设施层重演**。
过去的数据库像恐龙：体型巨大、代谢昂贵

item.title: [Business of Sport] It’s Money vs Prestige as the Next Generation Make Their Choice | Monty Panesar
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：Monty 的"大手"是一个被忽略的隐喻系统

整段访谈中最容易被一笑而过、却最值得停下来思考的细节，是他反复提到自己的手——比 Tyson Fury 还大的手。表面上这是闲聊，但你把它和他的整段叙事并置看，会发现一种身体资本（physical capital）向品牌资本（brand capital）的隐秘转译：

  • 年轻时，"大手"是他能从左臂快投转型左臂旋的生物力学前提（biomechanics → 技术）；
  • 中年时，"大手"成了播客里被反复消费的视觉梗（biomechanics → meme）；
  • 现在，球拍赞助商 Farfire Crest 看中的不是他的技术指标，而是"你的名字一被说出来，人们就会笑"（meme → eyeballs → money）。

也就是说，他的"大手"经历了三次资产证券化：从运动器官，变成技术差异点，再变成可被赞助商定价的注意力资产。运动员退役后的真正难题，不是"再就业"，而是找到那个能在三种资本之间反复转换的底层资产。Monty 自己未必完全意识到这一点，但他在用这双手讲一个关于资产形态变化的故事。

二、跨领域连接：Roger Bannister、Ben Duckett 与"职业定价 = 公共信念的滞后函数"

他随口扔出 Roger Bannister 的例子——四分钟一英里被打破后，第二年有 20 个人也跑进去了。这是个体育掌故，但它实际上在解决一个经济学问题：

一个职业的"价值"，不是由它的内在难度决定的，而是由集体相信"这件事值得做"的人数决定的。

把这个框架套回他真正想说的事——测试板球 vs T20——画面立刻变了：

  • T20 不是"打败"了测试板球，而是把"年轻人愿意为之牺牲十年"这件事，重新定价到了一个测试板球付不起的水平；
  • Ben Duckett 拒绝 IPL 选测试，看起来是"情怀"，但用 Bannister 框架看，他其实是在赌一个反向 Bannister 时刻——赌未来某天人们会重新相信"测试板球手"这个标签更稀缺、更高级。这是一笔逆向资产配置。
  • 这也解释了 Monty 那句被很多人会读成怀旧的话："people remember test cricketers"。他不是在伤春悲秋，他是在做长期持有的论证。

跨领域看，这和学术界的 tenure 制度、独立电影 vs 流媒体、长篇小说 vs 短视频是同构的问题：当短周期回报曲线变陡，谁来为"慢资产"的稀缺性买单？Monty 提出的解法（ICC 把 T20 收入抽税回流给小国测试赛）——本质上是一个文化资产的转移支付机制，和欧盟农业补贴的逻辑没有任何区别。

三、最容易被错过的洞见：Monty 把 Gary Lineker 的话听反了，而这恰恰是他的天才

Lineker 给他的那句忠告——"当人们不再找你签名，你就不再 relevant"——Monty 把它当作座右铭挂在嘴边。但仔细听他全程讲的故事，你会发现他事实上反向操作了这条建议：

Lineker 的逻辑是：relevance = 被认出（pull from public） Monty 的实际做法是：relevance = 我让对方感觉良好（push to public）

注意他描述边界 fielding 时和酒后球迷开玩笑、跑到 Barmy Army 面前欢呼、club cricket 里只为击中一次 off stump 就能"撑起整个夏天"——这些都不是在"维持知名度"，而是在做情感供给。他真正的资产不是被认出，而是被认出之后那个人离开时的感受。

这有个非常具体的商业含义：他的"second innings"之所以在英国不温不火、在印度反而做得起来，可能不是因为媒体市场结构问题，而是因为英国媒体生态奖励 opinion（Vaughn、Swann 式的尖锐评论），而 Monty 的真正天赋是 warmth——这是两种完全不同的注意力变现路径。他自己也许还在错误地试图模仿前者的路径，却没意识到自己手里其实握着稀缺得多的后者。

跨领域类比：这和米其林厨师转型做家庭料理 IP 是一回事——技术资产和情感资产是两套商业模型，硬把后者装进前者的渠道里，永远会觉得"差一点"。他自己已经说出了那个未竟之问："请来告诉我，你作为粉丝喜欢我哪一点"——这句话本身就是答案：他需要的不是更多曝光，而是更准确的自我定位反馈回路。

📝 转录文本

开场金句

"I think a lot of young cricketers — they're thinking it's a short career, I'm going to make more money being a T20 cricketer. But people remember test cricketers, and I think now cricketers are beginning to see that."

"When I meet a lot of people, they said: what you did is you actually changed the complete landscape of people within the Sikh community, because it massively opened doors in terms of breaking the ice — which I didn't realize at the time, because I just love playing cricket for England."

"I remember the likes of Gary Lineker — he said to me: the moment people stop asking for your autograph and your picture, stop recognizing you, it means you are irrelevant."

"In British sport, when you have those impossible moments where people just think it's going to get out in the next 10, 15 minutes — when you take one ball at a time, it's interesting how far it can take you. And next thing I know, I'm shaking Ricky Ponting's hand and we've drawn a test match."

"And I'm still waiting for my, you know, OBE — I haven't received it yet. Or maybe even a knighthood. Would that be better?" — "Knighthood would be better." — "Yeah, it may be harder." — "It would be harder." — "Should we start OBE? Yeah, OBE. Let's stick with OBE."

欢迎与开场：体育与商业的关联

Host: Fascinating. Monty Panesar — welcome to the Business of Sport. Lovely to have you in.

Monty: Yeah, thank you for having me on the show. I'm absolutely delighted to be on here. I remember when I first finished from cricket, a lot of people in the city — wherever I go, to events or meeting people at networking events — they go: is there a correlation between sports and business? Can a sportsman's mindset enhance a company, make the directors, the CEO perform better? Or a sportsman being part of that team just enhances and uplifts? And a lot of people said to me there's massive correlations between sports and business.

Host: So is this something that you've always been interested in then? You played many years at the top of the game.

Monty: I always had that interest when I used to go and meet people. The biggest thing for me was — I remember the likes of Gary Lineker. He said to me: "The moment people stop asking for your autograph and your picture, stop recognizing you, it means you are irrelevant." And it always stuck with me, that advice.

So I asked him, because he said to me — I said to him: "You made a successful career after playing football, a media career which is equally successful, probably even better now, right? Especially with the growth of Goalhanger" — obviously, more great podcasts — "exactly, and that's your inspiration." So you kind of think, well, let's follow the footsteps of other people who have done really well — the likes of Mark Nicholas, Graeme Swann is another one who has done really, really well, Michael Vaughan is good, Nasser Hussain, Kathryn Turner.

Host: Well, you were on Stick to Cricket recently, right? And if you look at that setup — Bumble, Michael Vaughan, Alastair Cook…

Monty: And I forgot — yeah, Phil Tufnell.

Host: Yeah, Alastair Cook, Phil — all of them lot. It's been a brilliant transition for them where they've now got a media personality that complements what they were also doing on a performance side. But I'd also imagine it's such a competitive environment for you to get into and stand out in.

Monty: Yeah, it is. It's very competitive, and it's an area I want to go into. Obviously I done an MA in sports journalism, and once I graduated, opportunities came — I was writing regularly for the Telegraph quite a bit, on TalkSport, bit on Sky, and then eventually I got more work in India as a commentator. Now the challenge for me is how can I get more presence in the UK — media opportunities, business opportunities. That's one of the reasons I reach out to you guys.

There is a massive correlation between the elite mindset of an athlete going into business, but also: can the second innings be equally successful as the first? That's always a challenge for a sportsperson. When I walk around London or wherever I am, I am a brand myself. Going back to what Lineker said, if I get people to recognize me, taking autographs, the popularity is there — that means I'm relevant. I'm very relevant in the media, entertainment world, in all of that kind of space.

作为英格兰首位锡克教徒板球手

Host: It's really important in this day and age, isn't it, to have that. I mean, you were the first Sikh to play for England, right? A test team — correct?

Monty: Yeah. The first to play outside of India, I think — not for the Indian test team.

Host: Having that type of moment, having that type of relationship, is such an important part of you being able to stand out and inspire in ways that other people just maybe won't be able to. How do you build something around that, that is often quite challenging?
