Right now I don't think any of the core assistants have that mentality — that referring people out makes them stronger. But I think that's the biggest opportunity.

主持人： What do you mean "referring people out"?

Josh： When I start trying to solve a problem with ChatGPT or Claude, it does everything it can to solve my problem itself. "Hey, I want to plan a trip." "Hey, I want to figure out a good bottle of wine." What would be really cool is if it said, "Hey, I know you're trying to plan trips — here are some things, and here are the resources to go deeper." They can't own them all. Being smarter about knowing when to hand you off — that'll be great. It'll create a lot of opportunity for those tools, and perhaps those tools are apps.

A lot of them will become apps and their own services. They'll have some chat experience, but a lot more — when you're planning a trip, chat's great to get a list of stuff, but then you've got to turn it into bookings and lots of other details. When you're dealing with a financial plan, it's great to work through problems but you still need to turn it into actual details. Those artifacts will be better managed in other services.

留存仍是最重要的指标

主持人： Are creators sufficient for AI consumer products to get to scale, or do we need an AI-native distribution channel?

Josh： I think the short answer is there's a lot of ways to get to scale today. People can get to 10 million, 50 million users in ways you never could before. All that really matters is if you get to them, do they actually use you? Do you stick? Do you become part of their lives?

I'm less worried about finding distribution methods for awareness as much as: what are the products that are actually going to move our habits and change us? Part of being an AI product today is it has to be so much better than the products were before.

ChatGPT was amazing for a whole bunch of searches that we thought were totally solved through Google. All of a sudden you start putting those searches into ChatGPT — "this answer is more than 10 times better, this experience is more than 10 times better than Google links back and forth." That's why ChatGPT blew up.

For every experience consumers have today, with these new possibilities of building things differently, presenting them differently, experiencing them differently — the moment you use it, you're like, "Oh my god, I can never go back to the old way."

主持人： So it sounds like you're saying retention is still the most important metric. Is that true?

Josh： It's always been this idea of — do you really use the product? If you can get me to try something… what's really cool about this era is the willingness to try is bigger than it's always been. That's part of what got me so excited to come back to the startup side and venture side. We can actually get people to try new things. But trying new things only matters if people stick and bring it into their lives in a way they're so excited to tell the next people.

Everybody asks "how do you get to this many users so fast?" The answer is: don't. Get to a smaller number who have moved their lives over to your product, who have truly found something new they will not want to go back from. Once you find that, then it becomes how do we repeat that?

Robinhood 案例：愿景与小步聚焦

主持人： There's some non-intuitive wisdom in that — like Paul Graham's whole thing about monopolizing a really small market being better than being diffuse across a big market. Andrew talks about the way to build a big network is to build a bunch of small networks. But we're so over-trained on this "go big, billion users" thing.

Josh： You have to be able to think in both at the same time. The best founders I've gotten to work with figured out how to have the vision of what is billion-user scale and why is the world different when everybody's using it — but then go, "I'm not going to get there today. Today I've just got to get to this, win this area, convince this group to become passionate."

Robinhood is an incredible example. They had a vision to democratize finance for everybody — imagine anybody in the world who didn't formally have access to the stock market, to the type of investing products other people had access to, being able to give that to everybody. But that didn't mean overnight needing every single person able to do it in their retirement account. It started very small — if you are actively thinking about wanting to invest, or are actively already trading, we should be a better product for you. Then over time, how can we earn the right to expand the platform to everybody else? But not launch with 30 products today — that would have confused everybody and landed no one.

Robinhood 的获客魔法：股票推荐机制

主持人： I'm glad you mentioned Robinhood — there are two companies we talk about as exceptions in consumer lore from a distribution perspective: Robinhood and TikTok. You've been involved in both. The way we tell the story for Robinhood is that they did do significant paid acquisition, but because of this very brilliant mechanic — give a stock, get a stock — you had a non-inflationary CAC versus being in an auction with Facebook and Google. So they were able to scale even though it was paid.

Josh： Robinhood didn't start paid. They started with a product they thought was just a better way to invest. Let's remove the cost of commission — back then it cost five bucks or more a trade with the other big platforms. They said, "we're going to figure out how to give it away for free. We know there are other ways we can monetize." They found several mechanisms through the ecosystem.

The key was it was all word of mouth at the beginning. They were on Reddit — there were Reddit communities really excited about it. Anybody just word-of-mouth on mobile saying "I kind of want to invest in the stock market — this app is great." And it had beautiful design. One thing you needed when dealing with people's money was trust. Beautiful design showed people cared and put a lot into it. That really helped Robinhood have that initial credibility.

Over time, figuring out "okay, how do we grow from here?" — they tried a lot of things. Definitely tried some paid ads. They worked. Free trading was a message that worked for people already in a trading mindset, but if you weren't, it wasn't.

But the referral program was really magic. There were a lot of referral programs at the time — Airbnb, Uber had "give 10, get 10." Those are nice because you have a little discount, but it's a dollar amount. You're like, "is it worth 10 bucks? Am I trading off my friend for 10 bucks?" Robinhood — "here's 10 bucks to invest" — what do I even invest in for 10 bucks? You couldn't even buy a share.

So the team came up with this mechanic: give and get a share — an actual share — and used the fact that the share was a lottery. Sometimes it would be a $2 stock, sometimes a $100 stock. Enough people got the $100 stock that they raved about it. People who got the $2 stock said, "oh, I have a whole share of stock — I can sell that, I have $2 in my account I didn't have before." Robinhood's ability to balance that made it a very cost-effective mechanism.

When you're doing paid ads to bring in people already susceptible to the message, then bringing in this referral program where people share and advocate it — referral is not quite as pure as word of mouth, but it's close. I'm only going to bother somebody if I at least believe in it, or the incentive is so good. It became this powerful mechanism. The brand kept building. Design got better. One day you're like, "whoa, this is no longer the little upstart financial thing — every other company is dropping commissions because they now see the threat." Schwab did it, Ameritrade did it, Schwab bought TD Ameritrade. The brokerages got transformed — all from this little plucky "let's democratize finance, but get the people we can early."

主持人： It demonstrates the power of a founder company even being post-public — Vlad is doing AI trading stuff. He's leaned the most into it.

Josh： I'm just blown away by what they've done since I've been gone. I left at the end of 2019. It's turned into this incredible multi-platform — no longer just trading. Retirement, credit card, banking components. Vlad has done an incredible job navigating the seas. They went through bumpy times too — crazy downtimes in the stock market, GameStop felt like it might be existential for the brand. But they came back to their core values: great design, great product, trust for the user, something people are proud to talk about and share. They've really been able to win people back and grow a ton.

TikTok / Musical.ly 的故事

主持人： Okay, so we've established Robinhood was a referral program. Now talk about Musical.ly / TikTok. Not everybody may know the connection. They're the other great exception — anytime we say "paid doesn't really work at scale," people say "but TikTok."

Josh： This is a crazy multi-arc story. Musical.ly was a small team in China trying to build for the Western markets. They were actually starting trying to build an education app — hadn't really worked. They had seen some fun things happening around lip-syncing, and decided they'd build a little lip-sync app.

They put it on the App Store, called it "lip sync," and were seeing spikes every week. Alex, the founder of Musical.ly (and who ran a lot of TikTok), told me they were so confused. They finally found out the spikes on Thursday night were because of when Lip Sync Battle aired on MTV. Lip Sync Battle would run, people would go type "lip sync" into the App Store, download Musical.ly, and start creating lip-syncs.

Musical.ly designed their product in two really smart ways. First, it was really easy to take your video and post it elsewhere. You could create a great lip-sync video and post it to Instagram. Second, they curated a feed of the great lip-sync videos. When you'd open the app, you'd swipe through, add some friends, and create your video. They might post it on Instagram.

That loop was: create something you couldn't create anywhere else, then post it out to Instagram and your friends. You'd start following your friends, see what they had posted alongside other really cool stuff being curated.

They found out there were a bunch of B+ stars on Instagram who weren't quite getting enough credibility — only posting pictures (Instagram video was just coming out at the time). They were like, "let me use Musical.ly to create some cool content for my Instagram feed." And on Musical.ly, they became the number one stars, the A-listers, because they were getting featured in the feed.

Now you had this loop: influencers posting tons of stuff on Instagram, people seeing Musical.ly on Instagram, having a better experience in the feed, talking about it with friends. It all started to compound. The other secret was there was always a little Musical.ly bug at the bottom of the Instagram video. You could download the video — unheard of at the time. Create the video in Musical.ly, download it, upload it to Instagram, and it would keep the Musical.ly bug. People weren't cutting that out. It created this great loop.

The company got really big — tens and tens of millions of users daily, hundreds had tried it. Then they started thinking whether to keep going independently, raise more, or sell. Got an incredible offer from ByteDance. ByteDance had a product, Douyin, that was very large in China — they had modeled some after Musical.ly, had their own invention, had a great AI team. They were like, "we can help Musical.ly scale even more worldwide." So they bought Musical.ly.

They spent about six months tinkering, rebuilding the product, moving Musical.ly and Douyin into a shared codebase. Then they relaunched it with ads, with dollars — because to get from here to where we want to go, we need to pour a lot more into it. They were probably one of the biggest spenders on Facebook, Twitter, every social media to get people to download TikTok at that time.

But the product loop was so good that everybody retained. You'd sign up, and with a couple swipes you were deeply personalized. Your friends already on Musical.ly were coming back trying new things. It was really energizing — the whole loop worked. It still took them pouring a lot of money in to get distribution, but the retention really worked.

I joke at the same time, Quibi was pouring similar amounts of money to get people to download Quibi — which didn't totally work.

主持人： Didn't work as well.

Josh： So is the lesson — if you have a highly retained working loop, you can do paid and it's okay?

Josh： I have no problem with people doing paid if they understand it. Paid to bring in people who will become very good retained users and spread the word can be a great catalyst. What you can't do is spend a lot of money to burn 10 people to find the one who actually stuck — and think that scales. That only gets more expensive.

消费者付费意愿与推理成本

主持人： Adjacent to this is a debate we've had: it's amazing that consumers are willing to pay for software in a big way. Top ChatGPT SKU is $250 a month. Top Grok SKU is $300 a month. Gemini Ultra I think is $300. Consumers are paying for software. On the other hand, because there's no zero marginal cost of distribution — inference costs money — you can't really build a mass market free product without a massive balance sheet. Is it better, worse? Is it just changing the shape of startups?

Josh： This willingness to pay and necessity to pay is definitely shifting. 15-20 years ago everything was shifting to the cloud, and the startup cost became much less. You didn't have to buy a bunch of servers and rack them. Sure, serving cost a little money in bandwidth and compute, but relatively low for what you were getting.

Now if you're running tons of inference, you need your customers willing to pay for it. They are. The ability to pay in apps is so much easier than it's ever been. Getting payments is easy if you're providing value.

I think it hurts companies that need massive network effects — because part of the benefit of massive network effects was free distribution to everybody. I haven't totally figured out what the balance is, but that's what I'm excited to work through over the next few years with various startups.

主持人： It seems like one thing that could really change that is if iPhones get really powerful GPUs.

Josh： We're going to be figuring out, with the models you can run on iPhones or laptops, how to push more of the less-complex inference onto device to lower the cost. There's no reason that everything you're ever trying to do — refining a title, rewriting a paragraph, answering basic questions — can't use models that are incredibly powerful on the device. Then you only send the more complex stuff up. From both enterprise and consumer sides, really smart founders will optimize that.

主持人： I know Karpathy has done a bunch of work, a few folks have shown you can actually train and run models on 386s and 486s. There's a lot of good old-fashioned engineering that will push more and more to the edge.

实验室会赢一切吗？

主持人： One topic we discuss a lot: are the labs just going to win it all? Should we all just go home? It feels like we have a version of this conversation every product cycle. What's your view?

Josh： A funny way to think about this is — 20 years ago, everybody was like, "won't Google just do it?" Then Google could do everything. Certainly Google has expanded in a bunch of categories. But there's a ton of startups that have grown and become massive companies since then.

15 years ago, 2010 era, everybody was like, "well, isn't Apple just going to do it all? Mobile apps will all be their own apps." We had this great world of mobile-enabled apps and social apps we could have never imagined.

About 10 years ago, I kind of started thinking that too — I wasn't sure if you could truly build another consumer startup from scratch in these networks that are now 1 billion plus users. It's part of why I actually left venture to go build again. I went to Robinhood, which led me over to Apple. I said, "gosh, I'm not sure if you can build something big enough in pure consumer areas."

I'm not sure if I was wrong. ChatGPT obviously found it. I had invested in Discord. We had just sold Musical.ly. Those were going to make it through. I joined Robinhood — that one's going to make it through. But starting from scratch, you have to get enough momentum to get to that billion-user scale. I wasn't so sure. If I didn't really believe I could find that path in venture, I was going to work on them instead. Part of why I went to Apple was to learn and work at the biggest scale and try to deliver value to everybody's pocket.

But right now, I think the world is so open again. The main chatbots, the main assistants, are going to do a lot for us — we're going to rely on them for a lot of things. But we're going to expect everything to work like them. And we're going to rewire so much else of what we do in life — our services, the way we get things done — that the opportunities to fill all those areas of our lives, help us connect, help us get information, help us explore, are not just going to go to "there's only two products we ever use."

创业公司能做但大公司做不了的事

主持人： I totally agree. There are ways startups can play that incumbents and labs can't. Companion is such a great example — it touches on everything that every committee at a big tech company doesn't want you to do. There's disagreement, probabilistic behavior, sexuality — all these ways you can interact with a product that are very human. At really big scale, it's hard to ship things like that.

Josh： Having been part of one of the biggest companies, learning to ship things that matter and make the world better is kind of the fundamental value of what we do — and the fundamental value of what you can do at that scale. But when I think about companion products and entertainment and how AI can take us on these journeys — it's incredible how much different it can be than everything we've had in the past. We've never had an experience like that.

On the other hand, it's a high level of responsibility to be very careful — with kids, with safety, with sexuality, with letting people who might be going through mental challenges feel they're getting something served through a product that isn't bringing them back to humanity or helping them get the help they need. We have to find that balance.

But once you accept that's the baseline — there is so much to explore that not every product wants to be. Lots of products don't want to be your best friend. If you've used the new Siri — it's incredible, but it's very much trying to help you get things done. It's designed to help you get things done with the experiences and content you already have, what it knows about you, what it can access in the world. It doesn't need to be sycophantic. It doesn't need to say "you look great today" and create that personality.

But there are other experiences where you might want opinions on a wardrobe and how you dress, give you tips, treat you a little bit more in that human relationship. That's a very different product.

不同 AI 产品的不同灵魂

主持人： If you unbundle the two — there's a product that's just about getting things done, almost has no soul by design. We need that. But then there are products that might mirror things we do in the rest of the world. I might join a volleyball team because I'm looking for friends, and volleyball is a level of indirection to building new adult male friendships. There will also be companion products that look like that.

Josh： I agree 100%. There are so many that can help us do more of that and explore the world, do things with other people in the background, figure out what makes sense for you. And I'd argue even the "get things done" has a soul — but a very different soul. It's not trying to replace any human relationships. None of this really is — it's all trying to augment relationships. It's not trying to make you perceive it in some sort of human way. It's trying to really be a tool. That's what we are as technologists — we're tool makers. We make things, and they can be net neutral, and it's our job to infuse humanity into them.

主持人： I love that. It does feel like this is a technology to explore our humanity — and we've never had that before. We've had a lot of technology to extend our intellect. That's let us do incredible things — SpaceX this week is the best example. But we really haven't had any technology that's helped us be a little more self-reflective. Social products are fascinating, but exploring us as groups instead of us as individuals.

Josh： That's a really profound way to think about what we can do with this new technology. So much of it is helping you be better. I've seen these great little apps helping people learn to date by practicing first-date conversations. It sounds funny — we're both older and not in that lifestyle right now — but if you're nervous about something, being able to practice is incredible. I haven't done one of these in a long time. I have to admit, I did use a little bit of going back and forth to ChatGPT to just practice talking and getting back into this mindset. That's how these things help us be better.

Gen Alpha：最强的社交一代？

主持人： It would just be so funny and ironic if the result of all this technology is that Gen Alpha — they're just the most incredible social apex predators. The art of conversation is back. It's like the 1950s.

Josh： That would be amazing. We always forget — but that's the generation they've grown up with. They've grown up fully online, fully mobile. We've seen some downsides. A lot of things are happening right now to even prevent social media for people under certain ages — mostly because we haven't all done the work to make these as safe and additive as possible. On the other hand, this generation is going to know how to adapt and get the most out of this technology, hopefully in better service of others. Hopefully look at examples of what's gone right in the world that they can live up to — and what's gone wrong that they can say it's our turn to go fix.

主持人： It's fascinating — this class graduating right now is the first class that grew up, had ChatGPT for all of college. What I think ChatGPT or broad AI lets you do is what they call "the greats" in college — studying ancient Roman, ancient Greek, everything related to it, an individualistic very abstract intellectual exploration of things just for the sake of it. That's a lot of what the technology lets you do. So I hope they are as enthusiastic about it as we are.

Josh： I really hope so. One thing that's really heartwarming for me when I talk to kids is when they tell me, "Hey, I don't use it to do my work because honestly I just know that's not what I'm going to get graded on — I might get caught or something else. But what I use it for is to help me make my work better." The fact that you can turn to ChatGPT when you've done some writing and go, "How do I improve this?" — that's such a powerful tool to have in your arsenal.

I've been using new Siri on my Mac — you write something and they go, "How's this sound? How could I spice this up? How could I get my point across better?" The tips really help you go, "okay, that's a really good tip — I'm going to put that into what I'm writing." When you use it that way, these tools are very additive as opposed to replacement.

Eugenia 的洞见：花时间 vs. 省时间

主持人： Somebody we both know, Eugenia — you might have introduced me to her, or at least whispered a good word on my behalf. Thank you for that. We're so proud to work with her. She said this thing that was so profound: look, people are not trying to save time. They're trying to spend time.

Josh： I love that.

主持人： The entire original sin mistake we're all making in assessing these companies is that the average consumer doesn't know what to do with the blinking cursor. They actually need to have things pushed to them so they can explore, and not have to be so high agency.

Josh： This idea of "time well spent" is exactly the right method of what consumers want. This is why building for consumers is so different than building for enterprises — you can't just start me with something and "I've got a problem, I'll dig into it, give me a blank cursor."

What are the ways you want to best spend time? There can always be a lowest common denominator — more scrolling, more anything, just keep going infinitely, just keep pushing this button in this game and see what happens. You can pull people and lull people into that. But the best products can be smarter and actually take us from idling away time to spending it better.

You want to say to an AI, "Hey, I'm a little bored," or "I need some ideas of what to do" — and have it spark you, give you motivation, encourage you to go do things. Whether it's get out in the world, or do something new, or think about something, or be entertained — it doesn't always have to be work or learning. It can just be fun, but still things that add value, where you reflect afterwards and you're like, "I'm really glad I did that."

创业方向请求

主持人： Maybe to bring us home — give us your kind of request for startups. What are the areas you're personally most interested in? What's underexplored?

Josh： Coming out of having worked inside Apple for a long time, and at Robinhood, and coming back to venture — I'm thinking a lot about what is this next wave of all the ways we connect as people, spend time well, whether that helps us travel better, manage our finances better, manage our health better, get things done, learn how to do our how-tos, get through that list. All these ways we can use this new technology to help us get through our lives, spend it really well, and connect with other people.

Pretty open-ended — because so much right now is built for productivity and enterprise: "I can make this team more effective, here's 20 workflows, here's 30 prompts that help everybody." I'm like, what are the things that make me so happy to go, "I'm using this every day and you should too — and I can't wait till we all are, because it makes it better for everyone."

Josh 在 a16z 的角色

主持人： Take us through what you're going to work on here at a16z.

Josh： I am so excited to be back in the startup world and get this opportunity to help Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies figure out their way through this consumer landscape — figure out how to grow, tell their stories, build their products. And then I'm really excited to help all the future Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies — let's figure out who they are, what they're doing, how I can help them, how we can help them figure out their paths. And when it's the right time, make sure they come in and we as a team really fund them.

主持人： Talk about, for a moment, what are the specific challenges you've worked through with companies? Is it about distribution? About M&A? What are some of the ways?

Josh： The stuff I love helping with most is thinking about the product and the product loop. How do you onboard people to get to that retained experience? What do you need to do to get people all the way up the ladder? How do you let them bring other people into that network? What does a core product do for them every day? And how do you evolve a single-use product into multiple uses and scale that as a product platform?

So helping people right in the heart of what that product and product strategy is — but also happy to help talk about how you organize your product team and growth team. It's completely changed in this new era. Things are more different for a product management function and an engineering function in the past six to seven months than the past 30 years of my career. It's incredible how much has changed — and navigating that, knowing what worked in the past but translating that to modern times.

Then obviously helping companies figure out their own path, whether that is M&A. There was a period earlier this year where I had a couple different friends going through M&A processes — I was talking to them multiple times a week, helping them think through the options, how to set up right for every meeting.

And lastly is how to tell your story. The one thing I've gotten this incredible masterclass on, getting to work at Apple, has been right in the heart of: how do we tell a compelling story to regular people? How do we take this world of AI and intelligence and distill it into products that people would want to use and bring into their lives every day? I'm super proud of what the team put together at this most recent WWDC and how we've told the story of Apple Intelligence and Siri AI. I can't wait to bring more of those lessons to companies as well.

结语

主持人： Incredible. How can people get a hold of you?

Josh： They'll be able to email me at jelman@a16z.com, find me through all the social medias, find me through a friend. I'm really excited to help.

主持人： I love it. Josh, welcome to the team.

Josh： Thank you. Excited to be here.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：苹果的"无魂设计"恰恰是它的护城河
Josh 在谈到新 Siri 时说了一句容易被忽略的话——"它不需要奉承你，不需要说'你今天看起来真棒'"。这看似平淡，实则是对整个 AI 消费市场的一次精准切割。
当全行业（ChatGPT、Character.ai、Replika）都在争夺"陪伴"和"情感共鸣"这块高地时，苹果反其道而行之，把 Siri 定义为**"有灵魂但不假装是人"的工具**。这是一个极其昂贵的战略选择——它主动放弃了最容易产生留存和 DAU 的情感粘性维度，转而押注"工具性人格"。
这里藏着一个跨领域的连接：这非常像**日本的"職人精神"对比美国硅谷的"产品人格化"**。日本匠人做的茶碗不会试图跟你"对话"，但它在你手中的每一次温度反馈都在和你交流。苹果在 AI 时代复活的是这种"道具的克制"——而这恰恰是大多数 AI 创业公司学不来的，因为它们必须靠拟人化来制造留存。
## 二、被掩盖的悖论：Robin Hood 的"彩票化推荐"揭示了金融产品的游戏化本质
Josh 详细拆解了 Robin Hood 推荐机制的关键——**给的不是 10

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：保险公司是"反预测"的赌徒

大多数人以为保险业的核心是"精算未来"——用概率模型预测什么时候会出事。但 Vlad 抛出了一句颠覆性的话："我们不预测未来，我们为未来的所有可能性做准备"。这不是文字游戏，而是哲学层级的差异。

预测者押注于某个单点，所以一旦错了就崩盘；准备者押注于整个分布，所以无论发生什么都能存活。这其实和达里奥的"全天候"、塔勒布的"反脆弱"是同一种生物。但更隐蔽的洞察是：当一家公司放弃预测，它反而获得了最强的进攻能力——因为它不需要等待"对"的时机，而是任何时机都能下场。Liberty Mutual 的 1200 亿不是在等风口，而是把自己变成了"风口的反向收割机"。

二、跨领域连接：保险浮存金 ≈ Y Combinator 的批次结构 ≈ 大学的终身教职

你乍看之下，保险、风投、学术毫不相干。但它们共享一个稀缺资源：结构性免于即时反馈的资本/时间。

  • 巴菲特的浮存金 = 不必每季度向 LP 汇报的钱
  • YC 的批次制度 = 让创业者在 3 个月内只对一群同伴负责，而不对市场负责
  • 终身教职 = 让学者不必每年发顶刊也能存活

Vlad 透露了一个更深的机制："透明度换取自主权——没有透明度，就没有自主权"。这条法则在三个领域里都成立。永续资本不是"自由"，它是用持续的透明度赎买来的耐心。这反过来揭示了一个让人不安的真相：许多对冲基金宣称的"长期主义"其实是赎买不起的耐心，他们没有付透明度的钱，所以也没有真正的自主权。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：可乐颂悖论——稀缺塑造灵魂，丰裕塑造想象力

Vlad 用 6 岁时在苏联排队买面包的故事，和纽约联合广场一千种可颂的对比，表达的不只是"市场经济好"。更深的洞察在于：稀缺不仅约束你能买什么，更约束你能想象什么。在苏联，"面包就是面包"——这不是物质问题，是认知问题。当一个人没见过可颂的一千种形态，他甚至无法形成"还可以再迭代"的念头。

把这个思维迁移到投资上，就出现了一个惊人的镜像：Vlad 投资框架里最强的部分（"先问要什么 exposure，再问怎么获取"），本质上是在给自己制造可颂式的丰裕。绝大多数 LP 只有一种"工具"（基金投资），就像苏联人只有一种"面包"。当工具集贫瘠，连"我应该想要什么"都被工具反向定义了。这是组织设计层面的"croissant economics"——你的工具集决定了你能想象的投资宇宙。

四、彩蛋洞察：AI 让"折现模型"失效，市场尚未消化

最被低估的一句话是 Vlad 谈估值的部分——"未来变得不可见时，所有倍数是否都应该下调？"。这不是宏观问题，是认识论问题。

历史上的估值下调要么因为利率、要么因为衰退——都是"环境变量"。但 AI 引入的是结构性的存活率不确定：你的现金牛 10 年后可能根本不存在。这意味着——这是真正的盲点——长久期信用利差应该结构性走陡，但今天的信用市场几乎没有定价这件事。Vlad 在很温和地暗示一个万亿级的错位机会：30 年期的 Salesforce 债券，定价像在 2015 年，但风险像在 2030 年。

📝 转录文本

Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors

开场：保险业的独特位置

Insurance is one of these industries that serves so many different purposes. Where we sit in the economy is quite a unique place. We're not in the business of predicting the future. We're in the business being prepared for all its eventualities.

The market's changing. The world's changing. We need to have the liquidity to always react. We need to be flexible, but at the same time have permanence in stay of some of these businesses because that's what makes you a good partner.

You will likely have trillion dollar companies in 2030 that currently don't exist. And you probably have trillion dollar companies or many hundred billion dollar companies that will not exist. We're starting to see that, right?

一、平台介绍：1200 亿美元的独特结构

Vlad, this is going to be a fascinating conversation given that you sit in one of the most interesting investment seats probably in the world. I think to give people context, the right place to start is for you just to describe the platform — how much money you manage, how it's managed, why that platform is unique and different. The seat itself is so interesting to me, and then we'll go into all the things that you've learned sitting in the seat and building the platform. But first, just ground us in: what is the thing? How big is it? How does it work?

What is the thing? The thing is the balance sheet of one of the largest insurance companies in the world — one of the most diversified insurance companies in the world, Liberty Mutual Group, which has, I would say, two primary insurance businesses that ultimately feed the investment platform.

First is the one that probably most are familiar with — the Liberty Jingle — which is our personalized business, one of the largest in the US. That, of course, is the home and auto component of our business.

And then there is the business that is much more global in nature and really serves a sophisticated set of companies, brokers, partners providing commercial and specialty insurance across many domains of business.

Those two insurance businesses ultimately feed an investment platform that takes the reserves as well as the surplus capital of Liberty Mutual Group, and invests for the benefit of our balance sheet, ultimately for the benefit of our policyholders, so that we can ensure that our promises are always met and have the financial strength behind them.

So we are about $120 billion in capital. And what's interesting about our platform is that it allows us an incredibly unique way of behaving as investors — which is to say we are focused not on any form of third-party capital, which has lots of benefits but also lots of challenges when you manage money. And it allows us to think about investing from a long-term perspective, and it allows us to do the right thing, not the expedient thing, and it allows us to maintain what I would describe as investment hygiene. That is one of the most difficult things to do when you're managing other people's money.

The other part that's really quite unique is the ecosystem that we're in, which allows us to grow our capital base in the service of our policyholders. We are not driven by shareholders whose priority is return on capital in the form of dividends and buybacks. That's not part of our structure, and it allows us to again think about making decisions that are the right decisions, not expedient decisions.

二、保险浮存金的力量：与巴菲特模式的对话

So maybe explain one level more why the insurance idea is so powerful. Very famously, Buffett built a big chunk of Berkshire's success on this idea that if you have a sort of insurance part of your business, you control this float — he would call it float — and that this magic access to capital creates this permanence that allows you to do things others can't do. Maybe build that bridge between the Buffett way of thinking and how it feels to actually operate this thing.

What really is interesting to me is how insurance is one of these industries that serves so many different purposes. The way I like to think about it: one side of our business — and one side of our balance sheet — is all about protecting and syndicating that risk, allowing risk to be taken by the people in the world.

And so we have a very appropriate way we think about that at Liberty. We say we want people and businesses to embrace today and confidently pursue tomorrow. That's what the insurance part of our business does.

When we sell those promises, when we take in those premiums, we then move them to the other side of the balance sheet and we do something else that's really interesting for the economy and society. We invest that float, as Buffett would say — but ultimately invest our policyholders' premiums in order to grow the economy, support the economy, invest in critical infrastructure, fund entrepreneurs, create jobs.

Where we sit in the economy is quite a unique place. We do things on both sides of our business that allow us to protect and create the foundation of commerce, and at the same time grow the economy. That's a unique spot.

三、1200 亿的拆解：储备金、成长信用、成长股权

So coming back to this unique combo — you're providing the value through insurance, hedging people's risk one way to think about it — that creates this pool of capital, $120 billion, that you can then (at least some portion of it, we'll get into that) invest and support the growth of the economy, creation of jobs.

Break down the 120 — how much of that is tightly controlled and has to be a certain way because it's heavily regulated. How much of that is more open? And then for the open portion, how do you think about how to allocate it? It's a lot of money. It's one of the bigger investment platforms in the US. So it's a challenge. That's a lot of money to put out. Something that's going to move the needle for you needs to be pretty big. I'm curious how you think about that.

So 120 is a snapshot. I think that number will be bigger next time we talk a couple years from now. But let's talk about the 120.

I would say you could probably think of it as roughly 70 to 75 billion of that is reserves. So that could be described as tightly managed. Again going back to this notion — of course you want to make sure that whatever happens in the investment portfolio you will always be in position to fulfill that sacred promise of writing an insurance policy to your policyholder.

But I would say even there, I think we have quite a unique approach. We are not just buying investment grade bonds, putting them in the drawer, and waiting for that coupon to come once a quarter and then ultimately maturity. That could be a sleepy, boring way — frankly the way this type of capital pool was managed historically. We're quite innovative. We do lots of different things that allow us to be a liquidity provider into that marketplace. But that's about $75 billion.

I would say you could think about the rest as broken out between what we describe as growth credit and growth equity. Those two pools of capital are growing as a function of our surplus. And to your point, they are a way we can really be a full-service kind of capital provider into all parts of the economy.

Growth Credit

What we've done with our credit business is taken an approach — not of public-private, which currently of course gets lots of headlines — but really what is our levered corporate credit business, and we've put those parts together. So our public credit, high yield, and leveraged loans business sits with our capital solutions business, sits with our direct lending business, and with our partnership structure that's focused on credit. All of them sit together, have one platform, one reporting structure, because we believe the expertise frankly is what's important.

And you'll do direct deals, you'll do manager allocations, you'll do big partnerships — whatever?

100%. The way I like to describe that: people very often start with a product — direct lending, or high yield public, or whatever. I think we asked the question: what exposure do we want in the totality of our business? When you ask that question, the next question is: assuming you could figure it out and have the ability to build that portfolio of risks, portfolio of exposures — what's the best way for me to get those risks?

And the options are many. The challenge is most organizations don't have options. You can take perhaps an organization that really has one way, which is to be an LP. So then you are going out meeting managers and ultimately allocating capital. You have people who are direct originators of that risk and pursue that — perhaps that's a GP — and many more.

Our toolkit is vast. So once we determine what exposure we want, we've got lots of different ways of getting that exposure. And this is really critical because as an investor that sits in our platform, you have the choice set that very few investors have. Do I want that in direct form? Do I want that as a co-invest? Do I want that in some kind of club format with other sophisticated investors? Do I want to be an LP, because frankly the particular risk is so difficult to access, so specialized, that I have no aspiration or ambition of trying to replicate that. I view that as an extension of my workforce, and that's the way I'm going to get that exposure.

Growth Equity

Same applies in our growth equity portfolio. Multiple businesses housed there. One is private equity, second is real estate, third is energy and infrastructure, and fourth what we describe as alternative credit. I just talked about the corporate credit business. Alternative credit tends to be all forms of asset-backed finance, where you're lending not against corporate balance sheets but against ultimately some pool of collateral.

So same concept applies — we've got many different ways we can go to market, get that exposure, and that totality of a toolkit has led to a very, very broad and interesting ecosystem in itself. We get to talk to lots of different people with very interesting approaches to the marketplace, and our job is to be competent across all those choices, because if we are, we naturally become a hub — a hub of both interesting transactions that come our way, opportunities to participate in really unique off-market things.
