item.title: [a16z] Jake Paul on Going From YouTube to Boxing to Investing | a16z ft. Anti Fund
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：Jake Paul 其实是"疼痛套利者"

大多数人把 Jake Paul 当成网红或拳手，但播客里 Jeff 无意中暴露了真正的商业模型——他在做"疼痛套利"。

物理层面，他真的被人打脸换钱；数字层面，他被全网喷了十几年。这两种疼痛在普通人眼里是成本，但在他这里被定价成了资产。他说"我从高中开始就被全校讨厌"——这不是创伤叙事，而是早期就完成了对"社会负反馈"的免疫接种。

这里有一个让人意外的跨领域连接：在金融市场，最赚钱的策略往往是"卖出波动率"——你承担别人不愿意承担的尾部风险，换取持续的溢价。Jake Paul 干的事情，本质上就是在注意力市场上做 short volatility 的交易者：他承接了别人无法消化的羞辱、嘲讽、争议，然后把这些波动率折现成 pay-per-view、Forbes 排名、growth fund 的 LP 信任。

二、Anti Fund 的真实结构：一个"注意力做市商" + 一个"资本做市商"

表面看是网红 + VC 的组合，但底层逻辑更怪。Jeff 自己点破了："在资本越来越多的世界，稀缺的是 attention 和 mindshare。" 这句话拆开看，其实在说一件 a16z 自己都在做却很少明说的事情——风险投资正在从"资本中介"变成"叙事中介"。

Andreessen Horowitz 砸钱搞媒体矩阵，是机构在向影响力靠拢；Jake Paul 拉 Jeff 做 fund，是影响力在向机构靠拢。两条曲线在 2025-2026 这个节点交汇，意味着未来十年最贵的不是 carry，是 narrative carry——谁能让 LP、创始人、政客、消费者同时相信同一个故事，谁就能在每一层都收一道费。

跨领域的类比：这非常像 17 世纪阿姆斯特丹的"双重身份商人"——既是货物商人又是消息商人，先用消息影响价格，再用资本捕获价差。Anti Fund 不是网红基金，是21 世纪版的东印度公司式信息-资本一体化结构。

三、被忽视的灰色信号：他对"streamer 文化"的愧疚

整段访谈里最反常的一刻，是 Jake 突然说："I feel bad for sort of contributing to where this has gone." 他在承认自己当年把娱乐的天花板撑得过高，逼得后来者必须做"out-of-pocket shit"才能维持注意力。

这句话被一笔带过，但它泄露了一个关于平台生态的隐藏定律：每一代头部内容创作者，都会无意识地为下一代设定"羞耻预算"。Jake 把预算撑到 X，Kai Cenat 们就必须在 X+1 的位置开始表演，否则没有流量。这其实是注意力经济版本的军备竞赛 / 红皇后假说——为了停在原地，必须越跑越快。

更有意思的连接：这和 AI 训练里的"奖励黑客 (reward hacking)" 高度同构。当奖励函数=观看时长，系统会自动演化出越来越极端的行为，直到撞上平台规则的硬墙（"can't die, can't go to jail"）。Jake 用一句懊悔，无意中描述了人类版本的 RLHF 失控曲线。

四、一个埋得很深的预测

Jeff 把 Jake 比作"已经达到逃逸速度的 startup"。如果把这个隐喻推到极致：当 AI 生成内容变成无限供给时，真实人类的"被记得"会成为奢侈品——不是被关注，是被记得。

这意味着未来 5-10 年，"个人 IP"会出现两极分化：要么进入"亿万富翁的记忆账户"（被一代人当成童年的一部分存档），要么彻底蒸发。Jake 押注政治、Jeff 押注资本+技术，本质上都是在抢占"在 AI 洪水里仍然被人类大脑分配 cache 的极少数席位"。

这不是名人战略，这是认知带宽里的房地产生意。

📝 转录文本

开场：宣布 Growth Fund

"We're officially announcing the $100 million oversubscribed growth fund and some of the tier one names, maybe all the tier one names. Anduril, Cognition, Saronic, Modal..."

Good morning, party people. What is going on?

Jake Paul is one of the biggest YouTubers and influencers on the planet. He didn't just pick a fight with one good guy — he picked a fight with an entire sport. When there's a lot of people watching you, sometimes you want to take it up to the next level.

And then boom, it became a thing to hate Jake Paul. "He is completely unhinged." Today we are talking about the blockhead. Is Jake Paul a boxer?

"When we announced Anti Fund, people were like, 'GW, you're making a mistake. Jake is not good. You're ruining your career.' And we were just like, whatever, bro. Marc and Ben and Chris were some of our very, very first LPs. When you have the living goats of the industry being like, 'Hey, you guys are good,' it's like, okay, let's do it."

Jake, given your career, you could have partnered with a lot of different people. When did you guys know you were going to be such partners?

One way I think about it: if Jake can just live his life and get a bunch of views, and someone else has to light $100 million on fire to get a bunch of views — who do I want to be partners with?

I think a lot of people in the space can have a lot of followers, but not the cash. And cash is key.

正式宣布 Growth Fund

Jake, Jeff — we've got some big news. Thanks for coming on the podcast today to discuss it. We've got a growth fund. Why don't you share the big news?

Yeah, we're officially announcing the growth fund that we've been working on for quite some time, and some of the tier one names — a lot of the tier one names, maybe all the tier one names: Anduril, Etched, Cognition, SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, Saronic, Modal. So yeah, the list goes on. A lot of collaborators in.

Jake 的"超能力"：Jeff 的视角

Jeff, for those who are not familiar — from what you can see beyond the surface, how would you describe Jake's superpowers that led him to be the amazing partner for you here?

I think Jake from the outside could be like an entertainer or professional athlete, but to me Jake's always been a constant entrepreneur. And then two, I think he's really created himself as a tastemaker and a cultural leader of his generation. All the young kids today look up and want to be an influencer — Jake literally created the influencer term and that job profile, instantiating a daily vlog.

So it's easy, especially with an internet character, to take pieces of that persona and put it as a whole human. But the underlying thread is: he's a very generous, thoughtful, kind human being who is very ambitious and is going to not stop in whatever venues he chooses to participate and compete in. Entertainment, media, sports, and hopefully with venture capital, growth equity, investing — we take that on together.

Jeff 的"超能力"：Jake 的视角

Why don't you say the same for Jeff?

Jeff is one of the smartest people that I know, just across the board. The work ethic, and his background being so different than mine — we make such a great team. Computer science, Stanford, deep deep networks — and we just think similarly on a lot of things, and I think that lends itself really well to being great partners.

He's nonstop thinking ahead and sees a lot of things before they come around the corner. He actually said no to investing in my first company when I was 18 years old, and the company failed. So he was right. Just really great judgment, great people-read. This is the people business — being able to really read people and have that EQ as well as IQ is really important.

You're just making us flatter at each other.

OG 八卦：Cory Levy 的介绍

I remember Cory Levy many years ago said, "Hey, I've got this kid you've got to meet. He's building something super interesting. He's super talented." And I was out of town. I was like, "Ah, I don't know." He ended up introducing him — we never ended up meeting. And I remember sometimes I reflect back, I'm like, "Holy shit." First off, Cory's an incredible judge of talent. But two, you've got to take bets on the up-and-coming, because you never know where they're going to end up.

Jake 的多线职业生涯

The idea that someone would become a media mogul, then a professional athlete, then a tech investor — that's a pretty distinct path. What can other people learn from how you took your path?

Always being entrepreneur-first and very analytical. When things are working — double, triple, quadruple down. When they're not — sweep them under the rug.

("That's what someone once said about a16z. I love that — they just launch a bunch of stuff, and when it doesn't work, you just pretend it never happened.")

Seriously, I've failed so many times to get to this point and made so many mistakes. I think it's resilience — in this day and age, and in having a public career, being a public figure, you have to have tons of resilience. You have to know yourself. You have to go through ups and downs.

Being an entrepreneur at heart, I always looked at everything from a business lens, always hustled, showed up every single day, knocked down every single door of opportunity, and always wanted to grow my network. Always knowing the right person at the right times — to ask them for help or a favor, and also doing favors for them when they need you, and not charging for it.

People in this entertainment world are very transactional and short-thinking and not professional. They show up late. I've always tried to be the most professional entertainer.

从内容到拳击：转折点

When I went to box, it kind of started as this crazy thing. But then it was a million pay-per-view buys, 30,000 tickets sold out. I had a blast doing it.

Was that an experiment for you, or did you know you were going to go all in?

No, I didn't know I was going to go all in. But after the first one, it was like — this was absolutely massive. I'm going to become world champion. That was a pivot moment. This content stuff is cool and doing great, but I can still do content within boxing. This boxing match was the most viewed amateur event ever — 30,000 tickets sold, press conferences getting tens of millions of views. So I was like: clearly people love this and I love it, so I'm going to go all in.

That lends itself to now being one of the highest paid athletes on the Forbes list — because of just following the numbers. Being analytical as an entertainer is also important.

进入科技：起源

So you have the content, you have this massively high-grossing boxing career. Why get into tech?

Just being an entrepreneur at heart. One of my first loves was the Valley — coming out here when I was 17 with Cory and Alexis Ohanian. They showed me all these crazy companies, I got to meet some really smart people who were building the future. Seeing Cory's AfterSchool setup, where he had all the screens and all the employees there — it was just so different and so cool.

That's when I fell in love with wanting to make my own startups and being a founder, but also being in the investor world and understanding how it works — all the VC lingo — and started growing my network. It's always been there since I was a kid.

When I was 23, I wanted to take a bigger bite at the apple. That's when I met Jeff, the stars aligned, and we just decided to go bigger and bigger and bigger. Turns out we're actually really good at it. It's fun helping shape the future of America with these companies.

首轮 LP：Marc、Ben、Chris

It's a full circle. Marc and Ben and Chris Dixon were some of our very, very first LPs. Appreciate them for helping put us into business and also giving us confidence. They've seeded almost every single emerging manager. When you have the living goats of the industry being like, "Hey, you guys are good" — it's like, okay, let's do it.

I think they were our first pitch call for the fund, and I was all nervous before, didn't even have the full pitch perfected yet. By the end of the call they were just like, "Alright, we'll invest." And we were like, "Okay great, perfect."

Antifund 的早期质疑：Jason Calacanis

Really funny — we were just spending the weekend with the All-In podcast besties and Jason Calacanis. I remember when we announced Antifund, J-Cal was like, "GW, you're making a mistake. Jake is not good. You're ruining your career." And we're just like, "Whatever, bro."

There's a full-circle moment — I joke about it now. There's no beef, it was fun to play poker with those guys. But two: J-Cal, you're a podcaster. You're trying to be what Jake invented. You are a VC-turned-influencer, and I think we will give you guys a run for your money on who can be the better investor.

(Shots fired.)

Jeff 对 Jake 的深度评价

I found that Jake was very wise for 23, 24. Very few people have the amount of inbound requests, outbound requests for their time across so many different spectrums. One way I compliment Jake: he context-switches so widely.

There's one moment where there's paparazzi, he's going to events, he's a celebrity on a red carpet, blinged out in influencer gear. Next moment he's a professional athlete training, preparing for a massive Netflix headline fight. Next moment he's negotiating business deals, inside the weeds of numbers and his metrics. Then he pivots — he's filming a TikTok.

In Silicon Valley there are a lot of smart people, and context switching is pretty wide as a founder. But to go from entertainment mode to serious quantitative analytical mode that broadly is very, very impressive.

Two: Jake has — the one word you mentioned — that resiliency. Jake has just seen a lot of shit. Coming up in Hollywood, going through the influencer phase, going through controversy, surviving it, having haters. Most people do not eat that much pain. If you think about investing or startups — it's pain tolerance.

And if you're literally fighting and punching people, or getting punched in the face for a living — and then two, the internet punching you, which is the most toxic environment — if you can survive that and thrive in that environment, that's presidential, head-of-state level courage and resiliency.

Those two things — how much can I context switch, how much pain tolerance can I have — that's what's inspiring to me. Hopefully that's what we can imbue with our startup founders, our portfolio companies, and our co-investors. That human quality is very rare.

韧性与痛苦承受力

Jake, what can people learn from you about resilience and pain tolerance?

A little bit of all of those things. I had a very intense father, so I think it started there. Being able to deal with him, growing up in that environment, the hard work — that led me to a very public career.

It all started with people actually hating on me from day one in my school. There was never a moment where me making the videos didn't have a ton of haters. My whole school in high school turned against me — talking shit, making anonymous accounts saying things about me. My principal, teachers were saying things. So it's always been there. I'm just used to it.

What else are you gonna do? Be a coward and quit and give up and let people run you? I could just never be that person. I know who I am — that's very important. A lot of people know who they are, but they let the comments and critics get to them very easily. If you can just look yourself in the mirror and be like, "I'm doing good. I'm a good person. I'm doing good things in the world. I have a good heart. I mean well." If you can mean those things, then it doesn't matter what anyone says.

关于治疗与心理工具

Are tools like therapy helpful?

Definitely. I fully believe that. You just have to be careful — you don't want the therapy to turn into victimizing yourself and always being in constant sad loops. At some points, if it feels needed, yes. But I don't think you should get trapped into the cycle of therapy and always having a call with someone that is generally probably going to be negative. It's very important who that therapist is.

It's important to learn about yourself and unpack things. I really like childhood trauma work — going back and realizing, "Oh, you had this happen to you, and this is maybe why you have anxiety in this moment." You can connect to why you behave certain ways as an adult. It's important to unpack those things.

Jeff 的职业线索：从学者到资本

Jeff, when you look back at the threads that have tied your career together, how do you make sense of it?

I've always been almost an academic or engineer or scientist at heart. What is the nature of reality? What is the truth? What are the underlying systems or rules that govern yourself, a company, a society?

Some of the things I've been interested in: biohacking — how do you optimize your singular person? That's actually how I got to know the a16z family with Ketone-IQ — Chris Dixon was our lead investor. I wanted to figure out how we could engineer better brain performance, human performance.

Then at the larger scales: how do you impact culture? How do you impact society? What is the right way to organize a company, organize a venture firm, organize a nation state? That's the largest scale of optimization.

So there's a tinkerer, or a student, or an academic that just likes figuring out the different systems.

The second part: with a little bit of battle scars and wisdom, just fewer things that compound really really well. Now I think about why venture and growth is a very interesting problem to play with: managing $1 to $100 to a million dollars to a billion dollars is kind of the same level of hard. It's hard to spend $100 really well. It's hard to spend $100 billion really well. But you only need like one or two or three people to manage a hundred bucks or a hundred billion bucks. The best investors are just really really strong-conviction folks who are making big bets.

That's where we've gravitated towards. Jake has a different lens on how he can scale his thought leadership and cultural leadership and his ability to convene and bring people together at higher and higher scales. That's a shared level of ambition — and maybe arrogance — in terms of: we think we can play at those highest levels of wielding capital, wielding technology, wielding culture.

Science, computer science, physics are foundational to the nature of reality. Capital is the lever of human society and civilization. Where Antifund has been quite unique: in a world where there's more and more capital, attention is more scarce, the ability to control mindshare is more scarce. You really can't name a better partner than Jake Paul in terms of wielding attention and driving cultural taste. If we can really master those two levers — maybe we can control the world. Or let's just try and have someone stop us.

"AI Maxing" 与 "Looks Maxing"

There was a famous tweet: "If you're smart, pivot to being hot — if you're focused on intelligence, pivot to being willing to wield attention." You had this blog post about two jobs: AI maxing and looks maxing.

I think it's actually true. If we assume that we're pretty AGI-pilled — intelligence is just going to be metered out through compute — then what matters is your relationship with people.

Looks-maxing is a pretty narrow focus on physical appearance — people like people that look good and symmetric and healthy, so fine. But it's beyond just looks. It's your vibe, your ability to be charismatic and engage with people. Can you just have better vibes than everyone else?

With venture — what you've done with the media strategy for Andreessen Horowitz — you guys are literally executing on looks-maxing the VC world. You have your own propaganda arm just pushing out your view of the world.

The second part: if you're not maxing out on vibes, then you've got to be best at wielding intelligence. Play with Codex, be on the computer all day long. And on the other side, work out, sleep a lot, and looks-max. Then you've got everything covered.

假如今天 22 岁

If you were 22, just getting out of college today — but in 2026 — what would that look like?

We were just stopping by Stanford and Jake's going to go back to college, so that might be an answer that he can actually think about.

"Yeah, I want to play on the Stanford football team actually. So we'll see what happens." — (He's going to do one year on the Stanford team and then get drafted to the Cleveland Browns. It's happening. Let's go.)

I'm actually old-school. Earlier in my career I saw the thinking that — oh, if computer science and math are automated, then you don't need to study this stuff, just do cultural or fuzzy stuff. I would just go deeper and deeper into actually understanding math, actually understanding computer science. Because if you actually know how these large language models are working, you can wield them and prompt them better.

So I'm doubling down: yes, get really good at math, get really good at computer science. The people leading these industries are math champions — Scott Wu and all these guys are hardcore at that stuff.

A version of AI maxing is basically just max your IQ and max your EQ. Looks maxing and AI maxing are the more culturally relevant terms for it.

Would you start a company? Would you join Anthropic or OpenAI?

You've got to gang up with the smartest, most ambitious people possible. That could either be your own startup, or it could be joining OpenAI. The most important thing: team up with like-minded ambitious people that will push you to be the best version of yourself. If that's a company, go — Jake and I will back you. If it's joining OpenAI, Cognition, some of these high-growth companies so you can learn a little bit before you jump into the deep end yourself — that's also great.

I'm not technical, so it would be a harder place for me. I would probably try to be in VC coming out of college. Hopefully if I made a lot of good connections at Stanford, my buddies — I would seed their genius ideas.

"Don't cut yourself short — Jake was on his MacBook... I got my GitHub's popping." (Watch his commits.)

媒体 Playbook 是否还可复制？

If you were 22 today, or 18 even — could you run the same playbook?

No, I don't think it would. There are very few people breaking through on the media side of things. It's a lot harder to grow, a lot harder to amass a following, to be a known brand, to rise to the top. You kind of had to be there from the start.

That's why with brands that have stuck around at the highest level in social media — it's me, MrBeast, and my brother. There's really no one else.

What was your insight in terms of what you were doing that was so new?

You have to be entertaining. Me and my brother were doing that — posting every single day, 15-minute videos that took the whole day to film. Effort first and foremost, then the entertainment.

Diversifying with characters. I basically created a daily reality show with all of these people on it and all these crazy things happening — that was cutting edge at the time. Always being the visionary: what's working, what trends. "Okay, let's make a rap song" — and then all of a sudden it's one of the generational anthems, younger kids know every single word of it. "Oh, music worked. Okay, let's make more songs. Let's make a whole album. Let's make a Christmas album."

Constantly iterating, but spanning the full spectrum of entertainment — songs, comedy, scary videos, the fear aspect. Then all of a sudden athleticism, boxing. The modern-day entertainers have to do it all.

与 MrBeast 的对比

We went to MrBeast's place in North Carolina, Jimmy's place. He gave us a tour, showing how analytical the whole operation is. Was yours much more artistic, creative, emergent?

Similar, but Jimmy is a numbers guy and is super analytical. Mine is definitely analytical, but not at the level Jimmy is. Mine is more creative, entertainment focused, more like a feeling thing. That's why Jimmy just maxed out the numbers on YouTube — every little thing possible to hack the algorithm.

Why do some people get big for a period but aren't durable?

I've seen the biggest stars in the space from day one just not stick around anymore. It's weird. There was a group called Magcon back in the day — they were like the Justin Biebers of the internet world. Shawn Mendes spun out of there — he obviously made it really big.

People maybe don't have that resiliency. They get tired of working or making content or going to the new platform, or they lose motivation. The ones who are truly destined to do this and love it and are talented at it — those are the ones that have lasted longer.

You also have to diversify. We've gotten into politics, business, growing brands — so you have this audience that follows you for multiple different things. Some people love my ranch YouTube videos and only know about that. But I'm also on an a16z podcast where now people in the tech world are hearing about what I'm doing — but they probably won't watch my TikToks. I'm doing all these different things and growing audiences in all these different worlds.

Also, knowing how to monetize and make money. A lot of people in the space don't know how to actually pull cash out. They could have a lot of followers but not the cash — and cash is king. That's another reason for the I-don't-even-know — 13, 14 years of being at the top of the influencer game.

Jeff: Jake 作为"已达到逃逸速度的 startup"

To me, Jake is a startup in and of himself. And in startup land, it's all power law. Jake has hit escape velocity.

Jake gets even more powerful in this new era of AI-generated characters and infinite noise. You'll have a lot of AI-generated content, and the people that you grew up watching, loving, and supporting — or hating — as a child, that generation is now going to take over from the boomers who are dying off. You compound just like OpenAI and Anthropic are compounding even faster.

It's basically celebrity survivor battle. Literally everyone has died off except for the predestined few. The predestined few literally should be the future billionaire-trillionaires, if you just extrapolate out that exponential, because there's going to be fewer real people that you care about.

Where I would juxtapose Jake and Logan with a MrBeast: I feel like MrBeast is so quantitative and analytical that it doesn't matter if Jimmy's in the video or not. Swap him out for another game-show host — maybe they're not as smooth, but I don't think Jimmy would say he's even that smooth of a natural actor or character. His analytics and his taste in selecting the content format is very, very strong.

Where Jake has durability: you follow his story. One way I think about it — if Jake can just talk and get a bunch of views, or just live his life and get a bunch of views, and someone has to light $100 million on fire to get a bunch of views — who do I want to be partners with? Who has true skill and true charisma? That is the measure: how do you take an analytical lens and a structural lens to backward-engineer what Jake has been able to accomplish.

如何选择业务机会

You said you have to figure out how to monetize. How have you figured out what's worth doing from a business perspective?

That's something we've talked about and explored. There've been opportunities to act, for example. But: What's the time expenditure? What's the return — how much money are we making here? Who are the people involved? Is it going to perform well? Do we like the script? There's the risk of something going wrong — it flops at the box office and everyone shits on you. It happens all the time.

Staying focused — having the boxing as the core of everything for many years, and content. Never getting away from your bread and butter and staying extremely focused on that, having great teams around to help.

Things now have to be like $10, $20, $30 million opportunities for me to spend time on it. It's pretty measurable. Besides the money — brand building. I've just been doing it for so long that it's instinctual. Is this a good idea, yes or no? Pretty obvious.

But there are strategy talks and high-level people that huddle around the board. Sometimes I'll film a piece of content and maybe not post it because it doesn't make sense. There are processes and teams that look at everything. It is pretty strategic, but a lot of it is also instinctual and on the fly, and it becomes more and more obvious as time goes on what the good opportunities are.

"All press is good press"?

Is all press good press?

I would say not all press is good press for certain people. For me — yes, because I can survive it. (You're antifragile.) What are they going to say?

The first reason I was hated from the very beginning in the mainstream was because I jumped on top of a news van. That's seriously why people started hating me. They're like, "Look at this douchebag." I was making fun of the news reporters' shoes. It seemed like bad press at the time, but that was my first mainstream moment in the press.

For certain people all press is good press, but I don't think it's a saying that's good for everybody.

Jeff: Everything you do has risk. Just don't take fatal risk. Don't get yourself killed, and probably don't go to jail — though people have come back from that. Don't die. As long as you take risks and calculated bets and keep compounding, you'll eventually end up somewhere good.

Most people are just too afraid to do anything that even warrants press. When you already have a platform, then maybe you can be a little bit more cautious. But most people are just totally irrelevant. You need to do something a little bit risky to even get to a place to be cancellable. Most people can't even be canceled — because you didn't exist.

So the general advice: live more and do more shit in general, because you're nothing — and you maybe can get out of nothing.

(Yap-max. Yeah, another way — yap-max is cool too.)

案例分析：Kai Cenat

Can you explain Kai Cenat — why is he so successful? What's his talent?

He's doing it on the highest level. It's entertaining. I've had a saying I think holds true in the world of entertainment: people want to see something they've never seen before. It's so new and fresh and different and kind of crazy.

A lot of the stuff he says is true. His delivery isn't always the best, he's very abrasive, he doesn't care — but he's talking about a lot of relevant things. Everything is out there about him, so you fully know him, and he's not afraid to be fully authentic and expose himself to the world. I don't think people have done that.

He's also doing things that generate bad press, but good news travels fast, bad news travels faster. It grows his brand like wildfire. It turns into a loop and he's able to convert it into views.

The streaming culture is really interesting and probably not the best for society — there's so many kids watching. I actually feel like what I was doing on YouTube — this is the final iteration of it. I feel bad for sort of contributing to where this has gone. At the time, I never thought of that — like, okay, I set the bar here as crazy, and now the streamers have to do it every day for multiple hours, and to keep people's attention they have to do out-of-pocket shit. I don't think that's a good influence. But obviously, it's entertaining.

Just to give context — he's a streamer. Some of his content is going to the club, talking to girls, humorous conversations. Some is focused on his looks-maxing journey, giving advice, doing interviews, saying out-of-pocket stuff around relationships or gender norms or how to look much better. He's invested so much there were rumors he might not be able to conceive — I think that's no longer true.

Yeah, very controversial — taking drugs on stream, doing pretty crazy things. He joined Jake's first livestream ever in person. "I thought he was nice." Definitely pretty wild on his stream though. And I'm wild as an entertainer — I can turn that on. So if I'm saying that, then it's probably pretty wild.

Streamer 文化的未来

Where do you see it all going?

We'll see new formats and people pop up that are unique and different. But the players that are in it now — and are the biggest now — will likely remain the biggest. I don't think it'll shift too much in terms of all the personalities. Things will get slightly crazier, but it's probably already near its peak — the platforms aren't allowing certain things to be streamed, and they're already getting deplatformed if they do certain things.

(Talking about: you can't die, you can't go to jail. At some point someone's going to step on that — and boom, that's the endgame.)

I recently learned that Kick is like Twitch but more edgy. Then there's Rumble — an even more edgy version of that. Different streaming platforms for different levels of edginess.

应对争议与"被取消"

When other people come to you in a massive scandal — how have you bounced back?

Faith and God. God gives his toughest battles to his most important soldiers. In all of those moments I had to learn something, go through something, and grow. On the other side of it — once I've gotten out of certain situations and learned things — it's prepared me to fly higher in life. Everything happens for a reason. You can't victimize yourself. Know that if you come out on the other side, you will be stronger, and that will lead to a bigger and better life.

Jeff: Whether it's faith in a higher power or just a conviction in yourself that what you're doing is truly worth fighting for, battling for, standing for, and dying for — choosing the right mission is so important. A lot of things people choose to do, they probably do not care for. If you hate your job but still put in 40, 50 hours a week, and you're getting attacked or critiqued — there's no conviction there.

If you just choose the right battles to fight, and you're willing to take those arrows because you believe in the mission — that almost helps you with that faith. We're invested in some of the top most high-profile AI companies — those are some of the topics where Jake can share his perspective. If people are going through really high-profile hate on social media, or litigation and controversy — Jake having lived through that at some of the highest levels, sharing how he deals with it, is super helpful for founders.

If you think you're making beneficial AGI, or you really believe fusion energy or defense tech and making Americans have the best weapon systems is really important — which we think is really important — then having that strength to fight makes it easier with that self-conviction that what you're doing is correct.

政治

Someone once asked Kai Cenat: "It seems like you're not that interested in politics. Why?" He said something like, "It doesn't help me with my looks-maxing journey."

How have you guys approached politics?

It's always been my goal and a part of who I am since I was younger — to help the people around me, to be a leader, to speak my mind. When I'm in this position of having a large audience — and as an American, we all have our right to our opinion and to voice it; that's one of the most beautiful things about this country — with a lot of people following me, I've taken it upon myself to speak my mind on what I believe in. To hopefully help others understand as well, and grow a deeper understanding of politics.

I believe it's important as an American to exercise that right. I've slowly started to talk more and more about it openly.

It comes from my dad as well. He was very political growing up. I've always been involved in it, seeing it and talking about it. It's the biggest game in life. It's interesting to be in those rooms — to talk to those people, to help them potentially win certain elections, whether that's governor or president or senator. It really truly does make an impact to the American people and society.

Jeff: That's one thing I respect about Jake. A lot of people in the public eye are too afraid to step into politics because it's not good for business — Michael Jordan, "everyone buys shoes" or whatever, that quote. But I respect that Jake — we just had conversations about this — if you actually have conviction, like God or your faith will pay full dividends for that conviction.

Life and your family and your culture, your neighborhood, your community is arguably more important than just stacking more zeros behind your account. At a certain point, you win the money-maxing game. You win the money game. Then it's: can you actually create a better livelihood for yourself, your kids, your neighbors, your community?

It takes courageous people like Jake to be like, "Hey, I'm okay to take a little bit of risk and maybe piss off a segment of people, but I truly believe in this." There's a deep conviction that he will sacrifice monetary opportunity, or reduce optionality, because there's true belief and true conviction behind his perspective. That is what leadership actually is.

There's no leaders out there. We're totally screwed. So we need people to step up and lead.

竞选公职？

Could you see yourself running someday for some sort of elected position?

Yeah, I could see myself being involved for sure. It's become more and more of a thing. Jeff was the first person actually to talk about it six years ago. Then obviously President Trump endorsed me on stage to run for president, and then backstage was like, "I think you should really do it."

I would only want to be involved in it if I was the best person to do it, and to help the country. If some other people on the other side of things that weren't good for the country felt like they had traction, I would probably do it to help the people of America — and make sure we don't get wrapped up into this whole socialist-communist thing that's going on, like in New York with Mamdani. ("Mom-dummy. Yeah, that's it.")

It will lead to America being in a very, very bad place. If something like that's going on and there's something I can do to help — yeah, of course I'm going to want to get involved. Or even maybe just help push a candidate and support them.

接下来 5–10 年要加什么？

You spent the first 15 years on media, boxing, tech. Over the next 5 to 10 years, what's something else you might add to your repertoire?

The most obvious one I'm super passionate about is politics. That seems to grow as I get older — what Jeff was saying: okay, we have the money, we have this stuff. How do I help the world and do my part? I have the courage, I believe the right mindset on it, the right way to think about it, finding truth not only within myself but as a practice out there in the world.

A lot of my takes and opinions and thoughts on it are the ones closest to truth — really finding what's best for the people, for the country, and for creating the future for my kids. If I can be helpful in that — and I know I can, and I have a very good way of thinking about things — then yeah, I would definitely see myself in the next 10 to 15 years in that arena for sure.

关心的政策议题

What are the topics you care most about?

Education, first and foremost. It's bad. It hasn't been reformed since — what, the 1920s, the 1930s? There's been no change, and the world has changed so much. Being in the investing world, you see some of these newer-age startups focused on school popping up. What's the one? Alpha School — very impressed with what they're doing.

You're almost planting seeds of a fruit tree by changing the education system, because then in 30, 40 years the country is going to have a lot smarter, better-educated people. That ripple effect impacts society in a massive way. We also don't want to fall behind other countries.

It also lends itself to finance. The number one problem with young men and young women is: how do they make money? Where do they make money? If that education is in place — and you actually learn how to do taxes in school, and not the fucking Pythagorean theorem — I think that would be a much better outcome for the people of America. It's wild to me that something like that isn't taught. I believe the economy would thrive more from education. The list goes on.

Jeff: One thing that's cool is the Invest America accounts — the Trump accounts. Jake is supporting the team there. In terms of allowing the next generation of young Americans to actually participate with the fastest-growing private companies and having them be a shareholder and a stakeholder in the success of American innovation — that's really important.

That ties into education. If people don't grow up knowing how to compound their money — I think people should know the Pythagorean theorem and the quadratic equation. ("They shouldn't. It's literally taking up space in my brain. And I don't have a lot of space.") But knowing how wealth compounds and how investing is really important. With the Trump accounts, having every little kid see their little stock portfolio and realizing, "Hey, this is how money and investing works" — super important.

18 岁是否该上大学？

Jeff, if you were 18 today, would you go to college?

Peter Thiel in some sense is kind of hypocritical when he's like, "Don't go to college" — when he had Stanford undergrad, Stanford Law School, got all the badges. But the underlying point is correct. To me, getting a badge is useful if you don't have any other badges.

Jake doesn't have to have the institutional badge of being educated because he became world-class at what he does — as a content creator, an athlete, a media personality, an entertainer. But if you're not like Jake, then you need to get some external badges. You've got to win a science fair, win a math competition. And if it happens to be that you go to Harvard, that's a really good badge — at least some external validator that you're good at something.

All of us should aspire to be best in the world at something. It could be the best violin player, the best businessperson, the best boxer, the best podcaster, the best artist, the best doodler. Everyone should have some God-given talent to potentially be the best in the world at. And if there are education badges that help validate and give you the confidence — that's very valuable.

In terms of educational content, I think that will be reformed — the education system will change. But the badging system will not change. My advice: collect some badges as a kid. Become the best YouTuber, or go to Stanford, go to Harvard — get some badges. That gives you a lot of confidence to do the next thing, like a real thing.

Jake 真的要去斯坦福？

Jake, are you seriously considering going to Stanford?

I went there today and I was like — this is beautiful. We went to the church. I don't know, I think it could be funny or interesting. I do have NFL aspirations.

(Oh, wow.)

After boxing, I want to play slot receiver. To get that experience in college first would be good, so I could walk onto the Stanford team. I'm being serious though. (Well, this might be the first podcast you mention it.)

The idea happened today. I was originally thinking I'd just go straight to the Cleveland Browns or Dallas Cowboys — but they're probably going to want to see me play first. So it might be better if I start off in college and have a little bit of an advantage over the younger, not-as-big kids before going into the NFL.

关于 Anti Fund 与"创始人品味"

Jeff, you talked about founder taste — how important and how rare it is. What does it mean? How have you guys developed it?

Through both our journeys. Some of our early conversations with Jake — he's met some of the most weird, crazy, hustler, scammy people through his career, going 16 to Hollywood and getting thrown to the wolves. Me coming up in Silicon Valley, Stanford, Y Combinator — we both got a lot of reps at meeting people, getting pitched things, doing bad deals, making mistakes.

There might be some talent or EQ or people-reading skill, but a lot of it is probably just a ton of reps. Going full circle: when Jake was 23, 24, it was like — oh man, this guy's got a lot of reps on a lot of stuff that 50-year-old men never deal with. Litigation, business deals, business issues, success. He just lives in dog years.

Laura recently joined to stand up Jake's family office and run it. The amount of stuff we've done in the last couple months is probably two years' worth of shit in two months.

There's that people-reading aspect, but it ultimately goes down to: Do they have a passion and a reason to believe why they can be world-class at what they choose to pursue? And two: Are they resilient and tough enough to eat a ton of shit to get there? Those are the two attributes — some measure of IQ, and personality read.

And track record. Leaning on other people that we trust and respect for background on certain people and their dealings. Reference checks are very important. One of the companies we invested in — a defense tech — we just hit up Palmer Luckey's team: "Yeah, what do you think of this group?" Always referencing our network for the certain categories of things we're working on — that's proven to work really well.

自孵化公司

You've made investments in iconic companies. We listed some earlier and we'll put the rest in the show notes. But you've also incubated — for example, Betr. Could you see yourself incubating more companies where you have an unfair advantage?

Definitely. I've been noodling on certain things to found currently — just seeing how the world is evolving with this current AI boom and seeing the world change. There've been ideas. We've grown some of these companies where they run themselves better — for example I spend a limited amount of time on Betr now because the team is fully built out.

There's a dynamic shift happening for us right now where there's an open slot to potentially found and start something new in this current landscape. It's definitely enticing. But obviously there's so much going on right now — you could launch a company and then OpenAI or Claude replicates it next week and you're kind of shit out of luck.

创作者阶层的"金融化"

Anecdotally, I've seen the creator class get a lot smarter and more sophisticated about monetizing — yourself getting into venture, getting into private equity. Even Jimmy: originally making chocolate, now he's just bought a bank. He's like, "Hey, I can make a dollar off a customer — maybe I can make hundreds of dollars off a customer."

You guys getting into actually building software product and companies — people are getting more sophisticated about where the value is. I'm excited to see that trend continue.

Thank you. As we become more sophisticated, yeah, it's cool to sell — I won't say which companies — but we want to play a bigger game. And shift humanity while doing that.

收尾

Yeah, I think it's a good place to wrap. Jake, Jeff — you guys are doing something really exciting. We're excited to be co-investors in a number of companies together, and more to come. Thanks so much for coming on the podcast.

Thank you, man.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Jake Paul 其实是"疼痛套利者"
大多数人把 Jake Paul 当成网红或拳手，但播客里 Jeff 无意中暴露了真正的商业模型——**他在做"疼痛套利"**。
物理层面，他真的被人打脸换钱；数字层面，他被全网喷了十几年。这两种疼痛在普通人眼里是成本，但在他这里被定价成了资产。他说"我从高中开始就被全校讨厌"——这不是创伤叙事，而是**早期就完成了对"社会负反馈"的免疫接种**。
这里有一个让人意外的跨领域连接：在金融市场，最赚钱的策略往往是"卖出波动率"——你承担别人不愿意承担的尾部风险，换取持续的溢价。Jake Paul 干的事情，本质上就是**在注意力市场上做 short volatility 的交易者**：他承接了别人无法消化的羞辱、嘲讽、争议，然后把这些波动率折现成 pay-per-view、Forbes 排名、growth fund 的 LP 信任。
## 二、Anti Fund 的真实结构：一个"注意力做市商" + 一个"资本做市商"
表面看是网红 + VC 的组合，但底层逻辑更怪。Jeff 自己点破了："在资本越来越多的世界，

item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：美光"业绩越好，估值越跌"的悖论

视频中藏着一个被多数散户忽略的精妙现象——美光过去8个季度有6次"财报超预期却股价下跌"。表面看这是市场情绪反复，但小陶其实点破了一个更深的机制：当EPS上修速度 > 价格上涨速度时，PE反而被"业绩压扁"。

这是估值学里一个反直觉的"压缩陷阱"： - 你以为利好出尽是情绪问题，其实是数学问题 - forward PE从14倍→12倍→11倍的阶梯式下移，不是熊市信号，而是基本面跑赢市值扩张的隐性证据 - 真正危险的不是PE下跌，而是PE在业绩平庸时还能维持——那才是泡沫

跨领域连接：这与生物学中的"异速生长（allometric scaling）"惊人相似——身体某些器官长得比整体快，比例反而下降。镁光的盈利就是那个"长得太快的器官"，让估值这个"身体比例"被迫缩小。健康的成长，往往伴随着比例的失衡。

二、被忽略的政治经济学博弈：川普为什么可能"主动后退"？

视频里最微妙的一句话是——"接下去要往后退一步的，可能不是凯文沃什，而是川普"。

这个判断颠覆了主流叙事（市场普遍认为美联储会向白宫低头）。小陶的逻辑链很狡猾： - 川普要的不是"降息"，而是房贷利率下降 - 而短端加息→曲线倒挂→长端收益率反而下行 → 房贷利率（挂钩10Y）反而可能降 - 所以川普可以"嘴上反对加息、心里默许加息"

这是一个典型的"目标与手段的错位"案例——和企业管理中CEO要"提升股价"却用回购损害长期R&D是同一个结构性陷阱：当人们把代理指标（降息/股价）当成终极目标，决策就会被路径绑架。川普的高明之处在于他可能没被绑架，凯文沃什的高明之处在于他赌川普不会被绑架。

三、日元Carry Trade的真正引爆点：不是利率，是汇率的"心理价位"

最容易被误读的是日元加息对美股的传导。小陶提出一个反共识观点：加息25bp对carry trade的融资成本影响微乎其微，真正的引爆点是汇率心理位。

24年8月那一波雪崩，不是加息本身，而是美元/日元从160→140的速度——是斜率，不是水平。

这里有一个深刻的跨领域映射：这和地震学中"应力释放"是同构的——日本央行长期QE+小幅加息，相当于在断层上不断累积应力却不释放。一次性的15bp加息是无害的，但当汇率突然从"被压抑的均衡"跳到"自由浮动"时，所有沉睡的carry trade会同时平仓——这就是2024年8月的"金融地震"。

真正值得监控的不是日央行的利率决议，而是152-160这个汇率走廊何时被打破——就像地震学家不看每天的微震，只看断层的临界应力。这是小陶在视频里没说透但暗示的关键信号。

额外的元洞察：整期视频的结构本身就在传递一个隐藏方法论——当所有人讨论"鹰派还是鸽派"时，去看债券市场的报价；当所有人讨论"加不加息"时，去看汇率的水平；当所有人讨论"财报好不好"时，去看PE的方向。这是一种"绕过叙事看价格"的认知纪律——和侦探学里"不听嫌疑人说什么，看他的银行流水"是同一种思维模式。

📝 转录文本

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小陶，欢迎大家来到周日的更新。

那今天正好借这个机会和大家回顾一下过去这一段时间市场的走势，包括我们看一下当下市场的一个情绪面参与度。有人问我怎么看待日本央行那边加息的这个决定，对于美股这边有没有些影响，carry trade这边有没有一些影响。那正好结合这个担心，我们和上一周的美联储会议放在一起，去讨论一下，接下去美联储如果开启加息，对于整个市场的一个影响，好吗？最后我们去聊一下本周市场的一个焦点——美光的财报。那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。

在开始之前，大家记得留言、点赞加转发，让更多的人可以看到我们这期内容。

一、当下市场情绪面回顾

先来回顾一下当下市场的情绪面到底在什么样的一个位置，那有几个角度出发。

CNN恐慌贪婪指数：截止到上周五的收候盘百分之差不多37吧。100的话就是极度贪婪，然后0的话就极度恐慌。基本上我们从来都没有看到过0或者100，最低的时候我们可能看到个2吧，我记得我没记错的话，是2或者是3。那现在是在一个恐慌的一个阶段。那这个CNN的恐慌贪婪指数，由七八个指标组成在一起，所以还是一个相对比较全面的指标。

市场参与度： - 标普500：20均线、50均线分别在50%和55% - 罗素2000：62%和61% - 纳指：46%和46%

所以纳指是在中性偏低的，标普是正好在中性的位置，而罗素2000是在一个中性偏乐观的一个位置。所以三大指数如果全部结合在一起的话，当下的一个市场就处在一个比较中性的一个位置，没有乐观，也没有悲观。如果把CNN的恐慌贪婪指数也结合在一起的话，相对处在一个中性偏悲观的位置。

所以这时候对于自己的持仓来说，我个人就是继续持有，5%多一些的一些现金不会做任何的一些操作。如果个股方面有一些买入机会的话呢，仍然可以买入。但从整个盘面、从整个指数带给我们的一个反馈，就是在当下的一个情绪面参与度的情况下，中性偏悲观的一个位置，我们适合继续持有。

二、第二季度财报回顾

第二季度的财报几乎马上就要结束了，那我们一起回顾一下。第二季度发布财报完之后正好上半年也快结束了嘛，然后7月份我会做一期直播，然后和大家分享一下，对于上半年做一个总结吧，然后下半年我们做一个展望。

上半年到现在为止，我个人其实是非常的满意的，当然主要的一个涨幅还是来自于英特尔。那我看一下现在，盘前英特尔也继续在出现上涨，那可能是因为陈立武的这个采访市场做出了各式各样的一些解读等等。那么陈立武的采访利好哪些板块等等，那从我个人的角度，最直接的直接去投陈立武就可以了嘛，对吧，就直接去投英特尔就可以。

我们从FactSet数据去出发，回顾一下第二季度的一个财报。

通常来说，我们知道在进入一个财报，就在财报的一个发布财报的一个过程之中，通常市场会对于EPS一个增速往下调，平均下调这个幅度是2%至2.7%，取决于我们看什么那个区间。但是从3月31日一直到截止到上周五为止，差不多3个月左右的时间，就是发布第一季度财报的那段时间以来，标普把EPS上调了2.9%，这其实是非常非常罕见的一个数据。然后第二季度EPS的一个同比增速也从3月底的18.7上升到了22%。

我们来看一下利润率，net profit margin市场对于第二季度的预期是14.1%，稍微低于第一季度的14.8。什么概念？过去5年的平均profit margin是12.3%，去年的同期是12.9%。说明什么问题？说明这一个季度——包括第一季度，包括第二季度——市场的预期企业的一个利润率要远远高于5年的一个平均。

我们也看到了非常罕见的，在一个季度之内，市场连续上调EPS这个增速。这其实也解释了为什么第二季度整体的一个走势、标普500整体的一个走势相当的强。当然，如果我们从涨幅的这一个宽窄度去看的话，整个这一次涨幅相对会比较窄一些，会非常的集中一些。但如果从板块这个角度去看，有8个板块我们期待第二季度的一个利润率将会都高于5年的一个平均，所以从涨幅来看确实比较的窄，但我从net profit margin从利润率这个角度去看，还是有很多个板块都远远高于5年的平均数。

所以整个标普500，其实今年市场对于EPS这个增速到现在为止其实比较的乐观。年初的时候其实也乐观，大家不知道还记不记得我在25年底做26年展望的时候，我说过15% EPS这个增速，还记不记得这个数据？反正15% EPS这个增速我说，因为PE在高位，如果今年标普500EPS的增速可以增长15%，在PE不变的情况下呢，指数也增长15%，我完全可以接受。那么这是我进入26年之前的这个setup。

所以26年之前是进行了降贝塔，然后在3月份、2月份的时候慢慢的把贝塔给加回去。那经过现在差不多6个月，那么6个月之后，我们来看一下EPS增速2026年，从年初的14%至15%，现在已经来到了23.3%。说明过去6个月，市场对于今年EPS增速上调幅度相当的大。这也解释了为什么标普500 YTD到现在差不多10%吧，所以这10%的一个上涨、价格的这个上涨我认为非常的合理，然后主要的一个驱动因素是什么？EPS增速基本面。

三、美联储与债券市场：鹰派与否的真正判读

上一周我花了两期视频和大家——包括我在X上也和大家——讲解了一下，为什么上一次美联储会议是属于鹰派的。但很多人不是非常的理解，他说股市这边的一个解读并不是非常的鹰派啊。我不会去过多去讲解了，我觉得没有这个必要。

那我们在理解、试图去理解美联储，无论是美联储本身还是美联储主席，是属于鹰派还是鸽派，更多衡量的一个指标不是股市这边，而是债市这边。而债市这边最简单的我们去衡量一下，市场对于接下去加息的这个预期，在讲话之前和讲话之后有没有出现上升，这是最直接的一个理解。债券市场这个理解，股市短期之内和债券市场这边不一定要走的一致，好吗？

我就讲到这边，我不希望和大家去争论到底这一次讲话是鹰派还是鸽派，说实话没有太多的一些意义，因为很多东西是比较主观的。但是我们就直接去从结果去看，我们直接从债券市场交易出来这个结果去看。

截止到我现在看到，因为美债这边也已经开始交易了嘛，7月29日会有下一次的美联储会议，然后9月16日会有再下一次的美联储会议。现在市场预期几乎100%的可能性，9月份将会进行第一次的加息。2026年底就是今年年底了，四次美联储会议之后，将会有加息的可能性是在1.73，也就是将近加1.5次这两次之间。如果我们再往远期去看的话，最多加息的一个幅度是在2.14，也就是市场预期，这一次就根据现在的一些数据、通胀数据，包括所有的修复数据全部结合在一起，美联储会在现在的这个位置加息两次之后就会停下来，然后保持在那个位置一段时间。

当然啊，如果接下去数据进来之后，我相信现在市场对于加息这个预期还会改变的呢。如果数据不是非常的好，甚至这个加息的预期还继续往上抬。如果CPI的数据又非常的好或者通胀的数据又非常的好，那加息的这个预期往下降也不会在意料之外。

利率曲线的变化

我们来看一下整条利率曲线。利率曲线其实告诉我们很多的一些东西。图里两条线，黄色这条线是年初的利率曲线，绿色这条线是截止到上周五收盘的利率曲线。

那我们看到这两条利率曲线最大的一个差别是： - 首先，这边三个月这边的利率是几乎没有变的，因为在过去的6个月的时间里面，美联储没有加息，也没有降息 - 然后整条利率曲线从YTD到现在为止是往上平移了一段时间 - 变化最大的一个点在哪里？是在4个月到3年期

而整条利率曲线的一个形状，我们看一下黄色这条线是：平的、平的，然后突然一个下降，平的，然后再出现反弹。说明什么问题？说明在年初的时候，市场其实预期今年美联储是会降息的，那所以一年之后的这个利率、一年期的这个利率比隔夜利率要低，也就是市场当时定价是会降息。

然后现在我们看绿色这条线完全不一样了。那么这条绿色这条线一年期和两年期的这个利率，一年期的4%，两年期的4.2%以上，那就说明，市场预计在一年之后，隔夜利率将会比现在的3.6要高，将会加息两次不到。所以YTD到现在为止，我这边箭头所指的这个位置，两年期的美债收益率直接上升了70个基点。

那在债券市场里边，有一个市场的一个共识，可能是两年期的美债利率是对于未来美联储隔夜利率或者货币政策的一个指引。根据以前的美联储或者鲍威尔的美联储，如果市场预期是错的，那美联储会试图把市场往自己身边拉一拉。但是，这一次凯文沃什这个美联储似乎不会去对于市场的这个预期去进行调整。凯文沃什这个美联储认为市场的这个预期、市场对于这些数据的一个反馈是正确的。

政治博弈：川普与凯文沃什

所以接下去吧，在凯文沃什面前，非常重要的一个问题就是9月份到底加不加息。11月份要中期选举，川普选他上来是进行降息的，不是让他来加息的。所以接下去对于凯文沃什是一个非常大的一个考验。

当然有一个问题我们需要思考的是，如果说因为加息而导致10年期美债收益率下降的话，这一点川普是希望看得到的。因为虽然川普让凯文沃什去降息，但是川普的一个目的不是为了降息而降息，他的目的是为了去解决房地产市场——通过让房地产市场的这个利率、通过房贷利率下降来拉拢民心、来拉动选票。

现在凯文沃什这个态度似乎和川普的这个想法出现了一定程度的一个背离。那接下去如果有人要往后退一步的话，谁可能退？我认为不会是凯文沃什，而是川普可能要往后退一步。什么叫川普往后退一步？就可能也许会允许凯文沃什进行加息在9月份。

至于加息对于市场的影响，我在上周我已经说过了。那从我个人对于整个美股的一个判断结构来看，最重要的还是基本面，那然后才是宏观这一块的一些影响。这样基本面没有太大一些问题，因为这一次就像前面市场预期的一样，这一次最多加息两次，然后接下去就会停了。加息两次和22年还是不一样的，但是加息本身短期之内，如果市场情绪面非常的高、参与度非常的高，那就会使得市场出现下跌，不然的话市场是可以去吸收接下去两次的一个加息。

四、日本央行加息与Carry Trade

那另外一个风险就是日本央行那边。这条图——这张图是什么？这张图是如果我把美联储这边的隔夜利率减去日本央行那边的隔夜利率的一个利差。如果两国之间有利差的话，就代表日本投资者那边可能会出现一个carry trade。而这一个carry trade本身其实只要有利差在那边，都会鼓励carry trade，都会鼓励套息交易。

那有人问我，日本央行的加息怎么去看，特别日本央行加息对于美股这边的一个影响。对于美股这边的影响，不是因为日本央行的加息怎么样，而更大的一个波动是加息本身因为这背后的一个套息交易本身的一个机制。的确，你加息是增加了这个成本、增加了融资的这个成本没有错。但是最大的一个问题，对于日本这边投资者来说，最大的问题不是日本央行的加息——加息本身你每一次加0.25个基点，不会对融资成本不会有太大影响。对于他们来说，最大的一个影响是汇率影响，是日元和美元之间的一个汇率影响。

所以只要日元和美元之间的一个汇率，日元持续保持贬值，或者持续在一个相对非常低的——对于日元来说相对一个非常非常低的——一个位置，那就OK了，就对于这些投资者来说，影响不会太大。并不是因为你加息就会造成非常大的一些影响，不会对carry trade造成非常大的一些影响，25个基点这个融资额外的一个融资成本影响不大啊。

24年7月份、8月份的时候，我们看到这一波当黄色这条线下跌的时候，就代表日元在升值、美元在贬值，就日元对美元在升值。当日元兑美元在升值的时候，就出现了白色这条线下降。那当时是从160直接涨到了140，那当时是出现了一个一波日元的carry trade的一个平仓。

但是现在，就我们要区分开来，只要我们看到过去起码2026年，你看到日元和美元之间的一个汇率基本上保持在152至160之间波动并不大，那么所以对于日元carry trade这些人来说影响不大。

那现在在接下去我的一个期待是有可能日本央行再次会出来去干预汇市。如果干预汇市的话，那可能使得现在161.46的这个价格会出现一波程度的一个上涨。出现上涨的话呢，日元这条线就会往下跌，但是跌到个第5期跌到一个又一波干预市场。你看到每一波的一个白色这条线的一个下跌，都是日本央行的干预。干预完之后继续往上涨，干预完之后这条线继续往上涨，就代表日元继续再贬值。

为什么日元在贬值？日本央行明明在加息，为什么日元在贬值？因为日本央行在QE，就他们在QE的一个同时在进行加息。所以一方面他们在进行举债、大规模疯狂的这个举债，一方面他们在加息，那加息本身其实是增加了他们的一个举债成本。所以对于日本整体、对于日本政府来说，其实这一个压力是很大的，因为你在举更多的这个债务，同时你央行那边在加息，等于是增加了你举债的这个成本，对吗？

所以但是无所谓嘛，从投资者这个角度去看，因为他们这个QE导致日元持续贬值、导致日元持续低迷，所以QE本身对于日元的这个影响大于加息本身对于日元的这个影响。只要日元还是在一个相对贬值的这一个趋势上去看的话，那对于市场来说就不会有太大的影响。

五、美光（Micron）财报展望

OK，最后我们来看一下美光的财报。

美光现在的PE其实并不低的——52倍，然后你去看一下，向前看的这个PE是在11倍，然后如果我们看28年的这个PE的话，就是9.35倍。那EPS这个增速非常的快： - 25年是同比538% - 26年现在市场的预期同比是650% - 27年的话会低一些，88% - 28年是4%

其实美光现在市场对于美光的这个预期有点类似于是在23年的时候、23年24年的时候，市场对它的这个预期，就是它没有办法预测到一年之后EPS这个增速或者整个基本面。

"Rerate"的迷思

那我有看到社交媒体一部分呢说，市场现在开始对于美光进行rerate，就是什么叫rerate？就是把它的一个PE开始往上买，他们认为美光远期的这个PE可能可以达到20倍或者说25倍。但其实如果我们去看历史上美光的这一波走势的话，你但凡了解25年美光那个forward PE在什么样的一个位置，就不会得出这样的一个结论。

为什么？25年的时候，美光forward PE就已经在13倍14倍，就比现在还要高。然后我这边圈出了两个时间点，一个是在12月份，一个是在今年的3月份。基本上白色这条——就图里两条线，白色这条线是PE，那截止到周五的收盘是10.78，forward PE截止到周五的收盘向前看市盈率是10.7倍，或者你理解为11倍吧。蓝色这条线是价格。

这边两个区间——去年的12月份和今年的3月份，你可以看到白色这条线下跌的幅度非常的大。两次下跌的幅度都非常的大，中间也伴随着价格小幅的这个下跌。那今年的3月份，价格下跌的幅度稍微大一些。那为什么白色这条线下跌的幅度那么的大？就是因为财报季度。

"业绩压扁估值"的机制

每一次发布完财报之后，我们都会看到美光向前看的forward PE往下跌。为什么？因为管理层大幅上调EPS这个增速，然后又配合着价格的这个下跌——价格在往下跌，然后EPS增速再往上抬，就会使得PE出现非常大幅度的下跌。

经典的例子就是今年3月份的一个走势：价格没有跌多少，但是EPS增速——整个标普EPS增速一直在往上调，所以它那个PE跌幅非常的快。无论是QQQ也好，整个芯片板块也好，包括大科技也好，那么PE跌的非常的快，价格跌的不多，但是主要的原因是因为EPS的增速一直在往上调。

那么所以这一次我的一个期待是发布完财报之后，白色这条线、美光的一个forward PE会继续往下跌，因为这一次他们的一个财报仍然会非常的好。财报非常的好，EPS增速往上调，那PE自然就会跌下来。

所以市场对于美光并没有进行一个rerate。甚至于你可以看到，那么在去年11月份的时候，美光forward PE是去到14倍以上了，然后接下去往下跌，12月份发布完财报之后出现了一波下跌，然后继续反弹——PE反弹，价格也反弹。就是每一次财报之前，其实价格都会出现反弹，到财报的时候PE一波下跌。

白色这条线在去年的12月发布完财报之后，然后1月份、2月份出现上涨，2月份、3月份出现下跌，发布完财报之后又一波下跌，然后从4月份到6月份一直出现上涨，从最低的5倍的PE啊、4倍多点的PE，然后现在来到了11倍的这个PE。发布完财报之后继续出现下跌，但是每一波的一个点位你看：去年的11月份是14倍，那今年的2月份是12倍，现在是11倍，马上又要发布财报，发布完财报之后白色这条线几乎一定是向下的。

向下不代表它不好，向下恰恰代表它的一个基本面非常的好——EPS增速往上抬，所以估值才会向下。那么但是每一波发布财报之前的这个PE其实都比上一波要稍微低一些。

历史财报表现

这边我让彭博做了一下总结过去8个季度的这个财报，可以看一下和市场EPS增速的这个预期去做对比： - 每一次都比市场预期要高 - 上一次的话比市场预期高出了34% - 和guidance去做对比的话，也每一次对于guidance指引这边都进行了上调 - 上一次对于指引这一块的一个上调是45%

但是不代表你价格都要上涨。美光是一个非常好的例子，就是每一次财报之前价格都走的非常的好，然后出来财报之后出现了一小波的这个回调，对吧？你可以看到，价格过去8个季度的财报，价格只有两个季度的财报里面发布完财报之后价格是出现了上涨，其他6个季度的财报价格都出现了下跌。

所以美光的财报过去8个季度的财报是一个非常好的例子，就是你每一次出来之后，营收和盈利都超出市场预期，且超出市场预期的幅度非常的大，指引也进行上调，上调的幅度也非常的大，不代表你价格一定要上涨。

结尾：富途、Moomoo活动

最后是我们今天的老朋友富途、Moomoo。

美国市场这边通过入金可以获得价值30美金SPACE X的一个正股，闲置资金可以获得最高年化8.1%的这个回报，然后通过不同的入金金额，最高可以获得价值1000美金英伟达的正股。这一次的一个活动截止日期是6月底。

加拿大这边这一次由Moomoo联合纳斯达克推出的一个Top Trader的挑战赛已经开放报名，其实在6月初的时候就已经开放报名，百万的一个奖金值，冠军可以直接拿走10万加币，前300名保底500加币。报名的链接我会放在置顶的留言里面。

当然如果开户完不报名的话，也没有关系，通过入金仍然可以最高获得价值4600加币的奖励。加拿大的观众如果对于自己的交易非常有信心的话，不要错过这一次机会。

那所有的链接全部在置顶的留言里面可以找到。那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：美光"业绩越好，估值越跌"的悖论
视频中藏着一个被多数散户忽略的精妙现象——**美光过去8个季度有6次"财报超预期却股价下跌"**。表面看这是市场情绪反复，但小陶其实点破了一个更深的机制：**当EPS上修速度 > 价格上涨速度时，PE反而被"业绩压扁"**。
这是估值学里一个反直觉的"压缩陷阱"： - 你以为利好出尽是情绪问题，其实是**数学问题** - forward PE从14倍→12倍→11倍的阶梯式下移，不是熊市信号，而是**基本面跑赢市值扩张**的隐性证据 - 真正危险的不是PE下跌，而是PE在业绩平庸时还能维持——那才是泡沫
**跨领域连接**：这与生物学中的"异速生长（allometric scaling）"惊人相似——身体某些器官长得比整体快，比例反而下降。镁光的盈利就是那个"长得太快的器官"，让估值这个"身体比例"被迫缩小。健康的成长，往往伴随着比例的失衡。
## 二、被忽略的政治经济学博弈：川普为什么可能"主动后退"？
视频里最微妙的一句话是——**"接下去要往后退一步的，可能不是凯文沃什，而是川普"**。
这个判断颠覆了主流叙

item.title: [Latent Space] ⚡️Every product of the future will be a living system — Ronak Malde, Trajectory.ai
item.dc:creator: Latent Space
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：他们卖的不是模型，而是"产品的新陈代谢"

大多数人把 Trajectory 理解为"帮企业做 RL 微调的平台"，但仔细听 Ronak 的叙述会发现一个隐藏的范式转移——他真正在做的，是把"软件"这个静态名词，变成"代谢"这个动词。

注意他那句话："你昨天用的模型，明天会犯同样的错误。"这其实是在指控整个软件工业一百年来的根本缺陷：所有产品都是"标本"，不是"生命体"。Word 文档不会因为你用得多就变得更懂你，Excel 不会因为千万会计师纠正过它就学会财务直觉。所有 SaaS 都是"冻干食品"。

Trajectory 真正的产品是一种新的物质形态——会自我修复、自我增殖的软件代谢系统。这就解释了为什么他放弃数百万美元收购金：他不是在创业，他是在押注一个尚未被命名的新工业革命的基本单元。

二、跨领域连接：从操作系统到生物学，他们正在重新发明"细胞"

Ronak 自己也提到了一个很有趣的关联——他们的 Continuous LoRA 看起来像"操作系统调度问题"（饥饿、抢占、并发池）。但我想推得更远一点：

真正的类比不是操作系统，而是细胞生物学。

  • 传统训练 = 蛋白质合成（一次性、目的明确、合成完即结束）
  • Continual Learning = 细胞代谢（持续、流动、与环境耦合）
  • SDPO 的"特权提示" = 表观遗传调控（不改变 DNA，但用上下文改变表达）
  • 他们的 trajectory 数据格式 = 细胞膜（决定什么信号能进入"基因表达"过程）

最惊人的隐藏含义：当软件开始持续学习，软件工程将不再是"建筑学"，而会变成"养殖学"。未来的 PM 更像园丁或牧场主，而不是建筑师。这也解释了为什么他强调"PM 应该能控制模型在哪些区域变好"——这是驯化的语言，不是编程的语言。

三、一个被埋藏的、可能比 SDPO 还重要的洞察：噪声的反转

他随口说了一句让我停下来反复回想的话："点赞点踩信号其实很嘈杂，因为大家在 vibe accepting 一切。但用户修改 agent 输出的方向反而是干净信号。"

这是对整个 RLHF 范式的悄悄颠覆。

过去十年的 AI 对齐建立在一个假设上：显式反馈 > 隐式反馈。但 Ronak 在这里指出：在 agent 时代，显式反馈反而崩溃了，因为人类太懒/太被讨好，倾向于点赞。真正的金矿藏在人类不得不动手纠正的"沉默不满"里。

跨领域映射这个洞察会很可怕： - 心理学：人嘴上说喜欢的（显式偏好），和身体诚实选择的（修正行为），后者更准——这正是行为经济学的核心。 - 教育学：学生说"懂了"的时刻往往最浅，真正的学习发生在他们改作业的笔触里。 - 产品设计：未来的产品分析将从"点击流"转向"纠错流"——用户每一次"撤销"都是一次最高密度的教学。

Trajectory 真正在挖的金矿，是人类的"懊悔信号"。这是过去所有数据公司都浪费掉的最贵的数据。如果这个判断成立，那么未来五年最值钱的 dataset 不是 RLHF，而是 "Undo-Redo-Modify" 的微观行为流。

额外彩蛋：Demis 亲自出现在收购电话里，加上 Sergey 在 Antigravity 视频里当主角——这件事被一带而过，但传达了一个产业级信号：Google 已经悄悄承认，它最大的劣势不是模型质量，而是"不知道真实用户在做什么"。换句话说，DeepMind 用 24 亿美金买的不是 IDE，是人类工作行为的实时观测站。这件事会重塑 2026 年之后整个 AI 大厂的并购逻辑。

Continual Learning 的产品视角：以 Harvey 为例

Swyx：So okay, you decide to work on this problem. And I guess what's the approach? Like, you know, I think continual learning is a hot topic among a lot of folks, but I think people approach it from different angles — from like the "train it into the weights" versus "just have a bunch of skills and markdown files" or whatever. We saw LoRA recently. Just tell people more about maybe the schools of thought in continual learning and the one that you're choosing.

Ronak：Totally. So I think it's helpful to actually — we've been talking about just the research and stuff that enables this. I think it's useful to think about this from the customer's perspective.

So take a company like Harvey for example. They have a ton of complex legal workflows where agents are trying to research documents and then redline a contract. And unlike coding where, obviously, a technical audience can just guide the agent in the right direction — we're able to be tolerant to mistakes — for a field like legal, like getting 80% of the way there is the same thing as zero.

And this is for a lot of fields where they have very expert data of what people are doing. Maybe an agent attempts something and then the user needs to go and correct it and get the rest of the 20% of the way there. That's that useful signal that we're able to learn off of.

Trajectory 平台总览

So actually — we, I can share my screen and show some of the work we've been doing with Harvey. But I guess before I get there, the high level of what we've built for all of these companies is a platform for continual learning.

We essentially take all of the data, all of the expert traces, the agents, and distill it into one format which is what we call the trajectory. And it's basically all of the data that is needed to then create the evals, the judges, the environments — everything, all the components that you would need for training. And then we're able to create that end-to-end loop of a self-serve platform to optimize the agent, the model, the harness, everything. Right now, we're starting with the model. And that's what the major kind of alpha is for every company: being able to own models that are faster, cheaper, better at the frontier.

Harvey × Nvidia × Nemotron 3 Super 案例

And so I can share some of what we've done with Harvey, which is really exciting.

One of the main things that Harvey really cares about is, obviously, they're a regulated industry, they want to have sovereign intelligence as well. And so we partnered with Harvey and Nvidia in order to train Nemotron 3 Super in order to get to the Pareto frontier — a lot of the legal workflows that they care about.

I can actually fast-forward over here where we were actually able to capture, with along with Harvey's lab dataset, a lot of their expert legal workflows. And so the things that they care about, for example, are: issue spotting, analysis explanation, citation reference, completeness coverage, etc. And all of these things — they have sort of paralegals and lawyers going in-house and working on mission-critical tasks.

And so with our model, we were actually able to improve these sort of metrics across the board. And then also, the really cool part is: Nemotron is a drastically cheaper and faster model than the frontier. And this matters at scale when you're deploying to not just a few users — hundreds and thousands, hundreds of thousands of users across the board.

And so this is something that's very exciting for the legal domain and something that we're able to do on our platform.

平台的可扩展性

The other exciting part is, because we're building this — I think essentially like every company has their data sources in different formats — we're able to build this as a scalable platform that gets all of that data without any kind of forward-deployed work, and really improve the model.

And the cool kind of proof point is: we started out working with a couple companies. Our current partners are Clay, Harvey, Rogo, Decagon, Mercor. The first engagement, it took like three months to set up the entire thing — we're kind of building the airplane as it was flying. And now we're able to train a model with Harvey in under a month. 数据策展：用户的"修正信号"

Swyx：The other — yeah, I think the other thing also is — so for me the general question of like why people haven't pursued, you know, let's call it online models or continual learning, is just the curation of the data. Like, what is the sort of data curation solution here?

Ronak：Yeah, yeah, totally. So I think the interesting thing here is like every company is storing their usage, their data, in slightly a different way, right? And you have this entire system of basically two things.

One, what are agents doing in a product? So you can imagine for Cursor, they're obviously like writing code, they're running commands. And then for like, I don't know, a GTM workflow, it could be that the agent is creating tables or enriching columns. And so that's the easy part — that's like in an OpenAI-like tool call / tool response format.

The interesting part though is: what are all of the other things that users are doing in a product? And so at Windsurf for example, this was users are modifying the code after an agent. They're saying like, "Hey, I actually wanted this button on the left after the agent wrote a website," or, "I wanted this class structure this way." That's all the very useful information that's happening in a product.

I think when people think about online learning, continual learning, they'll first think of like accept/reject or thumbs up / thumbs down — like some of those binary signals. It turns out that that's actually very noisy. You can imagine — like, when is the last time you put a thumbs up / thumbs down in Cursor or Claude Code? Never, right? And everyone's kind of vibe-accepting everything. So it turns out those signals are actually quite noisy.

But what is useful is all the directions of, like, the user is saying, "Hey, you should have gone this way," or modifying some of the attempts of the agent. And the cool part about this is this works in any product. So for a legal workflow it's the same thing: the agent attempts to do some research and then write some redlines or create an Excel spreadsheet, and then afterwards the lawyer goes back and maybe has to do some more research because the agent missed something — and that sort of taste — and then again also modifying some of the contracts.

And so now the tricky part is how do you take all this data and then condense it into the right format to train on?

SDPO：Self-Distillation Policy Optimization

And so there's a couple of really exciting things here that we've been working on. One is just purely an infrastructure question — when we figured out a way to take all this information and build an auto-research pipeline that is now able to take whatever these vectors are of what the user is doing and make those into judges, into evals, environments.

But the other exciting part is the actual algorithms behind continual learning. So one of the other really cool things — I'll just go ahead and share my screen again — that we released very recently is Scaling Up Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO).

RL 的局限

So if you're not familiar with this, I can give the high level real quick. So for RL — basically the way RL works, and that's the leading post-training paradigm — you have an agent and it goes and explores the world a bunch of times, and then at the end of the day you get a reward signal. It could be a judge, it could be like something from production. And then you basically take that number and then use that to update and say like, "Hey, this entire trajectory was really good, this entire trajectory is really bad," and then it learns from that.

So it works pretty well — obviously all of our frontier models that we see today are trained on RL — but it doesn't really work for this new world of continual learning. The reason is RL, it's still taking all of this kind of useful information from the real world — like I mentioned, all the corrections and everything — and putting it into just one number, which is really broken.

自蒸馏的解法

And so what self-distillation does, which is really cool, is — takes — or I guess the high level here is we start out with a student rollout. So if you're familiar with distillation in general, it's you have a student that is usually a less smart model and then a teacher which is a smarter model, and you're basically saying, "Okay, let me distill all the smart stuff from the teacher into the student."

The cool unlock with self-distillation is you don't actually need a smarter teacher. Your student could be the smartest model, but to make the teacher smarter, I'm actually going to take some privileged information or a hint and put that into context of the teacher. And now I have a suddenly slightly smaller — smarter — model that I can fit into.

So in practice, what this looks like — let's take this example here where we have an agent, it's asking — or a user asks like, "How much is my flight ticket to New York?" We have the agent like look up some stuff, and then we actually get like the wrong ticket information. Now we can actually go back, because from production we have some hidden information, and we can say, "Actually, let me give the teacher a smart hint." Then we match the student log probs to that teacher information, and then suddenly we're able to take not just like a binary reward, but truly actual text, and guide the model in that direction.

So this is a huge unlock of SDPO. And we've done some very exciting kind of modifications and scaling it up. It's been done in a lot of academic cases but no one's actually been able to scale it up to real-world use cases.

So we started training on APEX agents — which is an awesome benchmark that Mercor put out, which is modeling a lot of real-world behavior — and we're able to see very amazing gains on some of these workflows. So for example also the convergence rate just goes up way faster. This is normal GRPO-style training, and then we're able to obviously be way faster.

So this is on our blog post as well. It's very awesome to see. And we do some modifications as well to training on real off-policy data, which is what happens in production not necessarily from your current model, and making it a lot more robust to real-world use cases.

Ronak：Well, so I actually — we have 11 of us now. So we've been growing really fast.
