They're determining who will be chairman of the Politburo, who will assign what workforce to do what efforts for them, which 600-million-dollar stock trades their families will make to benefit and enrich themselves as they fly around in their private jets on the taxpayers' money."

"And we are watching it all before our very eyes as individual liberties are eroded. I am fired up about it. I see it happening. You know Sacks is on the show this week."

"You can't just take strays like that, Freeberg. Expand on who the Politburo is."

谁是"政治局"？

"Yeah, so basically — the Politburo is the leaders who elect themselves to dictate the flow of the economy, the allocation of capital, what work individuals are allowed to do, and what activities they're allowed to do in an unfree society — which is what they're creating."

"They are the true oligarchs. And this is Elizabeth Warren, Bernie Sanders, Ro Khanna. This is the group that is trying to coalesce power and create for themselves a system whereby they have greater influence, greater control over aspects of the economy."

"They want to seize the means of production. They want to control education. They want to control media. And anytime there's an effort by an individual enterprise, or an individual themselves, to go out and build a business and succeed and do something that's outside of their scope and their span of control, they lose their minds over it."

对"虚伪美德"的反击

"And that's what we're seeing. So I react to their tweeting and their bullshit, where they're basically trying to contort things about inequality and fairness and justice — when the truth is, they are the rising empire, the evil empire in Star Wars."

"They are the folks who want to take from all of us what we were endowed with when this nation was started, and what many people came to this country for — which is individual freedom and liberty, the ability to build a business in peace, the ability to make decisions to do what you want with your own assets, and to have functionally private property."

"And they're taking it all away. And they're trying to take it all away. And we're watching piece by piece, step by step. So they are forming a Politburo where they can effectively control the economy, control education, control the media, and tell us all what we can and can't do and say."

"And it's frustrating to me to watch it, because it's masqueraded as bullshit virtue, as justice, as equity. It's a bunch of nonsense words that they use to try and make themselves seem virtuous — when at the end of the day, they are fundamentally evil. And I hate watching it. So that's why I react so aggressively to it."

为什么中间派会"上钩"？

"Freeberg, I think you said something that's really important — which is, they present it as virtue."

"That's right."

"Put yourself in the body of a person that's probably middle-of-the-road-ish but is falling for it. Why do you think they're falling for it?"

"Because they have needs and wants and desires individually, that this person is saying this system, this evil empire, will give to you if you take the knee:"

  • "We will give you the education for free."
  • "We will give you the child care for free."
  • "We will give you the food for free."
  • "We will give you the paycheck every week."
  • "We will give you, we will give you, we will give you..."

"Those promises — in a democratic system like this, or in a fake democratic..."

[注：转录原文在此处循环卡带，以上段落在源稿中被重复多次直至截断。]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的元结构：这段转录本身就是它所批判内容的镜像
最让我意外的观察是——这份转录稿在技术层面"卡带"了二十多遍，同一段开场白和Freeberg的"政治局"演讲被一字不差地循环播放。**而这恰好就是Freeberg论点的形式化证明**：当一种叙事（无论是"自由vs暴政"还是"公平vs精英"）被高频重复、剥离上下文、不断回环时，它就从"观点"变成了"环境噪音"——即所谓的**意识形态背景辐射**。
播客作为媒介本身已经异化为它所批评的东西：一个**循环灌输的系统**。Freeberg激烈反对"控制媒体"的政治局，却在一个把他自己的话重复14次的频道上被消费。这是麦克卢汉式的反讽——媒介即讯息，而该讯息是：**所有政治批判最终都会被它依附的媒介结构所反噬**。
## 二、跨领域连接：Freeberg的"基于度曲线"与马斯洛倒置
Freeberg的同伴们调侃说：身价越涨他越"based"（敢说狠话）——"九位数Freeberg有顾虑，十位数Freeberg直接YOLO"。这看似玩笑，却揭示了一个鲜少被命名的现象：**财富-言论自由的U形曲线**。
把它放到马斯洛需求层次里

item.title: [a16z] The Media Game Has Changed
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察：企业品牌本身是"广播时代"的临时产物

Mark 在闲谈中抛出了一个几乎被所有人忽略的历史结构性观察——"公司作为抽象品牌"这件事，其实只存在了大约 90 年。1930 年代以前是 Ford、Edison、Chrysler——人名即公司；1930 年代后媒体高度集中（三大电视网、三大报纸的头版），信息必须被压缩到"原子化品牌单元"才能穿过那根"窄吸管"，于是 P&G、GE、Coca-Cola 式的抽象企业品牌应运而生。

换句话说，"Tide"和"Crest"不是市场营销学的胜利，而是带宽稀缺的副产品。当带宽回归无限（podcast、Substack、X），品牌自然回流到人——Elon=SpaceX、Karp=Palantir、Palmer=Anduril。这意味着 MBA 课本里教的"品牌学"大半是一种媒介物理学的化石，而不是永恒真理。一旦你这样看，"创始人即品牌"就不是新潮流，而是回归常态——异常的反而是过去那 90 年。

二、跨领域连接：Alex Karp 的"本体论与编排"= 苏菲派的"否定神学"

最大胆的连接来自 Karp 的策略。他从不解释 Palantir 在做什么，只反复说两个没人懂的词：ontology 和 orchestration。表面看这是营销失败，实则是精确复刻了中世纪苏菲派与基督教神秘主义中的"否定神学"（apophatic theology）——通过"说它不是什么"来塑造一种不可言说的崇高感。

更深的跨界类比是奢侈品定价学的"凡勃伦悖论"：越是无法直接描述的东西，感知价值越高。Hermès 从不解释 Birkin 包"为什么贵"；米其林三星从不写菜单细节。Karp 把这套逻辑搬进了 B2B 软件——一个本应最讲 ROI、最讲清楚的领域。他卖的不是软件功能，是"在场感（presence）的稀缺性"——只在最重要的世界议题旁边出现。

这揭示了一个隐藏规律：在注意力过剩的时代，"可解释性"反而成了贬值因子。能被一句话讲清楚的公司，是可被替代的公司。

三、最颠覆的元洞察：旧媒体训练 CEO 的真正功能是"驯化"

旧媒体培训的表层目的是"防止说错话"，但 Ben 和 Mark 反复描述的体验——录像、回放、当面羞辱、"看你怎么搞砸的"——这套流程在心理学上其实是经典的操作性条件反射 + 创伤式去敏感化，与训练战俘、训练演员、训练神职人员的核心技法同构。

它的真实产出不是"会说话的 CEO"，而是一个被去除棱角、能在镜头前自动激活'塑料人格'的躯壳。Mark 那句"他们走下台还很自豪——因为他们没有制造任何新闻"是惊人的——这是一个把'没有信息量'当作 KPI 的行业。

这里的隐藏系统性真相是：旧媒体生态需要 CEO 是无趣的，因为有趣的 CEO 会绕过记者。记者作为信息中介，其权力来源于 CEO 的"被翻译需求"。一旦 CEO 自己能讲三小时 Rogan，中介就失业。所以"媒体培训"本质是媒介阶层为了维持自身存在而设计的客户驯化协议——它从来不是为了帮 CEO 表达，而是为了让 CEO 必须通过记者才能表达。

新媒体革命的真正含义因此被低估了：它不只是"渠道更多了"，它是信息中介阶层的整体祛魅。这也解释了为何传统媒体的反应是越来越激进的"speak truth to power"——这是一个失去仲裁权的祭司阶层的最后姿态。

四、一个埋藏的策略性矛盾

Gabby 和 Mark 之间存在一个未点破的张力：Gabby 主张"先把 message 想清楚再分发"，而 Karp 的实践恰恰相反——他根本没有产品 message，他只是把自己嫁接到当下最有张力的世界叙事上。这两套打法对应两种创始人原型：

  • 传播者型（Ryan Petersen、Karp）：以世界为载体，公司是隐含的署名；
  • 产品讲述型（Gabby 描述的多数 SaaS 创始人）：以公司为主语，需要严格的 message-market fit。

普通 CEO 往往两头不到岸——既没有 Karp 的"世界叙事嫁接能力"，又懒得做 Gabby 强调的"输出倒推输入"的硬功夫。这中间地带，就是大多数科技公司营销失败的真正坟场。

📝 转录文本

开场：新旧媒体的根本差异

One rule of old media is don't be interesting. Like that's the worst thing you can do.

There is no way to get to anything resembling a story that you're going to like through the traditional media anymore. Like it's just basically not possible.

Old media you had very restricted channels with very restricted formats. New media is unlimited formats, unlimited channels. And the brand is now the person.

The grand wizard of this is Alex Karp. If you watch his interviews, he never talks about Palantir. Everybody just naturally thinks inside out — me and my company and my product out into the world. Don't think that way. Think in terms of: what are the most interesting things happening in the world, and then how do those things relate to us.

Old media is defense-oriented. New media is offense.

Talk more about why that is.

There's still this anxiety that people have, which is legacy media somehow is like where the respectability is, the prestige is. I don't believe that anymore and I think it's very important for people to kind of get that out of their system.

寒暄与引子

So, first I just want to say it's Gabby's birthday today. Welcome Gabby.

You know, I'm so excited the Knicks won 5 of 6 for many reasons, but one is that Ben had floor seats to the game and wasn't going to make it today. And so we're so lucky that we get to have Ben here.

And Mark — not a huge basketball guy — but he did tweet about how Jaylen Brunson, he had a comment where he operates purely on instinct, not on introspection. And Mark, I appreciate that.

[ __ ] maxing — obviously working.

Many people are saying, you know, across industries. And so I actually think that's a pretty interesting segue, because one of the rules of new media, Mark, that we were talking about with the CEO last week in our growth portfolio is authenticity — being able to have the same conversations on camera that you would have in person behind closed doors.

Why don't you talk about that a bit more, and why that's so important to really nail — in terms of nailing your voice and how to build that presence? Because a lot of mistakes that founders will make is trying to be too buttoned up, trying to be too media trained, etc. As we uncover the new rules of new media, why don't you start there?

一、Mark 的形成性经历：旧媒体训练的真相

Yeah. So, I had this really formative experience when I was younger. So in the 90s, when Ben and I were coming up — pre-blogs, pre-YouTube, all this stuff — in those days the assumption was 100% of what you did, if you were running a company or doing anything, was you're going to have to work through established media, legacy media. And then everybody would get media trained in how to do it. And, you know, most painful experience in the world.

Oh yeah. So for people who haven't been through media training — it's actually watch yourself on TV.

媒体训练实录

Yeah, so here's how media training worked. I don't know if they still do it — do they still do it? Do you guys still do it? Okay. Yeah, probably.

So for people who haven't been through it: you get put in front of one of these cameras in your face, and then somebody — who's supposed to be your friend — does the full 60 Minutes interview with you, you know, for like an hour. And then they literally make you sit there and watch it, which is just like the meanest thing you could do to somebody.

And critique you, and you're looking at yourself, [ __ ] it up. And they're going, "You see how you [ __ ] that up?"

And you're like, "Wow, I'm really uncomfortable now. I'm never going on TV."

Exactly. And you're like, "Can we just fast—" "No, you can't fast forward. We're going to watch the entire thing. And then maybe we'll watch it again."

It is very revealing. You discover all kinds of things about your personality. By the way, you also discover all the extra words you use.

How much you hate your voice.

Yes, exactly. How much you hate how you look. It's incredible.

旧媒体的"塑料人格"产出

And so, if your ego survives the beating, you go on to a successful career. That's what you did.

But it was always so weird, because the result is just like — wow, you see important people with important things to say on TV, and they just seem like plastic people. It's all very fake and staged. And, you know, in the old days all the anchor people or interviewers are using anchor-man voice, and then you get a CEO up there who just says the most innocuous things possible.

And a lot of CEOs in those days — and by the way still — they rank their success in doing an interview or giving a speech based on minimum controversy. So they come off stage — I've worked with lots of CEOs — they're very proud of themselves because they didn't make any news.

Yeah, that is the key.

So all news is bad news.

二、那位反常规的培训师：60 Minutes 制作人的颠覆建议

So I — we hire at the time a company — what was considered the best media trainer, a guy who had previously been a producer at 60 Minutes.

Lowell Bergman, who'd been a producer — he was actually quite a well-known guy at the time. He had been Mike Wallace's producer. Lowell Bergman became — Bergman, no…

Well, I mean, Mike Wallace, it was that whole complex.

He's like: we're not doing any of the classic media training that we do. He said, we're going to do the thing that everybody always underrates, which is: we're going to get you to basically just say all the things in public that you would say if you were sitting having lunch with a friend.

And I remember, like, the back of my head blew open. Because it's just like — okay. And then immediately you're like, "Why are we paying this guy? That's very obvious advice. Nobody else will give you that advice."

核心原则

He just said: look, if you are on stage or in an interview and you're talking about something and you don't know that topic inside out already — what the hell are you doing there?

The only thing you should ever be talking about is something you know intimately. If you know it intimately, you should be able to talk about it in a viscerally interesting way — relay your thoughts in a way that comes across as a very interesting person. Because it's just like if you're sitting across the table talking to a friend.

So his training was 100% to try to get you to not do all the other stuff and basically be able to do that.

"枢轴"技巧

And then — because you assume the media is adversarial — the other part was what he called "the pivot": basically, you never answer their questions, you always answer your own questions. Which, by the way, makes it a little unnatural.

It does. But it's the Jedi Knight thing that made that whole approach work: you're talking naturally; one of the ways you talk naturally is you just refuse to answer the bad questions and substitute in your good question.

30 年验证

So we've been watching the evolution of the whole new media landscape for 30 years. And if you watch what Palmer Luckey does, or what Alex Karp does, or what Elon does — or any of the great communicators — that's what they're doing. I'd say put Jensen in that rank lately as well.

And by the way, this is the rise of the long-form podcast — the three-hour conversation — and the idea of just interesting people having interesting conversations, which was very radical at one point and is now a common thing.

So I think that remains the actual advice. And of course, Eric, to your point, what that gets across is authenticity: in addition to what's being said, do I, as the viewer, actually feel like I'm meeting the real person?

三、为何旧媒体是防守，新媒体是进攻

Related — we've been talking about how old media is defense-oriented, new media is offense. Talk more about why that is and what that playbook looks like. One of the things we were talking about, Mark, in that meeting is the importance of outside-in — situating your story within the context of what's happening externally.

新闻业的两大使命及其崩塌

Yeah. So the press, in my view — and reporters get furious at me when I say this, which is how I know I'm correct — the traditional/legacy press defined itself as having two functions.

One was impartial, objective journalism — sometimes called "the voice from nowhere" — which meant presenting both sides of something. Under the assumption that everything has two sides, and not more than two sides. So there's always something a little bit weird about that. But at least the idea of showing an issue, articulating it, letting different spokespeople for different points of view actually say it.

The second mission was to "speak truth to power."

That was the one that got in the way of the first.

That's right. The way they ended up describing that was "afflict the comfortable and comfort the afflicted." And at this point we know what that means.

Well, it devolved into "power to truth." It's the press's way of intimidating you into not saying what you thought.

Yeah. There's a line between actually objective and then activist — somebody with an agenda.

My view is that over time, the second one just lightly swamped the first one — and then it just became the thing. As a consequence, the very nature of the interaction changed.

Mark 的亲历

The short version: I did tons of traditional media between 1994 and 2017. Every once in a while there was a hit piece or a bad-faith thing, but I don't know — 90% of the time I felt like it had been a good idea to do it, and I'd been given a chance to tell my thing.

By the way, for almost that entire run, most people in the country and in the press thought startups were kind of cool, and tech was kind of cool — kind of fun, and kind of good for America that there were these tech companies, and the products were kind of fun to use.

I kind of remember those days.

Yeah, in the ancient myths of the past — and it was exciting, and a lot of reporters, editors genuinely viewed it as: "Oh, we need to explain this to our readers and viewers."

And then, as we like to say, in 2017 things changed.

Since then, my view is that the second mode — the agenda-driven mode — really took over. And I think it probably will never actually unwind.

实践结论：Go Direct

So the practical reality, as we always tell our founders: there is no way to get to anything resembling a story you're going to like through the traditional media anymore. It's basically not possible.

Every once in a while you can land a story but you can't run a strategy.

Yeah. People point to individual successes, and I'll just be like, okay, that's the exception that proves the rule — there are 99 others that are not like that.

So there needs to be a new approach. And the new approach comes in two parts:

  1. What we all call "go direct" — you have to tell your own story, increasingly through your own channels and through the channels of allies.
  2. New voices and new media — because the legacy press has gone so bananas, this massive opportunity has opened up for the creation of new media. The examples — many of whom are in this room — are just spectacular.

怀旧：从 Charlie Rose 到今天

It used to be — even in the golden age of media — like, if you wanted to watch a smart person talk about something for an hour, it was Charlie Rose. When I was a kid in the 80s, it was Charlie Rose at midnight. There was something called CBS News Overnight, and he was literally at midnight. And pre-VCR, you'd stay up until midnight to watch a smart conversation.

To go from that to what we have today in the podcast world, in the Substack world — is just such an incredible advance. The new media world is supremely high quality, doing incredibly well.

But there's still this anxiety people have: that legacy media somehow is where the respectability or the prestige is. It sounds so silly to say anymore, but I at least don't believe that anymore. It's very important for people to get that out of their system, because the world has changed.

华盛顿的例子

So we were just in Washington, with all the Washington people, senators and whatnot. And I was asking them: what does everybody in Washington read? And it's the Mark Halperin newsletter — which is new media. I fully expected Axios, the Washington Post, and so forth — because the one holdout from people in the firm on old media is "well, except for Washington, they all read this old stuff." And not even that is true.

So Mark's right.

四、创始人成为代言人：能不能逃？

Mark, you mentioned "going direct." We're certainly in the era now of going direct. It feels like it's table stakes for founders. But I've talked to a lot of founders who feel that they now have a second job — being the spokesperson for their company on top of running the business day to day.

And you can think of a lot of great founders who spent their lives or careers in a lab or staring at a computer — this is not what they've been trained in. So I'm curious about your advice for founders who maybe are uninterested in doing this or don't know where to start. Do they have to get good at it, or are there other paths for them?

Ben 的回答：品牌就是人

So I think this is tricky. If you go: okay, old media — how did that work? And new media?

Old media: you had very restricted channels with very restricted formats, and the brands were the companies. So yes, you could talk to the New York Times or CNN if you wanted to get the word out, but you were forced into a quote or a very short interview, and you were representing a brand that wasn't you. The whole media strategy was basically: get your name out there without tainting the company to the point where it could never recover — which is how you got into this very defensive posture, because you could never take anything back. That was a big rule in old media.

New media is the opposite: unlimited formats, unlimited channels, and the brand is now the person. Were people talking about — like, when every Democrat came out and was mad about SpaceX? They weren't. They were mad about Elon, because he's a brand. And the same thing: is it Palantir, or is it Alex? Is it Anduril, or is it Palmer?

Over and over again — the companies that are winning in marketing, the brand is the person.

If you had another founder, or somebody who is a really permanent fixture and not the CEO, you could imagine that person being the brand — but it's going to be a person. I don't think there's a way around that.

新媒体的硬规则：必须有趣

The rule of new media is: it has to be interesting. Otherwise it gets drowned out. It has to be a person, and that person has to be interesting. Which is why old media is so dangerous — because the one rule of old media is don't be interesting. That's the worst thing you can do. You'll [ __ ] everything up.

I don't think a company can get away without it. Like, if you look at us as a firm: technically I'm the CEO, but Mark is more the brand — he does way more media than I do. And that works, because it's Andreessen Horowitz and nobody on the outside knows the [ __ ] difference. It's fine.

You can set up your company that way — they don't have to know how you're run. But it's got to be somebody who's there forever with the organization. It can't be the VP of marketing who's there for a three-year run and then is gone. That'll never work.

Angel is another example.

Yeah. One of my favorite things — happens about once a week — is when somebody congratulates me on writing Ben's book.

Which he always takes full credit for.

I do. I do. I just like — I really poured all my blood, sweat, and tears into it. I'm so glad somebody finally read it.

五、Mark 的历史结构性洞察：企业品牌只是 90 年的临时现象

I think there's actually a technological explanation for what Ben said, which is quite important.

Up until basically the 1930s, companies didn't have corporate brands. It was the Ford Motor Company. It was Thomas Edison. It was the Edison Electric Company. Just name on the door. It never occurred to Henry Ford to name his company anything other than the Ford Motor Company.

Well, he did have a company before the Ford Motor Company, right?

Yeah, which I think — somebody's got to look it up — I think it became like Cadillac or something. It was actually a car company that lasted, but he was CTO. He wasn't CEO, and he got in such a furious fight — because he was a hothead, Henry Ford — with the CEO that he quit his own company.

But a lot of those — Chrysler, those are guys. A lot of the early ones.

Oh yeah, they're all named after themselves. I think it was Ford, and then when he quit they renamed it.

So it was "name on the door." That was a given — because that's the guy running the company.

集中化媒体催生抽象品牌

Then in the 30s and 40s, you started getting these International Business Machines, General Electric, General Motors — abstract names. Then you got the consumer brand thing with Procter & Gamble: Tide, Colgate, Crest, Coca-Cola.

The Nifty Fifty — the conglomerate era.

Which led right to the conglomerates — basically piling up as many brands as you could.

In retrospect, I think that was because of the rise of centralized media. Before the 1930s, media was very decentralized — every town had 15 newspapers, tiny little radio stations everywhere. Starting in the 30s and 40s, media centralized hard, in a way that was historically unprecedented — probably a once-in-a-thousand-year thing.

窄吸管理论

The thing with centralized media is: it's like drinking everything through an incredibly narrow straw. By definition, if there are three TV networks and 24 hours in the day, how much time are you ever going to get? If there's the front page of three big newspapers, how many column inches are you going to get?

The message from any company had to get distilled down to the absolute minimum to get through those very narrow straws. That was the corporate brand phenomenon — getting things down to an atomic unit of a brand to get through that straw.

But everybody became convinced that was just universally how the world worked. I think it only made sense in the centralized media world. As that world unwinds and collapses, mechanically that's why this new approach is necessary.

Joe Rogan 案例

People running for president have to go on Joe Rogan, right? Up until 2024, the answer was absolutely not. After 2024, the answer is 100% they have to.

A big part of all the Democratic retrospectives of what happened in '24 was that Kamala didn't go on Rogan. 100% of the people who work in that world now think the next person is going to have to go on Rogan. And to go on Rogan means you're going to have to be on for three hours, and you're going to have to talk about anything.

So if you think about what that means for the bar of the person who's going to run — that's the new bar. You have to be interesting. And it goes right back to your question: you have to be the person who can do that.

If you're not the person who can do that —

Well, it's a big marketing deficit. You just put a ceiling on your whole opportunity.

I think the line from Succession — I can't avoid using based on your question — is: "If you can't ride two elephants at the same time, what are you doing in the circus?"

六、何时反击，何时沉默

I would love to hear how you apply this thinking to your own work. Ben, you mentioned before that you and Mark spend a lot of time discussing how and when to respond to things publicly — and also when to not say anything. So how do you decide when to fight back versus stay quiet? Are there times you wish you did things differently?

反击的诱惑与纪律

It's interesting — any time somebody says something negative about us, we want to respond. But you have to determine whether that's going to improve your position, or whether they're just feeding you bait to get you to highlight their stupid opinion.

It'll always improve your situation, if I —

Yeah. He always 100% wants to go back at them. You have to have some discipline, otherwise you'll spend your whole life responding. The bigger you get, the more people come at you.

The degenerate version is dealing with the people who comment on Twitter (or X, sorry). If you start answering the X replies of people who have 50 followers — by the way, my father always did that — it's a colossal waste of time, and you're amplifying somebody who has no audience. Those people shouldn't even be talking to you. They didn't do anything in life to have the right to talk to you. But there you are, talking to them and wasting your time. That's the extreme degenerate case.

反击当作品牌建设

However, if somebody comes out with something and it hits — and it's going after you — that's actually a real opportunity to boost the brand, to come across with your own point of view, your own position. This could be something in the media, or something somebody does in new media, that turns into real opportunity.

A reasonable portion of the brand we built was just responding to people attacking us. I always saw that every time it happened as: great, let's go.

One of my favorite things I ever wrote was about Instagram. We got into a kerfuffle over Instagram with the New York Times. We had a conflict — it was a thing. I responded, and it was at that time the biggest post I ever wrote. Everybody was like, "Yeah, [ __ ] that New York Times." It took us from here to here in one shot, just because everybody loves a fight.

So fights are good for brand building. But you've got to pick the right fight — to not just build somebody else's brand.

Eric 的早期困惑

To that end — when picking the battles: when I first joined, I would bring up these negative tweets we were getting and say, "Hey, these anons are saying bad things about us." And you guys would be like, "Who cares?" Like, this is in fact part of our success — meaning we're going to have more and more people saying negative things.

Well, specifically — they were saying things based on the things we were funding, right?

"You're funding slop."

It's like — bro, what even — if you think that — what is better in this world than the transfer of wealth from people who have a lot of money to people who have ideas, who want to build something? Even if we're wrong about everything, that's still good for humanity. So shut up, you hater. Idiots. Hater.

My response was: no, it's a great thing to get criticized for — funding startups and supporting those startups. That's fantastic.

不要中庸：要么爱你，要么恨你

There's an important change in new media within that: you really want people to hate you, and you want people to love you — but you don't want to be neutral. You don't want to be lukewarm, because then you're uninteresting.

You can't be interesting and not have people both hate you and love you. No matter what you do, there's too much jealousy and just hater-ate in the world to not have it be that way. As soon as you get big, that's what happens.

I used to — when people used to get upset about things written about us — say: nobody writes a puff piece on Rupert Murdoch or Elon Musk. It's never going to happen again. When you get to a certain size, people hate you. And that's good — that means you did it, you did something important, you made a mark on the world, people care about what you're doing.

You have to take it that way. If you take it as, "Oh, I've got to stop doing that so people like me," you'll ruin your marketing.

Mark, you've sometimes said the term, "they have all the right enemies." So it's picking the right people to hate you that galvanizes the people to love you.

七、如何组建新媒体团队

What about building the right marketing and media team in the age of new media? What have you guys learned, or what advice do you have for CEOs in terms of making sure you get the right personnel?

不要雇旧媒体老兵

The first principle: if you're trained in old media, it's very, very, very hard to do new media. You have to be a very exceptional person to make that transition. If you spent 10 years doing old media, there are laws of physics, there are rules of the game — vetting reporters, rude Q&As, this and that. Everything about it is opposite world.

There are very few people who can go: "Okay, I'm getting out of opposite world and into the new media world." So you have to be careful — for the new media side of what you're doing — about hiring too much experience in not new media. It looks like marketing, but it's not the same. It's a completely new skill set. That's probably the thing I'd worry about the most.

找做过的人

Then, like with Eric — what we looked for when we brought you on, for example, is: the best thing on new media is have you done it? Have you built some brand on something, some audience? Do you know how to build an audience? That's the core thing. If you can't do that, then it doesn't matter what other skills you have. You have to be able to deliver it end to end.

And in terms of people we've brought on — like Alex Denko or Henry or Brent — they were product managers or founders or investors, but they were obsessed with the discourse. They were listening to the podcasts. They were writing themselves. You could tell in the proof of work, even though they weren't doing the thing.

讲故事是核心能力

Yeah. So storytelling on your new media team — that's a really good point. The reason Alex is so good is he's a world-class storyteller. Take marketing out of it for a second: can you put together a story that somebody wants to read or listen to? That's a real skill, and the elite level of it is way higher than the average level. People who can't do it can never do it.

Many people from the old marketing world don't have that skill, because you always relied on the principal — the CEO, someone — for the story. But if you can build a team that has multiple good storytellers, that's a huge power boost.

真实性与"新闻 vs 宣传"的伪二分

There's a really big disconnect — it goes back to the authenticity point — in how people in the media sphere think about this. Every reporter listening to what we're saying would be like: "Oh, there are only two things — there's journalism, and there's propaganda. The mainstream press, the traditional press, does journalism. And if you're doing any kind of direct anything, it's propaganda — it's just marketing, it's just trying to sell something."

I'd say that's a distinction we 100% don't agree with. The critique aspect: I don't think the press does much of what even they would describe as objective journalism anymore.

But the positive side: one of the reasons you want to be authentic is because you want people to actually understand who you are, what you do, and the context within which you're doing what you're doing. You want to explain yourself, and you want to explain the world.

When we talk about storytelling, it's very much not storytelling like a made-up story. It's storytelling of: here's what's actually happening.

In an interesting way, with tension, with a beginning and an end, that somebody is interested in following the whole way.

That's right. As a consequence — when Ben and I get stuff on the street, when we routinely get told, "Wow, I watched the podcast, I saw this interview, I read that post from the firm" — 100% of the time they're like, "That was great. I really understand what that topic is about." That's a very honest, legitimate, positive reaction to honest, legitimate action on our part.

The other way to put this: this isn't just something people should do. It's a responsibility for people in our world and in tech now. The changes happening in tech right now are profound. They're really hard to understand from the outside and really complicated. There are huge amounts of noise in the environment. So actually explaining honestly what's going on —

I'll brag on our team: we put a SpaceX post up today. Although it has my name on it, I had nothing to do with — credit. I cannot take any credit for it. The feedback we're already getting is it's the best thing anybody's ever written on SpaceX. And I think that's true. It literally lays out the actual truth. I'm really proud of that.

八、给创始人的具体建议：Go Direct 的常见错误

I want to close with some deeper advice for founders on going direct. Gabby, I'll start with you. What are some mistakes you see CEOs making, and things to avoid?

错误一：先分发，后想信息

I'd say there are two mistakes I commonly saw working with founders, and they're related.

The first: it can be very easy to fall into this trap because the timeline feels so addicting — a lot of founders really over-index on distribution and tactics before actually getting the message right.

Distribution is really just a multiplier on the message. If the message is wrong, now you've amplified something that is either irrelevant for your business, or not the thing your audience needs to hear, or — as we've talked about, maybe worst of all — uninteresting. Now you've told everybody you care about that you're not very interesting.

In practice, a lot of founders would come to us and say, "How do we go viral?" or "How do we get on Joe Rogan?" If you actually think about it: you could get on Joe Rogan and then not say the right thing. That's maybe the worst thing ever.

This applies not just to old media but to new media. You could go on Bloomberg or Fox and get the message wrong. You could also do the same thing with new media or going direct. So the first mistake is not actually spending the time to get the message right. That's the highest-leverage, most important thing to get right.

错误二：从输入开始，而非从结果倒推

The next mistake is figuring out how to do it. You can get the message wrong if you focus too much on the inputs as opposed to the outputs or outcomes you want to drive towards.

This is paradoxical because the companies that are very successful struggle with this the most — there are a lot of interesting things you could say about your company. There are so many milestones. The mission is compelling. There are a lot of different things you'd want to say. But depending on what you're trying to achieve, the thing you want to say should probably be different. Just because everything is true doesn't mean it's all relevant or strategic for your business.

Instead of starting with the inputs — "here's this huge mess of everything we could say, let's just say it all and hope people remember the thing we think is most interesting" — start with the outcome. Do we want to sell to a certain type of enterprise customer? Do we want to hire a certain type of engineer who believes a certain thing about us and our role in the market?

Now you can work backwards: we know who these people are, we know what they believe, we know what they probably need to believe about us, and we know what feels in the discourse urgent and timely and personal to them. Then we work backwards and get the message right. Pair that with really powerful distribution, and you have a winning strategy.

团队与信息的耦合

And by the way — a really important point in what Gabby said — that really determines how you have to put the team together. If you put together a team you can't have a conversation with about your message and what you're saying, you've done yourself a disservice.

You need the team to help make the story great before they go, because it's real easy to go "okay, market this empty box." There are a few marketers who could probably do that, but it's hard. You really want the team to be invested in the message, and then all the variations you need to distribute that message are going to be really good and on point. If the team doesn't have an opinion on message, that's very difficult.

九、外向 vs 内向：Alex Karp 的"嫁接策略"

Mark, one of the things we said in that meeting with the growth portfolio company the other week was: what you admire about people like Alex Karp or Palmer or Elon or others is the ability to not just focus on what they're doing, but also go outside-in — talk about what's happening in the world, and how they situate their worldview, their company, their product within that.

Why don't you talk about that principle and how do people get good at it?

最常见、最糟糕的两类故事

So the most common VC self-marketing story is: "Oh, I met this great founder, and we went on a walk, and they called me many times, and I thought he was great, and I eventually convinced him to take my money."

The most common startup story is: "Oh, we're a brand new company, we're all fired up, we're ready to go, it's going to be great, we have new products, we hope you try it."

I mean, those stories just make you want to stab yourself in the neck. Like, as in — it's just the lamest story in the world.

细节的重要性

By the way, one of the keys is: good storytellers have great details. Details matter a lot. The thing about Mark's [hypothetical] thing that makes it so horrible is no details — just like, "blah blah blah, I'm so great, I talked to a founder, he really liked me, you'll probably really like me too." Like, what? Who cares?

And it's almost — they come across as fake humble. "I'm humbly… I'm humbled by…" That's the most fake humble thing in the world. "I'm humbled by —" you're not even remotely humbled.

There's a whole bunch of critiques: it's fake bragging, it's boring, it's egocentric to no purpose, it's passive-aggressive, and it's indistinguishable. There are a thousand — it's every single startup.

Everybody can gaze at their navel.

Everybody can do that. There's nobody who can't. That's not a differentiated story.

为什么人们不敢讲更大的故事

That's the default narrative people fall into. And then it also feels like doing anything bigger than that is arrogant — like, who are we to go tell some bigger thing? And I don't even know what the bigger thing would be. And are the people who tell a bigger thing full of themselves and trying to make themselves look even bigger?

So there's a real reluctance to expand the telescope out.

黄金法则

What I always tell people is the exact opposite of all of that is true: the story of you and your startup is not inherently an interesting story. But there is almost certainly an interesting story that involves your startup. And that story — this is the cheat code — is something else in the world that's happening that is incredibly interesting, that your company relates to.

Alex Karp 大师课

The grand wizard of this is Alex Karp. If you watch his interviews, he never talks about Palantir. The only thing he ever says about Palantir — Mark pointed this out to me — is "ontology and orchestration." Two words that nobody knows what they mean. Nobody knows what Palantir does as a result, but it doesn't matter, because it's like — the future of the US military: Palantir. Super-intelligence: Palantir. Neurodivergence: — I mean, whatever the story is that's really good, Alex will go tell that story. Whatever is interesting, he'll just start talking about. And then because he's the founder and CEO of Palantir, that just works.

I'd say it kind of as the C—but it's really a good strategy. He takes it to the very extreme. But that's the right idea: find the most interesting story you can plug your company into, and then tell that story.

Ryan Petersen 的同样手法

What happens is, when something happens in the world involving US military, AI in the military, geopolitics in China — Karp's the first phone call, because he's been out there talking about that.

By the way, Ryan Petersen is in the audience — he's done a phenomenal job of that.

Incredibly good at that. The difference between talking about freight versus talking about the global supply chain is completely collapsing during COVID and we're all going to starve to death — therefore he's the guy who literally goes on 60 Minutes to explain to the world that yes, we are all about to starve to death.

From the helicopter, right?

Yes. From the helicopter. "Look at all those ships that are never landing. Your children are about to starve. By Flexport."

It works incredibly well. And you don't even have to say "by Flexport" — it just happens. It's implicit.

进入"重要房间"

The other thing — especially for anything involving enterprise sales, which is certainly Flexport but also Palantir — a big part is: are you important enough to meet with the CEO of your customer? Are you important enough to get in the room with the decision maker? Are you important enough to meet with the Secretary of War? To be in the White House? To be with Fortune 500 CEOs?

I have a little startup that's doing interesting things — does not do that. But if you have seen me talk about the big important things happening in the world and how they relate, and you have potentially an answer to it — that's absolute catnip. Everybody wants that.

重新表述的法则

So the trick is: don't think inside-out — me and my company and my product out into the world. Think in terms of: what are the most interesting things happening in the world, and then how do those things relate to us?

Ben 的补充：CEO 必须研究世界

This is where CEOs go off the rails — they're so focused on what they're doing, all they want to do is tell their story, and they're not even paying attention to what's going on in the world. Which is understandable because you're trying to build something. But to do the marketing exercise, investing in understanding what's happening out there is critically important. You can't do it without that.

投资者关系也一样

This happens also in investor relations. Every public company by law does these S-1s, K-1s, 10-Qs, all these filings — they fully explain everything the company does, every possible hedge, all these incredible descriptions, the annual letters.

What percentage of investors in, you know, Palantir have read the S-1, the K?

Like, 0.0001%.

What percentage have you seen Alex on YouTube doing his thing?

100%.

And the amount of time that goes into that document — right? So you just take that time and just understand enough about the world to put together a good story.

十、收尾：这是一项可以学会的技能

On that note, we'll end with the idea that this skill set is not only something you're born with. It can be developed, learned, and cultivated. Mark, you were saying the other day that when you look back at Alex Karp's old interviews, they're very different. When you look at Alex, or Palmer, or Elon, or Ryan, or Amjad (who came here earlier) — you look back at their old tweets or interviews and they're nowhere close to where they are now.

Oh yeah, it's a skill set for sure. There are people who are gifted and people who are less. If you look at Donald Trump's interviews in the 80s, they're very old-media interviews. He's actually restrained — sorry. And then, he did figure out new media, and he's always super entertaining and interesting, which is kind of the magic of his popularity. So even at that level, you can develop it.

Well, on that note, it's a great place to wrap. Please give a round of applause for Gabby, Ben, and Mark.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：企业品牌本身是"广播时代"的临时产物
Mark 在闲谈中抛出了一个几乎被所有人忽略的历史结构性观察——**"公司作为抽象品牌"这件事，其实只存在了大约 90 年**。1930 年代以前是 Ford、Edison、Chrysler——人名即公司；1930 年代后媒体高度集中（三大电视网、三大报纸的头版），信息必须被压缩到"原子化品牌单元"才能穿过那根"窄吸管"，于是 P&G、GE、Coca-Cola 式的抽象企业品牌应运而生。
换句话说，**"Tide"和"Crest"不是市场营销学的胜利，而是带宽稀缺的副产品**。当带宽回归无限（podcast、Substack、X），品牌自然回流到人——Elon=SpaceX、Karp=Palantir、Palmer=Anduril。这意味着 MBA 课本里教的"品牌学"大半是一种**媒介物理学的化石**，而不是永恒真理。一旦你这样看，"创始人即品牌"就不是新潮流，而是**回归常态**——异常的反而是过去那 90 年。
## 二、跨领域连接：Alex Karp 的"本体论与编排"= 苏菲派的"否定神学"
最大胆的连接

item.title: [Business of Sport] Burnley’s £35m Win Opens the Floodgates, F1 on Water is Making it’s Mark, & The World Cup is Great
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Burnley 案件的真正破坏力：它把"违规"从财务题变成了博弈论题

表面看，Burnley 拿到的 3500 万英镑是一次性赔偿；但真正的转折点在于他们用了10 万次蒙特卡洛模拟来证明"如果 Everton 没超支，他们就不会降级"。这件事的意外之处在于——英超的合规判定从此进入了概率推理时代。过去俱乐部的财务模型只用来做转会决策，从现在开始，它会被反向用作对其他俱乐部提起诉讼的武器。

这意味着每家俱乐部的 CFO 实质上多了一项工作：不仅要算自己的 PSR，还要建一个"竞争对手如果作弊会如何影响我"的实时模型。这是从财务合规向对抗性数据科学的迁移，和量化对冲基金从单纯持仓管理走向"预测对手仓位"的演化路径几乎一模一样。

二、跨界连接：SailGP 与女足联赛是同一道题的两个相反答案

播客主持人自己没把这两件事连起来，但其实非常震撼——SailGP 和英国女子职业足球，做的是同一道"如何从零启动一个高端体育产权"的题，但走的是完全相反的路径：

  • 女足：先假设自己是男足缩小版，订下高薪资、高估值，然后被迫向下削减。
  • SailGP：先承认自己永远不会是大众市场，球队从 500 万美元起卖，再用 6 年涨到 6000 万。

这里有个反直觉的洞见："承认自己上限"反而创造了更高的增长曲线。SailGP 没有去争夺大众观众，而是精准锁定 Jars Morgan 所谓的"tribe"——付费力极强、密度极高的高净值族群。结果它的赞助商组合（Rolex、Emirates、KPMG）比许多传统大众体育还要奢华。

这不仅是体育商业模式的对比，它揭示的是一个普遍规律：当一个新产品想"快速变大"时，它通常无法定价；当它选择"先变密"时，它反而能定价。这和早期 Tesla 选择从 Roadster 而非 Model 3 起步、Substack 选择从一万个铁粉作者起步是同一种结构。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：英国体育的三起事件其实都在讲"业余文化的最后清算"

如果把本期三个看似无关的话题——Stokes 醉酒巡游、Everton 超支、Ineos 与 Ainslie 决裂——放到一起，会发现它们在讲同一件事：英国精英体育里"业余绅士文化"的最后挣扎。

  • 板球队认为"赢完球喝 11 小时酒"还是绅士文化的一部分；
  • Everton 老板觉得"砸钱救火"就是 ambition；
  • Ineos 一边收购顶级体育资产一边戏剧性撤退，把帆船队的开发当成富豪玩具而非长期产权。

这三种行为共有一个底层假设：体育是富人的特权领地，规则可以被姿态覆盖。而 Burnley 的胜诉、Sale GP 的成本帽、女足的薪资过剩，都是这套假设在不同维度被精算师文化取代的信号。

最意外的观察是：这不是道德问题，是会计问题。Stokes 那场 11 小时酒局之所以在 2026 年变得不能接受，不是因为社会更清教了，而是因为他每年挣 100 万英镑——当资本介入到足够深，"业余浪漫"就不再被精算师们买单。Premier League、ECB、Ineos 都在被同一只看不见的手——专业化的资本回报率要求——重新调教。

奇异的是：这与 18 世纪英国海军从私掠船过渡到正规舰队的逻辑完全一致——当一项活动的资本规模超过某个阈值，"绅士冒险家"就必须让位给制度、合规与可预测的回报机制。今天的英超 PSR 与 SailGP 的成本帽，就是这种过渡的体育版本。

But do you know what, Charlie? Calegp in Spain last year set a record for the most viewed event with 23 million viewers across 200 territories. The average about 18 million viewers.

So the numbers are the numbers are solid, right? There's something to build on.

I think if you're looking at the overall proposition, there's enough in there from a value perspective where there are people coming in here recognizing value, even if it's not how we generally judge value in sport to say, do you know what? I want to keep being associated with this because product is good. It's fast. It's entertaining.

Tribe 战略：精准 alpha 而非大众市场

When you say it's entertaining, I'm I'm you know th those TV audience numbers are half decent by the way, right? So I'm you know therefore those people find it entertaining.

Yeah. So therefore de facto it is entertaining to a degree.

But it strikes me that there's something which is almost clever than that going on which is basically to say okay as a sport we are never going to be a mass market sport. Therefore what we do need to do is absolutely capture our alpha within the premium market. We've got to package something here which the premium market can really buy into both in terms of the very rich parts of the premium market buying franchises. The next bit down from that having some luxury tourism around it. The next bit down from that turning up to the odd race and their home territory but also buying into the brands who are sponsoring it. And actually if all we do is get all of that absolutely spot on, we got a really decent proposition. Now, if eventually we can end up getting enough um TV audience to drive some TV rights money as well, great. But we're not based off that because that's not that's currently not even in the business plan.

Well, as I said, you've got teams here. We look at how teams and other sports generate revenue.

We know that over reliance on broadcast is an increasingly concerning element of any business model.

Absolutely right. This is basically looking at organizations that can create enough sponsorship to break even. Just sponsorship.

Yeah.

You know what's really interesting about Charlie, you and I um had a meeting last week with Jars Morgan, the former um head of sponsorship for HSBC and Hen subsequently who did the whole British Lion sponsorship

and Jars is now a consultant really advising the market on how to make yourself attractive to sponsors. And the thing which he is obsessed by is tribe,

right? You this tribe is so clear. This tribe is so clear and what Charles says is that the total numbers you're doing is less important than absolute clarity and spending power around who your tribe is.

And it seems to me that what this is is an absolutely brilliant commercial proposition. And if anything happens on the TV right side, that's the cream and the cherry on the cake, but the cake is having something which really hangs together for the big brands.

Ben Ainslie 的视角与 Ineos 纠纷

Look, Benley actually teed up very nicely, I saw in a statement. So Ben Ainsley uh is head of team GB, Sale GP, which is actually the Emirates GB racing. They're the defending champions. They won last year for the first time. And he just said, "Sailing has never had a commercially successful league or series of events before. The key is continuity racing around the globe, consistent teams, consistent branding, athlete recognition that builds over time."

Speaking about Benley, um probably a story for another day, but you know, he

did he had some challenges with Inos?

He he he has a very significant current dispute against Jim Jim Rackliff in any of this. I have.

It's quite an interesting one.

It is an interesting one considering that Inos's claims are actually on the boats

and their rights over these highly expensive I think up to like 180 million was spent on some of the development of some of them.

Um but Ben Enley now has got an umbrella company really. So it includes the America's Cup team for GB includes the sale GP team another uh indication of Inos's relatively rapid and dramatic retreat from a lot of their sporting assets

with the exception of their stake in Man United of course.

Of course,

or Rackliff himself has taken Emanuel, but we should do another one to ask you that that dispute between um Sir Ben Aninsley and Sir Jim Rackcliffe, two of the most prominent British success stories of the last 20 to 30 years in totally different fields, would be a really interesting bonus segment for for another day. We don't have the time to look at it now.

奖金池：与赛事现状匹配

No, it's not. And I just want to because we've been talking a lot about prize money and I I missed it out. Um you know, just interesting to see what they've actually been putting up for some of the teams. So, you know, the winner of each There's 13 of these events that go on throughout the season. 13 teams, 13 events. The winner of each takes $400,000. The winner takes um all championship final at the end of the season is $2 million. And the total prize pool is $12.8 million. So they've created a nice financial pool.

It's quite modest, isn't it? It's quite modest. And again, I like that that prize pool is reflective of where the series is right now. We can give you something, guys, but we're not going to pretend that we are F1.

Yeah.

Effectively, what women's professional football's done is it's tried to pretend that it is men's professional football before it's got revenues which are anything like it.

elements of Formula 1 and it's looked at how it can apply it in a different environment and it's recognized that there are lots of things in sailing

that will also speak to a similar demographic that formula 1 has managed to as you said tribe

tribe so I I I was I'm keen to talk about it you you brought it up as well because we're again it's very easy to be continually critical of a lot of things that come up however while this is absolutely not perfect and trust me there's plenty of questions as you said you could develop and have around the longer term financial viability of this as it grows and scales and as prices go It's working right now by the looks of it to an extent where you're seeing development, you're seeing interest increase, and you're seeing fans pay attention.

The challenge is can you really continue to bring people that don't know much about sailing, that new fan base in and start to care about this sport that cuz that was one of the aims of um Russell Cous and and Larry Ellison right right at the beginning. But I'm sure Larry Ellison will be quite relieved now that he doesn't have to finance the whole project cuz he did for the first few years. even if you've got $247 billion.

Um, so yeah, go and watch some of the events. It's on now. They're they're pretty much on.

And how do we find if we're in this country? What What's the best way to find out?

Guys, don't do that to me. Does

find out?

It's on TNT Sports or Discovery Plus or HBO or whatever the hell it's called now. Who knows? But no, TNT have the rights for it in in the UK platform.

Let's all head down to Portsmouth this summer.

Head down to Portsmouth or if you want, you know, you can go to Rio or

Yeah. Well, we we could do, but I'm not sure if the breakdown expense count yet um reaches that far, Charlie.

No, we need a few of you to to keep joining us on Substack. And maybe maybe speaking of which, we'll be going over to Substack now and having a little bit of fun in our bonus segment. But for those of you that aren't there yet, thank you so much for for joining us. What are you doing? Come over.

Um but Charlie, that was uh that was good fun. Thank you as always.

Thank you very much.

item.description:
## 一、Burnley 案件的真正破坏力：它把"违规"从财务题变成了博弈论题
表面看，Burnley 拿到的 3500 万英镑是一次性赔偿；但真正的转折点在于他们用了**10 万次蒙特卡洛模拟**来证明"如果 Everton 没超支，他们就不会降级"。这件事的意外之处在于——**英超的合规判定从此进入了概率推理时代**。过去俱乐部的财务模型只用来做转会决策，从现在开始，它会被反向用作**对其他俱乐部提起诉讼的武器**。
这意味着每家俱乐部的 CFO 实质上多了一项工作：不仅要算自己的 PSR，还要建一个"竞争对手如果作弊会如何影响我"的实时模型。这是从财务合规向**对抗性数据科学**的迁移，和量化对冲基金从单纯持仓管理走向"预测对手仓位"的演化路径几乎一模一样。
## 二、跨界连接：SailGP 与女足联赛是同一道题的两个相反答案
播客主持人自己没把这两件事连起来，但其实非常震撼——**SailGP 和英国女子职业足球，做的是同一道"如何从零启动一个高端体育产权"的题**，但走的是完全相反的路径：
- **女足**：先假设自己是男足缩小版，订下高薪资、高估值，然后被迫向下削

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1440期】9月加息来了！主动减仓：英特尔的操作思路✨20260618#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：鲍威尔（Kevin Walsh）这次"放权"，实质是把美联储从"舵手"降级成"乘客"

视频里小淘把Powell（视频转录里被翻译成"Kevin Walsh"）那句"让市场自己去解读数据"轻轻带过，但这其实是一次货币政策范式的静默政变。

过去几十年里，美联储花了巨大的政治资本去训练市场"听Fed的"——前瞻指引、点阵图、Fed Speakers轮番出场push back，全部目的是让Fed的叙事压倒市场的叙事。而这次主席说"你们看市场怎么解读就好"，等于公开承认：Fed不再有信息优势，也不再有叙事垄断权。

这背后藏着一个更深的政治结构问题：在特朗普高压、内部19人分歧极大（8人不加、剩余分散）的情况下，主席选择"不交SEP"+"听市场的"，其实是一种博弈论意义上的甩锅策略——把降息/加息的责任从"我决定"变成"市场逼我"。这跟一个CEO在董事会分裂时说"我们做用户调研决定"是同一种心理动作。

二、跨领域连接：两年期美债，是金融市场里的"前额叶皮层"

视频反复用一张图说"白线（2Y收益率）领先蓝线（联邦基金利率）"。但如果用神经科学的视角看，这其实是一个非常有趣的镜像结构：

  • 联邦基金利率 = 大脑的运动皮层（真正下达动作）
  • 2Y收益率 = 前额叶皮层（提前规划、预判、整合所有信号）
  • CPI/PCE/就业数据 = 感觉输入
  • 美联储Speakers = 基底神经节（决定是否要"否决"前额叶的冲动）

历史上Fed经常"否决"市场预判（2021年最典型——市场前额叶早就喊通胀了，Fed硬说transitory）。但现在Powell宣布"我不否决了"，意味着整个系统的决策回路从'多巴胺-基底节调节'退化成了'纯反射弧'——这会显著放大市场波动率，因为去掉了那个"踩刹车"的中间层。

小淘其实点到了这一层（"放弃沟通会增加波动"），但没说透：未来VIX的中枢可能系统性抬升，因为Fed主动放弃了波动率压制器的角色。这对所有卖波动率策略（covered call ETF、JEPI/JEPQ类）是一个结构性逆风，但视频里完全没提。

三、英特尔减仓决策里藏着的一个"反共识"哲学

小淘说："估值高从来不是清仓的理由，仓位占比才是。" 这句话听起来像常识，但实际上和99%散户的操作逻辑是颠倒的——散户的本能是"涨多了就跑"，而他的逻辑是"涨多了，仓位被动放大了，所以削回原比例"。

这其实是Ray Dalio的风险平价（Risk Parity）思想的家庭散户版，但应用在了个股层面。更深一层的连接是——这种思维模型和进化生物学里的"种群密度自调节"完全同构：当一个物种数量过多时，不是因为它"变坏了"才需要削减，而是因为它挤占了生态位的稳定性。

这里有一个真正反直觉的洞察：小淘对Intel的处理方式，恰恰证明了他不是"价值投资者"，而是"组合工程师"。 他口头说"基本面优先"，但行为上他的最高优先级其实是组合的反脆弱性——基本面是用来筛选标的的，但仓位管理才是真正的α来源。这跟巴菲特"集中持仓不动"的哲学是相反的，更接近Jim Simons的统计套利思路。

四、一个被所有人忽略的隐藏信号：Accenture暴跌的真正含义

视频说Accenture跌20%是因为"booking转负"+"市场担心AI颠覆咨询"。但真正值得警觉的跨领域信号是：

Accenture的现金流贴现率（FCF yield）跑到了银行股之下。这意味着市场在用"瓦解中的传统行业"（比如百货、有线电视）的折现率定价Accenture——而这通常是一个行业从"成长股"被重新归类为"末日股"的转折时刻。

历史镜像：2018年的IBM、2015年的Xerox、2010年的Nokia——它们都曾在某个季度因为"一个领先指标转负"被市场永久重新定价，再也没回到原来的估值中枢。Accenture这次的5.1%股息率，可能不是"便宜的安全边际"，而是市场在给它办葬礼前的最后一份慰问金。

这和芯片股估值28.6倍形成了一个极其讽刺的镜像：同一个AI叙事，把硬件抬到天上，把服务踩进地里。 而历史上，每一次这种"硬件溢价 vs 服务折价"的极端分化，最终都以毛利率向中间均值回归告终——这意味着英伟达的50%净利率和Accenture的6倍现金流，都不是稳态。

📝 转录文本

开场与本期框架

Hello, everyone. Welcome to a professional US stock channel that doesn't just talk about news. I'm Xiao Tao, a market analyst. Welcome to the update on Thursday. Friday is not open. So today's content, you can listen to it without worry. Because no matter what I say, the stock market will not rise or fall tomorrow. I wish you a happy weekend. Happy weekend to all the American viewers.

Today's content, because after yesterday's US-China meeting, I did a live broadcast. The live broadcast time is very long, so I will quickly summarize the first show of Kevin Walsh at the US-China meeting yesterday. Then we will talk about the market today. After the release of the financial report by Accenture, there was a very big drop. Why did it drop? Is there a difference between each shareholder and each shareholder between software blocks? Finally, I will share with you the situation of the chip block.

So Okay, I'll stop talking and start today's content right away. Before we start, I remind you to remember to leave a comment, like and forward.

一、周四市场回顾

Let's first review the trend of asset prices on Thursday. The US dollar and US debt revenue have a very large amount of US dollars. The US debt revenue has seen a small drop, but if we add the trend of the US debt market on Tuesday and Thursday together, the US debt revenue of two years has shown a very large drop.

So from the results, we can conclude that at least the market thinks that the US-China Fed meeting of Kevin Walsh is biased. We must not use the trend of US stocks and the market to understand Kevin Walsh or how to understand the meeting of the US Fed to make a connection. The trend of US stocks, US debt revenue and the US Fed, especially within a short period of time, is not very relevant.

Even if you still remember, in the beginning of 2022, the attitude of US stocks and US bonds, including the attitude of US stocks and the United States, came out of a gap for a while. In the end, US stocks will approach the US bond in this direction.

So the market was doing very well on Thursday. The stock market was at 1.1%, the Nasdaq was at 1.9%, and the Dow was at 0.1%. The main increase in the stock market and the Nasdaq is from the 1st quarter when the major tech stocks rose by 1.5%. The second most important point is that the chip version rose by 6.6%. The chip version also pulled the YTT directly to 112%. So the YTT has risen by 10% so far, and the Nasdaq rose by 14.1%. Most of the increase came from the chip version.

二、美联储会议复盘：Kevin Walsh首秀

Then we will summarize the US-China summit on Wednesday with the first show of Kevin Walsh.

点阵图与SEP

First, on the point-and-correct map, the remaining 18 members of the United States will be suspended once or more than the previous year. Only one person among the remaining eight people will be suspended this year. Only one person among the 19 members of the United States will be suspended this year. The rest of the people are considered to be suspended or suspended.

Kevin Walsh did not submit his prediction. Why? Because he thought that if he had no way to make a prediction, there was no need to submit it.

第一点：决心把通胀压回2%

Then let's take a look at the focus in his speech and Q&A. First of all, he talked about the fact that he was determined to bring the inflation back to 2%. This also expressed the determination of the US Federal Reserve and all other US Federal Reserve members to bring the inflation back to 2%. This sentence itself at least repeated three times.

Although he did not give his own opinion, the SEP did not submit, and then someone asked him what he would do next, he did not give any answer. The market also directly pulled up the two-year US debt rate and there was a rise. The market began to set prices, and the first increase was made in September. In fact, the market actually listened to this sentence. How do you press the balance back by 2% is through the increase. This is the first point.

第二点：世界变化快，政策要快速适应

The second point is that the world is changing very fast now. This means that the monetary policy of the United States and the United States should adapt and adapt quickly. It is actually very optimistic.

In 2021, the entire United States and the United States had said that the inflation was short, but it did not increase in time. It only began to increase in 2022. So the United States and the United States in this area is that if we see a very urgent need to increase, we will increase, but not for some political correctness put the matter of the increase drag on.

So the American Fed of Kevin Walsh this year and before not the same. Before, I and you compare the Fed is the sea inside the navigation of that ship — it is to turn from the decline to the increase is to need a very long time of this transition, and every step of the transition of time we can all be sure. But Kevin Walsh this year, the American Fed is not the same. His speed of this transfer may be very fast. The former US Fed may take at least three months to six months to transfer; Kevin Walsh is very likely to start transferring in just two months. For example, now it's June, July, August, September — the US Fed will be able to reopen again the possibility of increase.

第三点（最大变化）：让市场来解读数据

The third point is also the biggest change I think. Kevin Walsh said that he said let the market decide how to make this feedback to the data.

[过渡段：关于美联储与市场的相互引导关系的长段论述，原文以反复结构呈现。]

Because how the US dollar reads the data will affect the US dollar's monetary policy. And the US dollar's monetary policy is the reason why the asset price, especially the US stock, is rising or falling.

2021 is a very good example. I have mentioned it many times. In 2021, the inflation rate has reached 5%. The US dollar market has already believed that the US dollar will increase. But the bubble has been saying that inflation is short. They are still in the QE, so the US stock price rose all the way up until the beginning of October, when the bubble began to pivot. In December, January 2022, the US stock, especially the big tech side, officially began to fall.

So the turning point of the US-China relationship is the time when the asset price falls. It's not that the bond market starts to price increase, and the asset price must fall. So at a certain time, the US Fed can disagree with the market's view, and disagree with the view of the two-year US debt rate. They have their own idea, which is to pull the US debt rate or the bond market towards their view.

But now Kevin Walsh says, "Don't wait for our interpretation. You can directly look at how the market interprets this data." Because whether it's big financial data or CPI, PCE, the market will immediately make a response. Kevin Walsh's view is that you don't have to think about how we think and how we interpret it. You can just take the market interpretation as a more important judgment.

三、为何这是"致命的转变"

I personally think this is a more fatal reason. Why? It means that the market is right. We listen to the market. It changed. I said before that it changed the thinking model that I shared with you before.

If you listen to the market or take the market's voice as one of the very important reference indicators, the market price will be 90% increased in September. If the market is expected to increase now, we know that Kevin Walsh was a Trump's point of view. Trump wanted to lower the interest rate. If Kevin Walsh takes the market's voice as a very important reference, then is it a raise or maintain the same rate in September?

Another point is that it is irrational in the short term of the market. So the market will fluctuate very much in the short term. If the US-China relationship is often more distant, the US-China relationship must pull the market back. But if it gives up this kind of communication means now, gives up releasing this signal to the market, it wants to become a background version, and let the market be the leader, then it will often increase the fluctuation of the entire market. If the fluctuation of the US-China relationship increases, the fluctuation of the US-China relationship will definitely increase.

四、9月加息的可能性

In the video outside of the week, I talked about it. I said that they were going to start the holiday season as early as September, and I said that it was almost impossible at that time. Because according to the previous United States and the United States, this kind of rhythm, he didn't have time to make a turn.

But on Wednesday, when I finished listening to Kevin Walsh yesterday, I listened to Kevin Walsh this time. The United States and the United States — I said September is very likely because if it follows the market and the market has started to set prices, it is possible that September will really increase.

So now the market price increase in September is at 88.9%, which is nearly 90% likely to increase in September. It actually fits the signal that Kevin Walsh wants to release to the market. He said, I refer to the market and he said that the entire US dollar is now unified to press the inflation back to 2%. He said it three times. Doesn't this represent the current decision of the US dollar?

So this is why I think this time Kevin Walsh is very positive. The bond market also made a relative response. Then the market will expect to increase by 1.5 times throughout 2026. If we don't see the increase in inflation after the end of September, it will not be unexpected to increase by one time in December.

So the US dollar of Kevin Walsh will definitely be different from before. If it is still the bar one US dollar, I will be very confident to tell you that the price of the market is wrong. But now I can't tell you that it's wrong. On the contrary, Kevin Walsh told us to respect the market price. If it's now, you let me judge. Many people ask me if it will increase in September. If you ask me, I think the probability of increasing in September is very high.

五、历史回顾：市场如何"牵引"美联储

In fact, the US-China relationship, the market, and the bond market have always been a process of mutual influence. But from the historical data, we usually think that the market is leading the US-China relationship. Behind this, it doesn't mean that there is no data to support it.

For example, let's look at the picture. The white line is the revenue rate of the two-year US debt. The blue line is the share price of the US-China relationship. Basically, the white line is higher than the blue line when it rises and lower than the blue line when it falls. What does it mean? When in a period of increase, if the market thinks it will increase, the annual income will start to rise. Then we see that after a certain period of time, the blue line, the gap rate of the US dollar, starts to rise, which means that the market takes the US dollar to go.

2015年末

For example, in the end of 2015, we saw the white line rise first, and then the United States and the United States began to add it in a few months later. So this is the first time in the period of increase.

2018年末

Then at the end of 2018, I remember very clearly because I was in the market. I remember very clearly because in December — December 25th Christmas Day, the last few days of the exchange day, the share of stocks fell very much. It's because of the bubble still determined to do it in December. The market was very disliked at that time. You see the end of 2018 white line, the two-year US bond rate has begun to fall.

Then in the middle of 2019, this is very because it was Trump's government. Then Trump let the bubble go down. Then everyone thought that the bubble was not independent because Trump let the bubble go down. Then in 2019 the market fell down in the second quarter of 2017, and then after a while, the blue line, the US dollar started to decline.

So these two are very good examples of the market taking the US dollar to the group. The market tells the US dollar that you should decrease, you should increase, and then a few months later, the US dollar really respects the market's will to do so.

2021年：经典案例

Another time in 2021, this is a very classic example. In 2021, look at the white line. In September 2021, there was a very large amount of this injury. Because the inflation came out at 5% at the time of the year.

Then look at when the US dollar started to increase. The US dollar only started to increase in 2022. So in the middle of that time, you can understand that the market is taking the US dollar to go. But in the middle of 2021, especially in the second half of the year, there are many time nodes. The US dollar is pushback. The Fed said that I think the passage is short. So the market price is really set. The market price of 200 million dollars is set very, very early. But when it really hits the US dollar, it will be at least half a year later.

When did the US stock market fall? The US stock market did not fall when the US dollar started to rise in the first two years. It was when the US dollar really started to pivot, and started to say that I was going to start to increase. I'm going to start to increase again. After this period, the US stock fell. It was in November 2021 when the growth curve fell first. Then in January 2022, the entire stock market began to fall.

2023年SVB事件与误判

Let's look at the future. In February and March of 2023, we saw a drop in the white line. Why? Because of the SVB incident. After the SVB incident, the price of the two-year market price dropped rapidly. It was expected to fall into a downturn because of the over-expansion. What does the breakout mean? It means that the market has to go to the US dollar in advance and tell the US dollar that you are going to reduce interest rates. But the US dollar did not stop the market. It continued to increase interest rates.

Then after a while, the market found that the SVB incident could be solved and did not enter the breakout. The two-year US dollar-to-bill cabbage trade agreement immediately rebounded. Then at the end of 2023, there will be some sounds of a certain decline. The two-year US debt income will drop again, but the US dollar didn't go down at that time.

So these two times are very obvious — a misjudgment of the two-year US debt or the market debt market. Until when? Until March and April of 2024, the two-year US debt income began to fall again. Then in September 2024, the US dollar officially opened the downward trend.

小结：两年期美债作为先行指标

So the price of the two-year US debt income or the market response to the two-year US debt income is very much reflected in the future. The expectation of the currency policy or the outlook on whether it is going to increase or decrease. The trend of the two-year US debt income is not necessarily correct, but it is at least a pioneer.

For example, in March this year, when the World War I began, oil prices began to rise, and the income of the two-year-old American family began to rise. So does it mean that the next United States will be a global market? Not necessarily. But at least now the market has set a 90% probability.

You can see it in three months. And in the next two months, of course, there will be another US-China meeting in the middle. So in the next two to three months, if the US-China does not push back, if there is no data to support inflation, there will be a slowdown in the increase rate in September. It may increase greatly and may be set at 100% at the end. The US-China does not push back, so the market prediction is correct, or the market prediction is wrong.

六、Accenture（ACN）财报暴跌解析

Okay, let's talk about Accenture. Accenture is a company that I really like. I know two or three of the three institutions. There is one institution that I really like, Accenture. But it was sold a while ago. It's basically capitulated. I said that in the first quarter, many institutions have already capitulated in the software section. So the position of the warehouse is basically very low.

But it is not to mention that Accenture has dropped nearly 20% after the release of the report on Thursday. In the past year, the total decline rate has reached 58%. The market value is only 78 billion.

股息率与"软件公司金融化"

Then we look at the dividend rate in the back view mirror — it has come to 5.1%. This 5.1% is mainly because after the whole market value dropped, if it can still pay or cash in so many of these dividends, then this dividend ratio has reached 5.1%.

So now many software companies give me a feeling, because their current valuation is already very low, if you can still keep this dividend, what has this software company become? Become some financial, become some utility, even more than financial than the financial version. Many banks have a lower valuation.

暴跌真因：Booking

Why did the drop appear after the financial report was published? I think the market interpretation is because their index is not enough, but what I see is that it is mainly because of their booking. Because booking is the leading index for future revenue, the drop of 2% in the same rate of increase in booking has appeared, so it is not a positive interpretation for the market.

The story line that the market has been running is that they consult this area of business. The market believes that AI will cause a great deal of damage to the business of consulting. So now for software companies, because each software company has a different business, so they are basically different.

For companies like Accenture, the market writes a story line that consulting will be damaged. So they have to prove that they can't be damaged in consulting. And their increase is accelerating. Otherwise, the market will basically have a hard time for a basic return or a return on value. At the same time, if there is a negative growth in booking, it will basically collapse for the market.

估值视角：P/CF 与 P/E

After the fall, let's look at P/E. Actually, I don't look at P/E. I look at P/E. Why do I look at P/E here? Because their cash flow is still so high. Their valuation is down again. P/E, if you look at the back of the world, is 6 times. What is 6 times? If you can still maintain such a cash flow now, you can buy the entire company in 6 years. It's almost like that.

And if you look at PE, in the next three years, because this company, Accenture, is a low-income company, its revenue is very low. The low-income revenue is high. So its EPS is in the high-income range. So 7%, 8%, and 9%.

Similarly, if you look at the current current price to cash flow, 6.36 times in 2008, and 5.82 times in 2009. Indeed, now basically all software companies have a very low valuation, including Adobe, which I own. I think software companies can still run out of companies in the end, so they must look at different software companies differently.

So every software company must be very confident in its basic surface or at least have a certain sense of control, and then you can take part of it or the corresponding warehouse to try. Otherwise, if it's just because, for example, the valuation is very low and then you go to buy, I think you will be very uncomfortable because there is nothing to support you. It's just a valuation, then its valuation can remain low for a long time.

七、芯片板块估值与英特尔减仓思路

Finally, let's talk about the valuation of the chip. I am looking at SOX here. The valuation of the chip is 28.6 times higher than the closing price of JOS. It is close to 29 times the valuation in June. I drew a line here that is 30 times higher. So if it rises to 30 times, it is basically more than 30 times higher than it was in 2024. If it goes to 30 times this time, it is the new high of more than a year ago.

英特尔减仓的逻辑

I also shared with you in yesterday's live broadcast because I talked about Intel as my first-generation warehouse. After the decommissioning, it is still my first-generation warehouse. If it goes to more than 30 times, I should take the initiative to decommission the warehouse on the side of Intel.

The reason for the decommissioning is not that I don't like this company, but the scale of this company's overall investment group is too large. Basically, I still look at Intel as a company, and I don't think Intel's valuation is in a very high position. I can justify its current valuation, but the impact of the capital too big for my overall investment group will be too big. So from the point of view of risk control, to reduce the price and the entire chip section valuation, if it goes to a very high position, then we should make a certain adjustment.

为什么不清仓？

Many people may ask why not clear the warehouse? It depends on each person's investment logic or investment framework. In my own investment framework, the basic face is always the most important. If a company is getting better and better, it may be because of the emotional aspect in the short term, or because of the participation, it may be stubborn to go to a very high position. The high value of the stock price is not a reason to clear the warehouse. I can continue to hold oil and I can reduce the warehouse, but it is absolutely not the reason to clear the warehouse.

Unless in the same block, in the same mark, I can find a replacement mark, then I may clear the warehouse. Otherwise, the value of the stock price goes to the high position, I can reduce the warehouse, but I will not clear the warehouse.

仓位比例决定操作

And a lot of times, whether or not to cut the stock depends on the ratio of this title itself in my overall investment group. So now Intel still accounts for more than 20% of my investment. If today after the increase, Intel only accounts for 2%, then for me, I have no concern at all. On the contrary, it is stubborn and I will continue to hold it. If it falls down, there is any rebound of this rebound of 2% or 30%, I will still add the stock. But now, with my first-generation warehouse, which is above 20%, I am very stubborn and I should take the initiative to do the warehouse inspection.

So it's not a... A lot of times you will find that a site itself, its price, for example, after the price continues to rise to 150 or 160, it depends on the difference in each person's warehouse. I think the operation is actually different.

八、估值高 ≠ 必然下跌：SOX的两段历史

Another point is that from this picture, I want to tell you that I have always emphasized that it is not because the valuation is too high, then the price of this landmark must fall. It's not like that.

I give him two examples. In the same picture, we see that SOX is the entire chip version of the entire chip version of the ETF. The blue line is the valuation, right? Then the white line is the valuation, and the blue line is the price.

You can see that in 2024, the valuation hit 30 times and then the valuation dropped. At that time, the price did drop. Then in August, what happened in July and August? Carriage, a flat, right? Then the valuation did drop. The price also dropped. So in those two periods, the two valuation hits 30 times in 2024, there was a drop in the valuation and there was a rebound in the valuation. Then the price also appeared to rebound.
